name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads dynamic opportunity content via getHomePageContent and routes users to plans, programs, applications, webinars, the earnings calculator, and success stories.

core_features: Load homepage content using getHomePageContent on initial page render and distribute the data to all sections.
Display primary introductory copy using heroHeadline and heroSubheadline and provide main calls-to-action to apply, view plans, and access the free webinar.
Show a dynamic list of coreBenefits describing the work-from-home opportunity.
Render a featuredPrograms section showing selected Program items with navigation to program_detail.html and programs.html.
Render a featuredWebinar highlight using Webinar data with navigation to webinar_detail.html and webinars.html.

Render a teaser earnings calculator using earningsCalculatorTeaser values, allow user adjustments in-page, and provide a CTA to open earnings_calculator.html with the current calculator state passed via URL parameters.
Render a featuredSuccessStories section showing selected SuccessStory items with navigation to story_detail.html and success_stories.html.
Support URL-parameter-based state such as pre-selecting a section to scroll to, highlighting a specific featured item, or pre-filling the earnings calculator teaser.
user_workflows:
Explore opportunity and start application:
1) User lands on index.html with no required URL parameters.
2) home-page-loader calls getHomePageContent and populates heroHeadline, heroSubheadline, coreBenefits, featuredPrograms, featuredWebinar, earningsCalculatorTeaser, and featuredSuccessStories.
3) User reviews the intro summary and core benefits.
4) User clicks the primary hero CTA button labeled according to the requirement (e.g., "Apply Now" or "Get Started").
5) Browser navigates to application.html (no additional parameters required).

Compare programs before applying:
1) User lands on index.html (optionally with ?section=programs to focus on programs).
2) home-page-loader fetches data via getHomePageContent.
3) If section=programs is present, home-page-controller scrolls the viewport to the featured-programs-grid component once data is loaded.
4) User reviews the featuredPrograms cards (each card bound to a Program entity).
5) User clicks on a specific featured program card.
6) Browser navigates to program_detail.html?id={selectedProgram.id}.
7) From program_detail.html, user may proceed to application.html or plans.html as defined on those pages.

View and register for a webinar from the homepage:
1) User lands on index.html (optionally with ?highlightWebinarId={id}).
2) getHomePageContent returns featuredWebinar including its Webinar data and isLiveSoon flag.
3) If highlightWebinarId matches featuredWebinar.webinar.id, the featured-webinar-highlight component applies a highlighted state when rendering.
4) User clicks the "Learn more" interaction on the featured webinar card.
5) Browser navigates to webinar_detail.html?id={featuredWebinar.webinar.id}.
6) Alternatively, user can click a "Free Webinar" header navigation link to go to webinars.html.

Use teaser earnings calculator and continue to full calculator:
1) User lands on index.html with optional calculator-related URL parameters (e.g., hours_per_week, average_sale_value, monthly_leads, conversion_rate_percent).
2) getHomePageContent returns earningsCalculatorTeaser default values.
3) earnings-calculator-teaser initializes its local state using URL parameters if present; otherwise it uses the defaults from earningsCalculatorTeaser.
4) User adjusts one or more input fields in the teaser calculator; the component updates the estimated income display in-page according to its calculation logic without persisting any data.
5) User clicks the "Open full calculator" button.
6) Browser navigates to earnings_calculator.html and appends the current calculator state as query parameters so the full calculator can initialize with the same configuration.

Browse success stories from the homepage:
1) User lands on index.html (optionally with ?section=success-stories).
2) getHomePageContent returns featuredSuccessStories.
3) If section=success-stories is present, home-page-controller scrolls to the featured-success-stories-strip component after data is loaded.
4) User reviews the list or slider of featured success stories.
5) User clicks a specific story card.
6) Browser navigates to story_detail.html?id={selectedStory.id}.
7) Alternatively, user clicks the "View more stories" link in that section and navigates to success_stories.html.
interactions: Header navigation link click to Home: Navigates to index.html (no parameters) from any other page.

Header navigation link click to "Programs": Navigates to programs.html; if the user is already on index.html, clicking a local control such as a "View all programs" link scrolls to the programs section or navigates to programs.html depending on implementation.
Header navigation link click to "Plans & Pricing": Navigates to plans.html.
Header navigation link click to "Free Webinar": Navigates to webinars.html.
Primary hero CTA click (e.g., "Apply Now" or "Get Started"): Navigates to application.html.
Secondary hero CTA click to "Plans & Pricing": Navigates to plans.html.
Secondary hero CTA click to "Free Webinar": Navigates to webinars.html.
Click on a featured program card: Navigates to program_detail.html?id={program.id} based on the Program entity bound to the clicked card.
Click on the "View all programs" interaction within the featured programs section: Navigates to programs.html.
Click on the featured webinar title or explicit "Learn more" link: Navigates to webinar_detail.html?id={featuredWebinar.webinar.id}.
Click on a webinar-specific CTA (e.g., "Register now" shortcut): Navigates to webinars.html (optionally with a query parameter for the featured webinar ID if desired by that page).

Adjust any input in the earnings-calculator-teaser (e.g., hours per week, average sale value, monthly leads, conversion rate percent): Triggers an onChange event that updates the internal calculator state and recalculates the displayed estimated income without persisting the result.

Click the "Open full calculator" button in the teaser: Navigates to earnings_calculator.html with calculator state encoded in the query string (e.g., hours_per_week, average_sale_value, monthly_leads, conversion_rate_percent).
Click on a featured success story card: Navigates to story_detail.html?id={story.id}.
Click on the "View more stories" link in the success stories section: Navigates to success_stories.html.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page does not require any URL parameters to function, but it can respond to optional query parameters to control initial state and navigation behavior, without passing them into getHomePageContent. The page always calls getHomePageContent with no parameters and derives all content from its response.

Supported URL parameters (all optional and read-only on this page):
- section: A string indicating the section to bring into view after data load. Valid values are interpreted by the home-page-controller (for example, 'programs', 'calculator', 'success-stories', 'webinar'). On page initialization, after getHomePageContent resolves, the controller scrolls to and focuses the corresponding section component if it exists.
- highlightProgramId: If present, and if the ID matches one of the Program entities inside featuredPrograms, the featured-programs-grid applies a visual highlighted state to that card when rendering (e.g., emphasized border). This is purely client-side state and does not change the data returned from getHomePageContent.
- highlightStoryId: If present and matching one of the SuccessStory entities in featuredSuccessStories, the featured-success-stories-strip applies a highlighted state to that story card.
- highlightWebinarId: If present and equal to featuredWebinar.webinar.id, the featured-webinar-highlight component applies a highlighted state to the webinar card.
- hours_per_week, average_sale_value, monthly_leads, conversion_rate_percent: Optional numeric values used by the earnings-calculator-teaser component to override the initial default values from earningsCalculatorTeaser. On initialization, the component reads these parameters from location.search and, if valid, uses them as its starting state while still displaying the exampleEstimatedMonthlyIncome field as the baseline comparison. Subsequent user changes are managed locally in the component state.

The page’s state is thus derived from two sources: (1) the data returned by getHomePageContent, which is the single source of content truth, and (2) the current URL query parameters, which only affect client-side layout (scrolling, highlighting) and initial calculator values. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent browser storage from this page.

components.functionality: Initializes the Home page by calling getHomePageContent, handling loading and error states, and passing the resulting data object to downstream components via props or a shared state container.

Coordinates URL-parameter-based behavior (section navigation, item highlighting, calculator initial state overrides) after data is loaded. It reads location.search, computes derived UI state, and passes flags or IDs to section components.

Provides top-level navigation links to main site destinations, including Home, Programs, Plans & Pricing, Free Webinar, Success Stories, and optionally a direct link to the earnings calculator. Uses standard anchor navigation without additional data fetching.

Displays the main introductory text for the work-from-home opportunity using heroHeadline and heroSubheadline. Presents the primary calls-to-action that guide the user to apply, view plans & pricing, and access webinar information.

Renders the list of key benefits of the opportunity using the coreBenefits array. Each benefit item displays the provided icon (or identifier), title, and description. This component is informational and does not initiate navigation by default.

Displays a grid or list of featuredPrograms, each represented by a Program entity. Each card typically shows program name, level, focus_area, time_commitment, and average_monthly_earnings. Supports highlighting of a specific program based on the highlightProgramId URL parameter and provides navigation to both individual program details and the full programs listing.

Uses featuredWebinar data to show the key information about a selected upcoming Webinar, including title, subtitle, scheduled time, topic_type, and presenter if available. Uses isLiveSoon to conditionally display a proximity indicator. Provides interactions to learn more about the specific webinar or go to the general webinars page.

Shows a simplified earnings calculator using earningsCalculatorTeaser default values and any overrides from URL parameters. Allows users to adjust numeric inputs (hours per week, average sale value, monthly leads, conversion_rate_percent) and displays an estimated income result calculated on the client side. Contains a button that navigates to the full earnings calculator page, passing the current calculator values via URL parameters.

Displays a selection of featuredSuccessStories, each based on a SuccessStory entity. Each item may show the story’s headline, person_name or alias, lifestyle category, monthly_earnings, hours_per_week range, and summary. Supports optional highlighting of a story via highlightStoryId from URL parameters and offers navigation to the individual story detail or the full list of success stories.

Provides repeated key calls-to-action at the bottom of the page (e.g., apply, view plans, visit programs, see webinars, open calculator, browse success stories) for users who reach the end of the content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single dominant hero section that fills the initial viewport with a full-bleed lifestyle photo (beach) as the background. Center a tall, dark, translucent panel that acts as the main conversion funnel: headline and summary at the top, benefits in the middle, primary calculator CTA toward the lower third, and a brief credibility/payoff area at the bottom. All other homepage components stack vertically beneath the hero, still aligned to the same centered, single-column content track, each in its own translucent panel or band over the continuing background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single dominant hero section that fills the initial viewport with a full-bleed lifestyle photo (beach) as the background. Center a tall, dark, translucent panel that acts as the main conversion funnel: headline and summary at the top, benefits in the middle, primary calculator CTA toward the lower third, and a brief credibility/payoff area at the bottom. All other homepage components stack vertically beneath the hero, still aligned to the same centered, single-column content track, each in its own translucent panel or band over the continuing background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section occupies roughly the full initial viewport height, with about 60–65% of the panel’s height devoted to messaging (intro summary + benefits + payoff copy) and the remaining 35–40% to the primary conversion UI (earnings calculator teaser CTA and related microcopy). Below the hero, each subsequent section (programs grid, webinar, success stories, footer links) occupies distinct horizontal slices with generous vertical separation (24–32px between major sections), all constrained to a central column that is about 40–50% of the viewport width (max-width around 560–640px) so the background photo is always visible on the sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately dense promotional feel inside panels: stacked text blocks with 8–16px between typographic elements and 24–32px between logical groups (headline → body copy → CTA → payoff). Sections below the hero follow the same density: compact text plus cards or strips with limited but adequate breathing room, creating urgency without feeling cramped. Outside the panels, the large, unobstructed background image and consistent side margins keep the page from feeling overloaded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve a strong top-to-bottom funnel. The eye first hits the high-contrast header navigation bar at the very top, then drops immediately to the centered hero panel’s bold headline and supporting copy, then to the benefit list, then to the bright calculator CTA within the earnings teaser. After conversion-focused content, the flow continues downward to social proof (success stories) and deeper exploration (programs, webinar, footer CTAs). Color accents (yellow, red, cyan/teal) and bright buttons are placed on the primary CTA elements, while all secondary content uses slightly calmer treatments so the main hero CTA remains dominant.

layout.overall_description: The homepage opens on a full-bleed beach photograph that fills the browser window from edge to edge and top to bottom. A slightly translucent dark header bar spans the very top, holding navigation and a compact CTA. Centered in the viewport is a tall, dark, glassy panel—about half the page width—with bold, high-contrast typography. At its top, a large headline and supporting intro text immediately frame the work-from-home opportunity. Just below, a concise list of core benefits stacks tightly, reinforcing the value proposition. Toward the lower third of this panel, a vivid earnings-calculator teaser with a bright full-width button acts as the central conversion trigger, backed by a short payoff line that reinforces income potential. As you scroll, the background image remains visible and the content continues in a single vertical track: first a programs grid panel, then a highlighted webinar card, then a horizontal success-stories strip, all sitting on slightly translucent dark surfaces that let the beach scene glow through the sides. The page ends with a dark footer band of CTA links. Throughout, the layout is vertical, centered, and conversion-driven, echoing the original modal-like hero pattern while extending it into a structured, scrollable landing experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-page-loader component is non-visual and occupies no deliberate screen space. It is instantiated at the very top of the document flow, just before any visible layout elements. Conceptually, it sits behind the hero background layer, stretching the full width but with zero height, ensuring all dynamic content is ready before the central hero panel and subsequent sections render. It does not affect spacing, alignment, or visual hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, zero-height element injected at the top of the DOM; it participates in no visual layout and does not impact the grid or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-page-controller is also non-visual and acts as the logical wrapper for the page. It sits at the root of the main content area, conceptually behind the full-bleed background and encompassing all visible components. It spans 100% of the viewport width and height but has no visible representation, so it does not consume layout space or introduce additional margins beyond its children.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible structural container that controls state and routing; behaves as a full-page wrapper without any rendered box, letting child components define the actual layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation is a full-width horizontal bar anchored to the very top of the viewport, overlaying the beach background. It is approximately 64–80px tall, with 16–24px of internal vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding on both sides. The bar is a semi-opaque dark gradient so the background subtly shows through. Content inside is aligned to the same central column as the hero panel: a logo or wordmark is positioned toward the left edge of this central region, while primary navigation links are aligned to the right, ending with a brightly colored CTA button (e.g., yellow) that visually echoes the hero’s main button. On larger screens, the header stretches edge-to-edge, but the actual nav items are constrained to a max-width around 1040–1200px and centered, ensuring visual alignment with the central hero panel below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the top of the viewport on scroll, remaining fixed in place while the rest of the content scrolls beneath; maintains a translucent overlay effect on the background image.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The intro-summary forms the top portion of the central hero panel. The hero panel itself is centered horizontally in the viewport, occupying roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (max-width around 560–640px) and vertically offset so its top edge sits 80–96px below the header-navigation, with 24–32px of margin between the panel’s top edge and the first text line. Inside this panel, the intro-summary content is stacked at the top: a bold H1 headline sits flush-left within the panel, spanning most of its width, followed by 8–16px of vertical spacing, then 2–3 lines of supporting copy in a slightly smaller, high-contrast type. The text is aligned to the left but visually centered within the panel’s width. This entire block occupies roughly the upper 25–30% of the hero panel’s height, establishing the main message before any benefits or CTAs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered within the hero panel; scales responsively with the panel’s width while keeping consistent top padding and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The core-benefits-list is positioned directly beneath the intro-summary inside the same central hero panel, maintaining the continuous single-column flow. There is a medium vertical gap (16–24px) between the end of the supporting copy and the top of the benefits list. The list items (typically 3–5) are stacked vertically with tight spacing (8–12px between items) to maintain a moderately dense promotional feel. Each benefit line is left-aligned with the intro text and may use subtle icons or bolded lead-in text, but the overall block remains compact, occupying roughly an additional 20–25% of the hero panel’s height. The right and left edges of the list respect the same 24–32px internal side padding of the panel, ensuring a consistent text column width. This block visually bridges the messaging at the top and the conversion-oriented calculator teaser below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing directly under the intro-summary within the hero panel; collapses as a single block, maintaining vertical stacking and left alignment across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The earnings-calculator-teaser occupies the lower third of the central hero panel and acts as the main conversion focal point. It is placed directly below the core-benefits-list with a larger vertical gap (24–32px) to clearly signal a shift from information to action. At the top of this block, a bold, simplifying statement (e.g., “See exactly what you could earn in minutes”) spans the width of the text column, set in large white type. Below it, with 8–12px of spacing, is a shorter instruction line in a contrasting accent color (yellow or teal) that points to the CTA (e.g., “Use the free earnings calculator now”). Immediately beneath the instruction, a full-width, high-contrast button is centered horizontally within the panel, but its text is left-aligned, maintaining the overall left-anchored feel. The button stretches nearly the entire internal width of the panel, leaving 16–24px padding to the left and right edges, and has 12–16px of vertical padding inside. Beneath the button, after another 12–16px gap, a two-line payoff statement appears: the top line in a teal accent, the bottom in larger white type, both centered vertically within this lower area and left-aligned to the content column. This entire teaser block uses strong gradients and drop shadows on the button to visually pop against the darker panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored inside the hero panel; its button remains the visual anchor of the lower hero area. On smaller screens, the block stretches to nearly full width with the button always full-width and pinned to the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-programs-grid sits immediately below the hero panel, separated from it by a generous vertical gap of about 32–48px to mark the transition from the primary hero to supporting content. This grid is contained within a new translucent dark panel that is horizontally centered using the same central column width as the hero (approximately 50–60% of the viewport width, max-width around 800–960px to allow for multiple cards). Inside this panel, a section title and short description appear at the top, aligned left with 24–32px of padding from the top edge. Below, the programs are displayed in a simple grid that still respects the single-column feel: on larger screens, program cards can appear in two columns with equal widths, but the entire grid is visually perceived as one block centered on the page. Vertical spacing between card rows is 16–24px, with 16px gaps between columns. The bottom of the panel is padded by 24–32px. This section occupies roughly one full screen’s worth of vertical space on desktop, but remains clearly secondary in intensity compared to the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked below the hero; the grid is responsive, collapsing to a single column on smaller devices while retaining the same central alignment and internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-webinar-highlight appears beneath the featured-programs-grid as a distinct but smaller promotional card. It is separated from the programs grid by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace, still floating over the same full-bleed background. The card is centered horizontally and constrained to a width similar to the hero panel (around 40–50% of viewport width, max-width 560–640px), reinforcing the single-column pattern. Inside, the layout is primarily vertical: a bold title at the top, a short line of copy emphasizing the live or on-demand nature underneath (8–12px spacing), then a bright CTA button spanning most of the card’s width, aligned left but visually centered within the panel. An optional small thumbnail or date badge can sit above or to the left of the title, but the overall impression remains a stacked, linear card. The interior padding matches the base panel padding (24–32px), and the card’s height is compact, creating a dense promotional feel that nudges users to engage further.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card centered within the scroll; does not stick or float, but its bold CTA and compact size make it an eye-catching pause in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-success-stories-strip is positioned below the webinar highlight, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. Unlike the discrete panels above, this component presents as a low-profile horizontal strip that spans nearly the full central content width (around 70–80% of the viewport, max-width 1040px) and sits on a slightly darker translucent band that runs edge-to-edge behind it, subtly anchoring this as a credibility section. Within the strip, success stories are displayed either as a horizontal carousel of cards or as a row of 2–3 testimonial snippets, each compact and vertically centered within the strip. The text and avatars are kept to a moderate height so the strip feels long and continuous, rather than tall like the hero. Teal accents are used for names or highlight lines to echo the hero’s payoff line. This entire block occupies roughly 20–25% of a screen’s height and visually reinforces outcomes before the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal strip; its content can horizontally scroll or auto-rotate within the strip, but the strip itself remains fixed in the vertical flow, bridging the main content sections and the footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-cta-links component forms the final band at the bottom of the page. It is a full-width, dark footer bar that overlays the background image and visually closes the layout. The bar’s height is determined by its content, typically 120–200px, with 24–32px of vertical padding. Inside, the content is again constrained to the central column (max-width around 1040–1200px) and centered horizontally. Links are grouped into 2–3 vertical stacks or a single compact grid, but still perceived as one linear band of CTAs: key routes like plans, programs, applications, webinars, the earnings calculator, and success stories are listed with a slightly promotional hierarchy (primary links in brighter accent colors, secondary in muted tones). The vertical spacing between link rows is 8–12px, giving a tidy but accessible list. This band aligns with the overall visual language—dark, high contrast, crisp type—and offers a final set of navigation choices once the user has scrolled past all promotional content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer anchored at the bottom of the document; it is not sticky, appearing only when the user scrolls to the end of the page.

name: Plans & Pricing
title: Plans & Pricing
description: Page for exploring and comparing available work-from-home starter plans using filters and sorting, with navigation to detailed plan information and the application form.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options for starter plans using the getPlanFilterOptions interface.
Display a dynamically filtered and sorted list of active starter plans using the searchStarterPlans interface.
Allow users to filter plans by maximum price, whether 1-on-1 coaching is included, and minimum training modules count.
Allow users to sort plans using sort options provided by getPlanFilterOptions (for example, by price or recency).
Provide clickable plan items that navigate to the plan detail page via plan_detail.html with the plan id as a URL parameter.
Provide a contextual call-to-action link to application.html for users who prefer to apply without choosing a specific plan.
Keep filter and sort selections in sync with URL parameters so that the page state is shareable and restorable via the browser URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View default starter plans list
1) User navigates to plans.html (via header, footer, or direct link) without any query parameters.
2) The page controller component calls getPlanFilterOptions with no parameters to obtain available max price presets, training module count options, and sort options.
3) The page controller parses the URL (which has no relevant parameters) and derives default filter and sort values (for example, using the first option returned for each control where needed).
4) The page controller calls searchStarterPlans with onlyStarterPlans set to true and remaining parameters derived from the (possibly empty) URL state.
5) The results list component renders the array of Plan entities returned by searchStarterPlans.
6) The filter bar component displays controls using the options from getPlanFilterOptions, pre-populated to match the current URL-derived state.

Workflow: Refine plans using filters and sorting
1) User adjusts one or more controls in the filter bar (e.g., selects a max price preset, toggles 1-on-1 coaching, selects a minimum training modules count, or selects a sort option).
2) The filter bar emits a filter/sort change event to the page controller with the new filter/sort values.
3) The page controller updates the browser URL query string to reflect the new state (without a full page reload) and normalizes values (e.g., converts booleans to string representations).
4) The page controller calls searchStarterPlans again using the updated parameters mapped from the URL: maxPrice, includesOneOnOneCoaching, minTrainingModulesCount, onlyStarterPlans, and sortBy.
5) The results list component receives the updated plans array and re-renders the plan list accordingly.
6) If no plans are returned, the results list component shows a dynamic empty state message (not hardcoded to any specific plan information) and still allows further filter changes.

Workflow: View detailed information for a chosen plan
1) User identifies a suitable plan in the results list and clicks anywhere on the plan card (or a dedicated details control within it).
2) The plan card component reads the associated Plan.id and triggers navigation to plan_detail.html with a query parameter id set to that Plan.id (e.g., plan_detail.html?id=<Plan.id>).
3) The plan_detail.html page (outside the scope of this specification) uses the id URL parameter to load and display detailed plan information and sign-up options.

Workflow: Apply without selecting a specific plan
1) User is unsure which plan to choose and clicks the contextual call-to-action component that links to the application flow.
2) The CTA component triggers navigation to application.html, optionally passing through non-plan-identifying context parameters from the current URL if desired (e.g., a generic source indicator such as source=plans, without using static plan data).
3) The application.html page (outside the scope of this specification) uses its own form and logic to handle the application process.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load
- Trigger: Browser loads plans.html.
- Behavior: The page controller parses the current URL query string, calls getPlanFilterOptions, determines initial filter/sort values (using URL values where valid and falling back to defaults inferred from getPlanFilterOptions), then calls searchStarterPlans with onlyStarterPlans set to true and other parameters mapped from the derived state. The results list and filter bar components receive the resulting data and render.

Interaction: Change maximum price filter
- Trigger: User selects a different maximum price preset in the filter bar or adjusts a numeric max price input (if implemented).
- Behavior: Filter bar emits an onFilterChange event with the new maxPrice. The page controller updates the maxPrice query parameter in the URL, calls searchStarterPlans with the updated maxPrice (keeping other filters and onlyStarterPlans fixed), and passes the new results to the results list component.

Interaction: Toggle 1-on-1 coaching filter
- Trigger: User toggles a control representing includesOneOnOneCoaching in the filter bar.
- Behavior: Filter bar emits an onFilterChange event with the updated includesOneOnOneCoaching value (true, false, or an unfiltered state if supported). The page controller updates the corresponding URL parameter (e.g., coaching=true/false or removal of the parameter for an unfiltered state), calls searchStarterPlans with the new includesOneOnOneCoaching value, and updates the results list.

Interaction: Change minimum training modules count filter
- Trigger: User chooses a minimum training modules count option in the filter bar.
- Behavior: Filter bar emits an onFilterChange event with minTrainingModulesCount. The page controller updates the minModules (or equivalent) URL parameter, calls searchStarterPlans with this value, and refreshes the results list.

Interaction: Change sort order
- Trigger: User selects a sort option in the filter bar (options sourced from getPlanFilterOptions.sortOptions).
- Behavior: Filter bar emits an onSortChange event with the selected sortBy value. The page controller updates the sortBy URL parameter, calls searchStarterPlans with the new sortBy, and updates the results list to reflect the new ordering.

Interaction: Reset filters
- Trigger: User activates a reset/clear filters control in the filter bar.
- Behavior: Filter bar emits an onReset event. The page controller clears relevant filter-related URL parameters (e.g., maxPrice, coaching, minModules, sortBy as appropriate), recalculates default values (using getPlanFilterOptions results if necessary), calls searchStarterPlans with onlyStarterPlans true and no additional filters, and re-renders the results list and filter bar.

Interaction: Click on a plan card
- Trigger: User clicks a plan item in the results list.
- Behavior: The plan card component retrieves the associated Plan.id and navigates the browser to plan_detail.html?id=<Plan.id>. No local state or localStorage is required; navigation is purely URL-based.

Interaction: Click contextual application CTA
- Trigger: User clicks the CTA link/button indicating intent to apply without choosing a specific plan.
- Behavior: CTA component navigates the browser to application.html. Optionally, it may append non-identifying query parameters (e.g., source=plans or encoded current filter context) to the URL so the application page can infer that the user came from the Plans & Pricing page.

Interaction: Browser back/forward navigation
- Trigger: User uses the browser back or forward buttons after changing filters or sort options.
- Behavior: The page controller listens to URL change events (e.g., popstate). On each event, it re-parses the URL parameters, updates its in-memory filter/sort state, calls searchStarterPlans with the parameters obtained from the URL, and sends the updated results to the results list and filter bar components so the UI matches the URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Plans & Pricing page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter and sort state, avoiding localStorage. On initial load and on browser history changes, the page controller parses the current URL and derives the parameters required for searchStarterPlans. The mapping between URL parameters and searchStarterPlans parameters is as follows:

- URL parameter maxPrice -> searchStarterPlans.maxPrice (numeric). If maxPrice is missing or cannot be parsed as a valid number, the controller treats it as undefined and omits the maxPrice constraint from the search call.
- URL parameter coaching -> searchStarterPlans.includesOneOnOneCoaching (boolean). The controller converts known string values (for example, represent true/false states consistently) to booleans, and if the parameter is absent, it may treat the field as undefined (no filter) depending on product rules.
- URL parameter minModules -> searchStarterPlans.minTrainingModulesCount (numeric). If missing or invalid, the controller treats it as undefined and omits the minimum modules constraint.
- URL parameter sortBy -> searchStarterPlans.sortBy (string enum). The controller validates the sortBy string against the values returned by getPlanFilterOptions.sortOptions. If the URL value is invalid or missing, the controller selects a default sortBy value from the available sortOptions (for example, the first option returned) and may update the URL to reflect this default.
- No URL parameter is used for onlyStarterPlans; the controller always passes onlyStarterPlans = true to searchStarterPlans so that only starter plans are returned on this page.
- Additional URL parameters not mapped to searchStarterPlans (such as a view mode parameter) are used only for client-side UI state and do not affect API calls.

When the user changes any filter or sort control, the filter bar notifies the page controller with the new values. The controller updates its internal state and synchronizes the URL query parameters to match (using the same mapping rules as above), then calls searchStarterPlans with the new parameter set. This ensures that copying the URL, refreshing the page, or using the browser's back/forward controls will always restore the same filter/sort state and results. The getPlanFilterOptions interface is typically called once per page load (or when the page controller detects it has no filter configuration) to obtain the current valid presets and sort options; these results are not derived from URL parameters but are used to validate and normalize them.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL state management, and communication between the filter bar and the results list for the Plans & Pricing page. On load, it calls getPlanFilterOptions to obtain filter and sort configuration, parses the URL to determine current filter and sort values, and then calls searchStarterPlans with mapped parameters. On any filter or sort change event from child components, it updates the URL and refreshes the plan list. It also listens for browser history events to re-synchronize UI state with the URL.
event_handlers: onLoad: Called when plans.html is loaded. Invokes getPlanFilterOptions, parses URL parameters, derives normalized filter/sort state, and calls searchStarterPlans with { maxPrice, includesOneOnOneCoaching, minTrainingModulesCount, onlyStarterPlans: true, sortBy }.

onUrlChange: Triggered on browser history events (e.g., popstate). Re-parses the URL parameters, validates them against the current filter options from getPlanFilterOptions, updates internal state, and re-invokes searchStarterPlans with the URL-derived parameters.

onFiltersChange: Subscribed to events from the filter bar. Receives a state object (e.g., { maxPrice, includesOneOnOneCoaching, minTrainingModulesCount }) and updates the URL query parameters accordingly, then calls searchStarterPlans with the updated values plus onlyStarterPlans: true and the current sortBy.

onSortChange: Subscribed to sort change events from the filter bar. Receives a new sortBy value (validated against getPlanFilterOptions.sortOptions), updates the sortBy URL parameter, and calls searchStarterPlans with the current filter values and new sortBy.

onResetFilters: Subscribed to reset events from the filter bar. Clears relevant filter and sort URL parameters, recalculates default state (using getPlanFilterOptions results if necessary), and calls searchStarterPlans with onlyStarterPlans: true and default parameters.
onResultsLoaded: Invoked after each searchStarterPlans call resolves. Passes the array of Plan entities to the results list component for rendering.
onViewChange: (Optional UI-only) Handles changes to a view mode parameter (e.g., card vs. list view) by updating a view-related URL parameter without affecting searchStarterPlans.

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls for users to filter and sort starter plans. It does not directly call APIs; instead, it displays options supplied by the page controller (which come from getPlanFilterOptions) and emits events when the user changes any control. The controls include a maximum price selector or input, a toggle or selector for including 1-on-1 coaching, a minimum training modules count selector, and a sort selector using options from getPlanFilterOptions.
data_binding: URL-derived filter state (via props from plans-page-controller)
event_handlers: onFilterChange: Fired whenever the user changes any filter control (maxPrice, includesOneOnOneCoaching, minTrainingModulesCount). Sends the updated filter object to the page controller for URL and API update.

onSortChange: Fired when the user selects a different sort option. Sends the selected sortBy value (matching one of the sortOptions.value fields returned by getPlanFilterOptions) to the page controller.
onSubmit: If the filter bar is rendered as a form, prevents default submission and instead triggers onFilterChange/onSortChange, allowing the page controller to perform the searchStarterPlans call.
onReset: Fired when the user activates a clear/reset control. Notifies the page controller to clear filter-related URL parameters and reload plans with default settings.

components.functionality: Receives an array of Plan entities from the page controller (obtained via searchStarterPlans) and renders each plan using the plan-card component. It manages list behaviors such as displaying an empty state when no plans match the current filters and optionally respecting a view mode (e.g., card vs. list) derived from the URL. It does not call APIs itself.
event_handlers: onPlanClick: Emitted when a user interacts with a plan item in a way that indicates interest in details (e.g., clicking the card or a dedicated detail control). It passes the Plan.id to either handle navigation directly or inform the page controller.

onEmptyStateAction: (Optional) Fired when the user interacts with an action shown in the empty state (for example, a button to reset filters), which can be wired to the filter bar's reset behavior via the page controller.

components.functionality: Represents a single Plan entity within the results list. Displays key fields such as name, short_label (if present), price, currency, includes_one_on_one_coaching, training_modules_count, and any highlighted_features provided. It supports user interaction to navigate to the plan detail page. The component is purely functional in terms of data binding and events and does not hardcode any plan-specific content.
event_handlers: onClick: Triggered when the user clicks the card surface. Reads the bound Plan.id and initiates navigation to plan_detail.html?id=<Plan.id>.
onDetailsClick: (Optional) Triggered when a user clicks a more explicit detail control within the card. Performs the same navigation behavior as onClick.

components.functionality: Provides a contextual call-to-action for users who prefer to start the application process without choosing a specific plan. It links to application.html and may append non-identifying context parameters (e.g., a generic source indicator or current filter context) to the URL. It does not interact with getPlanFilterOptions or searchStarterPlans directly.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigates the browser to application.html, optionally including query parameters such as source=plans or encoded filter context. No plan-specific data is required for this navigation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: All components live inside a single tall, dark, translucent central panel that sits on top of a full-bleed, high-contrast beach background. The layout is strictly linear, top-to-bottom, in one centered column. At the top of the panel, the page controller acts as the messaging header (title, short explanation, and basic view/sort controls). Directly beneath it, the filters bar behaves like the 'form' band from the mockup, giving users controls to refine plans. Below that, the plans results list occupies the middle stretch of the panel as a stacked column of plan cards. The global apply CTA sits at the bottom of the panel as a final, visually strong call-to-action, echoing the hero-style CTA from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components live inside a single tall, dark, translucent central panel that sits on top of a full-bleed, high-contrast beach background. The layout is strictly linear, top-to-bottom, in one centered column. At the top of the panel, the page controller acts as the messaging header (title, short explanation, and basic view/sort controls). Directly beneath it, the filters bar behaves like the 'form' band from the mockup, giving users controls to refine plans. Below that, the plans results list occupies the middle stretch of the panel as a stacked column of plan cards. The global apply CTA sits at the bottom of the panel as a final, visually strong call-to-action, echoing the hero-style CTA from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width and about 80–90% of the viewport height, centered both vertically and horizontally on larger screens, with background image visible around it. Inside the panel, the top 30–35% of vertical space is devoted to the page controller messaging and high-level controls, the middle 50–55% to the results list and plan cards, and the bottom 10–20% to the global apply CTA band (and any brief reinforcing copy). Internal padding around the panel is roughly 24–32px on all sides, and each major section (controller → filters → list → bottom CTA) is separated by 24–32px of vertical spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content inside the panel follows a moderately dense promotional style: type and controls are stacked with compact 8–16px spacing between related lines, and 24–32px between major structural blocks. Buttons and inputs use 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. The list of plans is visually tight but not cramped, with each card separated by a 16–24px gap. Outside the panel, the full-bleed image remains mostly unobstructed, providing visual breathing room and contrast against the dense central content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is drawn first to the bold, high-contrast page title and key pricing message at the top of the panel (within the page controller). From there, the user’s attention drops to a secondary line explaining how to use filters or compare plans, then naturally down to the filters bar, which is visually framed like a form row. Below that, the flow continues into the vertical stack of plan cards, where each card has its own internal hierarchy (plan name → price → main benefits → per-card action). Finally, the bottom global apply CTA spans the width of the panel in a bright, saturated color, acting as the final focal point and encouraging users to proceed after browsing the plans.

layout.overall_description: The page loads with a full-bleed, saturated beach photograph covering the entire viewport. Floating in the center is a tall, dark, semi-transparent panel with soft edges and subtle drop shadow, occupying about the middle half of the screen width so that the beach image is clearly visible to the left and right. Inside this panel, content flows in a single centered column. At the very top, the page controller presents a dramatic Plans & Pricing headline with supporting text and compact controls, setting a high-energy, direct-response tone. Immediately beneath, a dense, bar-like filters section gives users powerful yet simple ways to refine the available work-from-home starter plans. Scrolling down within the same column, a vertically stacked list of bold, card-like plan tiles fills the central portion of the panel; each card stretches nearly the full width of the panel with clear separation, strong pricing typography, and highlighted key benefits. At the very bottom of the panel, separated by a generous band of spacing, a wide, bright CTA strip invites the user to apply, mirroring the hero-style button from the original mockup. The entire experience feels like a focused, conversion-oriented funnel placed in front of an aspirational lifestyle backdrop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller is anchored at the top of the central dark panel, starting approximately 24–32px below the panel’s top edge. It spans the full usable width inside the panel, leaving 24–32px of horizontal padding on both left and right. The main page title ('Plans & Pricing') sits centered or slightly top-aligned within this block, using large, bold, high-contrast typography that consumes roughly the upper half of the controller’s height. Directly below the title, separated by about 8–12px, a 1–2 line supporting description runs full-width, set in a smaller but still prominent type size, explaining that users can compare work-from-home starter plans and refine them. Beneath this, with another 12–16px of spacing, a compact horizontal strip hosts any primary page-level controls (such as a sort dropdown or toggle between views). These controls are aligned center or slightly right-biased within the same single column and maintain consistent 16–24px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding inside their elements. Overall the controller block visually occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the panel’s height and serves as the main anchor that pulls the eye in from the background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sits at the top of the central overlay panel in normal document flow. It does not float independently but defines the top boundary for all subsequent components inside the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters bar sits directly beneath the page controller, separated from it by a clear 24–32px vertical gap to signal a new functional section while still feeling tightly connected in the overall funnel. It extends the full internal width of the panel, respecting the same 24–32px left/right padding as the controller. Visually, it behaves like a dense, single-row or stacked-form band: at the top of this band, a short instruction line (e.g., 'Filter plans by budget and time commitment') is left-aligned within the column, using a medium-weight type size and sitting about 4–8px above the input controls. Below this instruction, the actual filter inputs (dropdowns, checkboxes, range sliders) are arranged in a single column on smaller widths, each full-width and stacked with 8–12px vertical spacing. On wider screens, 2–3 filters may sit side-by-side in a loose grid but still feel like they belong to one unified bar, with each filter occupying roughly a third or half of the panel width and 16px horizontal gaps between them. The overall band has a slightly lighter or subtly outlined background treatment compared to the main panel (e.g., a darker gray sub-panel or faint border), creating a clearly defined 'control bar' that occupies about 10–15% of the panel height before yielding to the results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the column; it scrolls with the content. On taller viewports it remains near the top; no sticky behavior is required by default, though it should visually feel like a cohesive bar within the central panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list begins immediately below the filters bar, with a vertical gap of approximately 24px to distinguish the transition from controls to content. It spans the same internal width as the components above, running almost full-width inside the panel with 24–32px padding on both sides. The list area forms the central, scrollable column within the panel, occupying roughly the middle 50–55% of the panel’s height on initial load (and extending further as more plans are available). Each plan card within this list is stacked one after another in a linear vertical sequence, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between cards. The list itself does not introduce complex multi-column layouts; it maintains the single-column, funnel-like flow from the mockup, keeping the user’s focus moving straight down the page. If the content exceeds the viewport height, the list scrolls naturally beneath the fixed hero background, while the central panel simply grows taller or allows the browser window to scroll, preserving the illusion of a tall promotional strip floating over the beach image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard scrolling content area in normal flow. It expands vertically as more plan cards are added, pushing the global CTA further down the page while keeping everything within the same centered panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each plan card is a horizontally full-width tile within the results list, inset by the panel’s standard 24–32px side padding. It visually resembles a miniaturized version of the main panel: a dark, slightly translucent rectangle with a subtle gradient or vignette, soft edges, and a shallow drop shadow to lift it off the background of the central panel. The card’s height is content-driven but typically occupies about 20–25% of the panel height on first view. At the top of the card, a bold plan name and tier label (e.g., 'Starter Plan', 'Pro Plan') are left-aligned and take up the first line or two, with strong, high-contrast type and about 8–12px of padding above. Immediately beneath, with a tight 4–8px gap, the monthly price or entry cost is displayed in larger, more dramatic typography (echoing the simplifying statement style from the mockup), potentially paired with a smaller secondary line for notes (e.g., 'No hidden fees'). Below the price, separated by about 8–12px, a compact list of 3–5 key bullet points or a short paragraph summarizes the main benefits, maintaining the promotional tone. Near the bottom edge of the card, and aligned left or full-width depending on design, sits the per-plan action area: this may be a button or text link (e.g., 'View details' or 'See full plan') placed atop a small visual accent (underline, subtle highlight) that points users into the next step while allowing the global CTA to remain the ultimate conversion anchor. Internal padding on all sides of the card is about 16–24px, with vertical spacing inside following the 8–16px scale to keep the card visually dense yet scannable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline item within the results list; cards expand vertically with content and push subsequent cards downward in the single-column stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global apply CTA sits at the bottom of the central panel, directly beneath the last plan card, separated by a generous 32–48px vertical gap to clearly frame it as the final step in the funnel. It forms a wide, high-impact horizontal band that spans nearly the entire internal width of the panel, with consistent 24–32px left and right padding. Within this band, the primary Apply or Get Started button is centered horizontally and sized to occupy roughly 60–80% of the panel’s width, making it feel substantial and impossible to miss. The button itself uses the brightest, most saturated accent color from the palette (e.g., yellow or red), with 14–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, and a strong drop shadow to echo the hero CTA from the original mockup. Above or just below the button, a short, 1–2 line payoff statement (e.g., 'Launch your work-from-home business in as little as 7 days') is aligned center, using a mix of teal and white text to mirror the bottom statement from the mockup. This CTA block consumes roughly the bottom 10–20% of the panel height and serves as the culmination of the visual flow: after scanning the list of plans, users are gently but clearly directed down into this brightly emphasized next step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored at the bottom of the content flow. As more cards are added to the list, this CTA is pushed further down but always remains the last visible section within the central panel.

name: Plan Detail
title: Plan Details
description: Displays full details for a selected starter plan based on a URL parameter and provides an embedded sign-up flow for that specific plan.

core_features: Read the plan identifier from the URL parameter `id` and fetch full plan details using the `getPlanDetails` interface.

Display key plan information from the `Plan` entity, including name, formatted price, currency symbol, description, training module count, module titles, highlighted features, and coaching availability.
Show FAQs specific to the selected plan using `getPlanDetails.faqs` to help users understand inclusions, conditions, and how the plan works.
Show user reviews specific to the selected plan using `getPlanDetails.userReviews`, including reviewer name, rating, headline, and comment.
Provide a primary call-to-action button labeled "Get Started with This Plan" that focuses or scrolls to the embedded sign-up form for the currently loaded plan.
Provide an embedded sign-up form where the selected plan is preselected based on the URL parameter and `getPlanDetails.plan`, and where the user can enter name and email and click Continue.
Submit the sign-up form via the `submitPlanSignup` interface and show success or error feedback based on the response.
Handle loading states (while fetching plan details or submitting the sign-up) and error states (missing/invalid plan ID, failed API responses) without using static fallback content.
user_workflows:
Viewing a plan’s details:
1. User navigates to `plan_detail.html?id={Plan.id}` from another page.
2. On page initialization, the page reads the `id` URL parameter.
3. The page controller calls `getPlanDetails` with `planId = id`.
4. While waiting, a loading state is shown instead of plan content.
5. When `getPlanDetails` resolves successfully, the page stores the returned `plan`, `formattedPrice`, `currencySymbol`, `faqs`, and `userReviews` in page state.
6. The plan summary, modules, features, coaching info, FAQs, and reviews components render based on this dynamic data.
7. If `getPlanDetails` fails or returns no plan, the page shows a functional "plan not found"/error state and hides the sign-up CTA and form.

Starting sign-up from the primary CTA:
1. User reviews the plan information on the page.
2. User clicks the primary button labeled "Get Started with This Plan".
3. The page scrolls or jumps focus to the embedded sign-up form section corresponding to the currently loaded plan.
4. The sign-up form shows the selected plan’s name and formatted price in a read-only way, using data from `getPlanDetails.plan` and `formattedPrice` (no re-selection control is required).
5. User inputs their name and email into the form fields.
6. User clicks the form’s main action button (e.g., "Continue"), which triggers validation and, if valid, submission via `submitPlanSignup` with `planId` from the loaded plan, plus the entered name and email.

Completing the embedded sign-up form:
1. Upon form submission, client-side validation checks that name and email fields are not empty and that email format is valid.
2. If validation fails, inline error indicators appear on the relevant fields and the submission is blocked until corrected.
3. If validation passes, a submitting state is shown (e.g., disabling the button) and `submitPlanSignup` is called with `planId`, `name`, and `email`.
4. When `submitPlanSignup` resolves:
   - If `success` is true, a confirmation message is displayed using the returned `message` and/or `planSignup` snapshot fields, and the form can optionally clear name and email inputs or mark itself as completed.
   - If `success` is false, an error message is displayed using the returned `message`, and the form remains editable so the user can retry.
5. User can dismiss the confirmation or error message if desired, which keeps the loaded plan details visible.

Handling missing or changed URL parameters:
1. If the page is loaded without an `id` URL parameter, the controller does not call `getPlanDetails` and instead shows a functional error state indicating that no plan can be displayed.
2. If the `id` parameter changes (for example, through in-page navigation that updates the address bar), the page controller detects the change, re-enters a loading state, and calls `getPlanDetails` again with the new `planId`.
3. All plan-specific components (summary, FAQs, reviews, and the sign-up form’s preselected plan) re-bind to the newly returned plan data.
4. Any pending sign-up state for the previous plan is reset or clearly separated from the new plan’s state so that new submissions are associated only with the currently loaded `planId`.
interactions:
Initial load / data fetch:
- Trigger: Page load or URL change to `plan_detail.html?id={Plan.id}`.
- Behavior: The page reads `id` from the URL and invokes `getPlanDetails(planId = id)`. While awaiting the response, a loading indicator is shown instead of plan content.

"Get Started with This Plan" button click:
- Trigger: User clicks the primary CTA.
- Behavior: The page scrolls the viewport to the embedded sign-up form container for the current plan and moves keyboard focus to the first editable field (typically the name input). No additional API calls are made here, as the plan is already loaded.

FAQ expand/collapse interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks on an FAQ question in the FAQ list.
- Behavior: The corresponding answer toggles between expanded and collapsed states for that specific item. This interaction only affects local UI state and does not call any interfaces.

Review list navigation (e.g., pagination or show-more):
- Trigger: User clicks a "Show more" or pagination control in the reviews list.
- Behavior: The component either reveals more of the already loaded `userReviews` items or switches between pages of them, using only the data provided by `getPlanDetails.userReviews` and local component state.

Form field input:
- Trigger: User types into the name or email fields in the sign-up form.
- Behavior: Local form state updates for those fields and clears any validation errors previously shown for that field. No API calls are made on input.

Sign-up form submission:
- Trigger: User clicks the form’s main action button (e.g., "Continue") or presses Enter while focused inside the form.
- Behavior: The form validates required fields (name and email) and basic email format. If validation passes, the component calls `submitPlanSignup` with `planId` from the current `plan`, and the current `name` and `email` field values. The submit button is disabled while waiting for the response.

Sign-up result handling:
- Trigger: `submitPlanSignup` returns a response.
- Behavior:
  - On `success: true`, the form displays a success message using `message` and can read `planSignup.plan_name_snapshot` and `planSignup.price_snapshot` for contextual confirmation text. The form may lock or clear fields based on the desired flow.
  - On `success: false`, the form displays an error message using `message`, keeps the input values so the user can adjust them, and re-enables the submit button.

Dismiss feedback message:
- Trigger: User clicks a close icon or button on the success/error feedback component.
- Behavior: The feedback message is hidden while retaining the underlying plan details and any completed sign-up state. No additional API calls are made.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state driver is the URL parameter `id`. On every page load and on any detected change to `id` in the query string, the page controller reads `id` and, if present, calls `getPlanDetails(planId = id)`. If `id` is missing or empty, the controller does not call `getPlanDetails` and instead renders a functional error/empty state indicating that no plan can be shown. If `getPlanDetails` returns successfully with a plan object, the page stores the following in memory for the current view: the `Plan` entity (`plan`), `formattedPrice`, `currencySymbol`, `faqs`, and `userReviews`. All components that display plan information derive their content from this stored state. The sign-up form uses the loaded `plan.id` as the `planId` parameter for `submitPlanSignup`, ensuring that the submitted sign-up is always tied to the plan specified by the URL `id`. Sign-up-related UI state (such as current form inputs, validation errors, loading flags, and the last `submitPlanSignup` response) is kept in local component state and is reset when the `id` parameter changes so that prior submissions do not affect new plan views. No persistent storage mechanisms such as localStorage or cookies are used; the URL parameter is the only external state, and all other state is in-memory for the duration of the page session.

components.functionality: Coordinates reading the URL parameter `id`, fetching plan details via `getPlanDetails`, managing global loading and error states, and providing the loaded plan data to child components.

Displays the core plan summary including plan name, price (using formattedPrice and currencySymbol), description, training module count, and status (active/inactive/archived), giving the user a concise overview of the selected plan.
Shows the number of training modules and the list of module titles for the selected plan so users can see what topics are included.
Displays key highlighted features for the plan as a dynamic list of bullet points, based on the `highlighted_features` field.
Indicates whether the selected plan includes one-on-one coaching and may show plan attributes related to training intensity using the plan’s boolean coaching flag and other fields.
Lists FAQs specific to the current plan and allows users to expand or collapse individual questions to reveal the corresponding answers.
Displays user reviews associated with the current plan, including reviewer name, rating, headline, and comment, and optionally supports pagination or show-more behavior using only the loaded data.
Renders the primary button labeled "Get Started with This Plan" that, when clicked, scrolls or jumps the user to the embedded sign-up form for the currently loaded plan.

Provides an embedded sign-up form where the currently loaded plan is preselected, and the user can enter name and email and click Continue. On submit, it validates inputs and calls `submitPlanSignup` with the selected plan ID.
Shows success or error feedback after a sign-up attempt, using the `submitPlanSignup` response to inform the user about the result of their submission.
Displays a functional message and hides plan-specific components when the URL `id` parameter is missing or when `getPlanDetails` does not return a valid plan.

Centered over it, a dark translucent panel spans roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width around 560–640px) with generous left and right breathing room to showcase the background image. Vertically, above-the-fold space within the panel is roughly split so that about 60–65% is devoted to messaging and context (plan-summary-section + plan-coaching-info + the introductory part of the signup-form) and 35–40% is taken by the actual form fields and primary CTA. As the user scrolls, the lower half of the panel is consumed by the details: modules, features, reviews, and the FAQ, each separated as clear stacked sections with 24–32px gaps between major blocks and 8–16px between internal text elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Inside the central panel, content is moderately dense: text blocks, lists, and controls are stacked with limited but sufficient vertical space so that the content feels focused and promotional without becoming cramped. Layout uses an 8px spacing base: 8–16px between related texts (headlines and subcopy, labels and fields) and 24–32px between larger sections (summary to signup, signup to modules, modules to features, etc.). Outside the panel, the background remains visually open and uncluttered, providing breathing room and contrast that keeps the central column from feeling heavy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a strong top-to-bottom funnel, just like the mockup. The eye first lands on the bold, high-contrast plan summary at the top of the panel, then is led to the subtle but reassuring coaching/status indicator directly beneath it, and then down into the signup form fields. The full-width, saturated primary CTA button at the bottom of the form is a clear focal point and primary action. After that, secondary attention flows to the modules and features sections that justify the offer, followed by social proof in the review section and risk-reducing clarity in the FAQ at the bottom. In an error or empty state, the not-found-panel takes over the central position with a single simplified flow back to discovery or selection.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a full-bleed, high-energy lifestyle photo background, such as a bright beach scene, covering the entire viewport. Floating in the center is a tall, dark, translucent panel with soft edges and subtle shadow, about half the width of the screen, clearly separated from the background but still letting a hint of the image show through. All content is stacked in a single vertical column inside this panel, starting near the top with a powerful plan title and key promise, followed immediately by a compact coaching status band. Just below, still above the fold, a focused signup block presents a small cluster of form fields and a bold, full-width CTA button, echoing the direct-response feel of the original mockup. As the user scrolls down this same column, the panel reveals progressively more detailed sections: an itemized list of plan modules, a supporting feature list, testimonial-style reviews, and a final accordion FAQ. Gaps of roughly 24–32px visually separate these blocks, while smaller 8–16px gaps manage spacing within each section. The result is a vertically flowing, conversion-centric layout: all attention is funneled through the bold central panel, with the saturated background and strong typography creating a dramatic, promotional atmosphere aligned with the original design DNA.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the full-page container and orchestrator rather than a visible UI element. It occupies the entire viewport, setting a full-bleed lifestyle photo background that extends edge to edge and top to bottom. Over this background, it centers a single tall, dark translucent content panel both horizontally and with generous top and bottom margins (approximately 10–15% of viewport height above and below the panel on larger screens, reduced on smaller screens). This central panel has a max-width of about 560–640px and occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop, shrinking responsively down to around 90% width on mobile. Inside this panel, the controller arranges all other components in a single vertical column, aligned left but visually centered within the panel, respecting a consistent internal padding of about 24–32px on all sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-viewport container: the background image is fixed or covers the viewport with a cover setting, while the central content panel scrolls normally within the page. The component itself has no discrete visual footprint beyond defining the overall canvas and placing the overlay panel in the center.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the very top of the central dark panel, this section functions as the hero headline and primary pitch area. It begins immediately below the panel’s internal top padding (about 24px), occupying roughly the upper 20–25% of the above-the-fold content. The plan name appears as a large, bold headline spanning the full width of the panel content area, using high-contrast typography and, if desired, a subtle gradient or highlight treatment to echo the dramatic promotional style. Directly beneath, with 8–12px of vertical space, sits supporting copy: short sentences or a tight bullet cluster summarizing the main benefits and core promise of the plan. An optional line for price or key value metrics appears within the same block, separated by 4–8px spacing from adjacent text. All elements are left-aligned inside the panel, but the total block is visually centered horizontally by the panel itself. A larger 24–32px gap below this section clearly separates it from the next component, the coaching/status indicator.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level behavior within the central column; it scrolls with the panel content and anchors the top of the information hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The modules list appears in the mid-lower portion of the central panel, after the signup area and any feedback message. There is a clear major-section gap of roughly 24–32px above it (from the bottom of the signup-feedback or, if no feedback is visible, from the CTA). The section starts with a bold subheading like 'Inside this plan' or 'Training modules included', set slightly smaller than the main hero headline but clearly more prominent than the body text, spanning the full content width. Beneath this heading, with 8–12px of spacing, individual modules are listed in a vertical stack, each item occupying 100% of the panel’s inner width. Each module row can include a short title on the left and one or two lines of description beneath it, separated by 4–8px of space, with 12–16px between modules to keep the list readable but compact. The overall section runs down the panel but remains comfortably within the central column, maintaining 24–32px spacing below before the plan-features-section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing section; it scrolls with the rest of the content and does not stick or float. On smaller screens, the list remains single-column and full-width within the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section follows directly below the modules list, separated by a medium major-section gap of approximately 24px. It occupies the same full inner width of the central panel and continues the single-column layout. A concise heading (for example 'What you get with this plan') sits at the top of this block, aligned left, with 8–12px of space before the feature content. Features are presented as a compact vertical list, where each feature line may include an icon or checkmark at the far left followed by 1–2 lines of descriptive text. Individual features are spaced 8–12px apart to maintain a promotional density without looking crowded. This block typically occupies about 20–25% of the total scrolled panel height, depending on the number of items, and ends with a 24–32px gap before the review section starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally as a single-column section; it is not sticky or fixed and adjusts its height naturally based on the number of features.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coaching info status indicator sits directly below the plan-summary-section and above the signup form, acting as a compact reassurance band. There is a modest 8–12px vertical gap separating it from the summary section above, to keep it visually associated with the plan but distinct as a discrete element. The component spans the entire inner width of the panel and typically presents a pill-shaped badge or small horizontal strip with an icon on the left (for example a headset or checkmark) and a short status phrase such as 'Live coaching included' or '1:1 support available' to the right. The background of this band can use a teal or yellow accent that contrasts with the dark panel while respecting the overall saturated, high-contrast palette. The height of the band is modest (roughly 40–56px), ensuring it does not dominate the hero copy. Below it, a larger 16–24px gap leads into the top of the signup form so that the coaching info reads as a bridge from the promise to the conversion area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a compact inline section in the column; it scrolls with the content and sits in a fixed position in the vertical sequence between the summary and the signup form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ accordion occupies the bottom-most major section of the central panel, anchoring the end of the vertical scroll. It appears after the review section with a clear 24–32px gap. The FAQ heading spans the full inner width and is left-aligned at the top of this block, using a size similar to other secondary headings. Below, each FAQ item is rendered as a full-width accordion row, stacked vertically, with a 8–12px gap between items. The question text is visible by default with a clear affordance (chevron or plus icon) on the far right, and expanded answers push content downward within the central column. The entire FAQ block may consume roughly the lower 20–30% of the panel’s total scrolling content, depending on the number of questions. A final 24–32px padding separates the last FAQ item from the bottom edge of the panel’s inner padding, ensuring the content does not feel cramped against the panel boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling section at the bottom of the panel; expanding and collapsing questions simply grow or shrink the block in place without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review list sits immediately above the FAQ section, functioning as social proof after the user has seen the offer details. It is separated from the features section above by a 24–32px gap. This block begins with a heading such as 'What our members say', left-aligned and spanning the full inner width. Beneath the heading, each review is presented as a vertically stacked card or row occupying 100% of the panel’s inner width. Cards may use a slightly lighter panel tone or subtle outline to differentiate them from the background and are separated by 16–24px of vertical space. Within each card, reviewer name and rating appear in the top row, with the review text below, all arranged in a compact 8–12px spacing pattern. The entire reviews block typically takes up 20–30% of the scrolled panel height, and is followed by a 24–32px gap before the FAQ section starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document order as a vertical stack of cards; it scrolls naturally with no fixed or sticky positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary CTA is rendered as a bold, full-width button within the signup-form component, positioned at the bottom of the form fields block. It sits directly beneath the final input field, separated by 12–16px of vertical space, and spans nearly the entire inner width of the panel content area (minus the panel’s left and right padding). The button height is relatively tall (around 44–52px), with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal internal padding, and uses a saturated color (often bright yellow or red) that contrasts strongly with the dark panel background.
