The label text is centered inside the button and set in bold, uppercase or high-impact type to emphasize action. A small 8–12px gap below the button separates it from the feedback message area when present, ensuring visual distinction while keeping it tightly integrated into the conversion zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the main panel, the CTA behaves as a standard button inside the signup-form, scrolling as part of the form block. Optionally on smaller screens, this CTA may also be mirrored as a sticky bottom-bar button that hugs the bottom edge of the viewport, but the primary definition remains as the full-width button inside the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The signup form is the core conversion block and is placed approximately one third of the way down from the top of the central panel, directly under the coaching-info section, separated by 16–24px of space. It occupies the full inner width of the panel and functions as a compact sub-panel within the larger overlay. The form starts with a short instruction line or subheading (for example 'Secure your spot in this plan now'), in smaller text than the hero but still prominent, followed by 8–12px of space. Below this, one or more input fields (such as name, email, and password) are stacked vertically, each spanning 100% of the available width. Each input field is approximately 40–48px tall, with 8–12px of vertical spacing between fields. Field labels are placed above their respective input boxes with 4–8px spacing, keeping the layout clean and legible. After the final field, a 12–16px gap leads into the plan-signup-cta button, which anchors the bottom of the form’s main body. Overall, the signup block occupies a noticeable but not overwhelming portion of the above-the-fold view, ensuring that both the messaging and the CTA are visible without scrolling on typical desktop screens. Below the button, the form leaves 8–12px of reserved vertical space for the feedback message component, allowing error or success states to appear tightly coupled with the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a single, vertically stacked form block within the panel, scrolling naturally with the rest of the content. It does not fix or float, but its placement high in the panel ensures it is visible early in the scroll experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component appears directly beneath the primary CTA button within the signup-form area, occupying the full inner width of the panel content. When present, it is separated from the button by a small 8–12px gap. The feedback message is contained in a compact horizontal band or small card, approximately 40–64px tall depending on the amount of text. It uses color coding consistent with the high-contrast palette: for example, a warm red or orange background for errors, a green or teal accent for success, and a more neutral dark tone with colored border for informational states. Text is left-aligned and kept to 1–2 lines, with 8–12px of internal padding around it. The message feels tightly integrated with the form, visually reinforcing that it is a direct response to the user’s attempt to sign up. Below this feedback block, there is a 24–32px gap before the modules section starts, so the conversion area remains visually distinct from the deep content that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline within the signup-form area. When hidden, it effectively collapses to zero height, allowing the modules section to move up. It scrolls with the rest of the form and does not use sticky or fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, this empty state replaces all the usual plan detail content inside the central dark panel. The panel itself remains centered over the full-bleed background, but its interior is simplified. A large, clear message is vertically and horizontally centered within the panel’s inner padding area, occupying roughly the central 40–50% of the panel’s height. The main line communicates that the plan was not found, using bold white text similar in scale to the hero headline. Below it, separated by 8–12px, a shorter explanatory line or two is shown in smaller, softer text. Beneath this copy, with a 16–24px gap, a single full-width button or link (reusing the primary CTA styling or a slightly less intense variant) invites the user to return to the plan selection or home page. The overall empty state keeps the same dramatic, high-contrast aesthetic but significantly reduces content density, leaving generous negative space above and below the text block to emphasize clarity and prevent confusion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The not-found state occupies the same spatial footprint as the regular content column but takes over the full interior of the central panel when shown. It scrolls minimally or not at all, depending on viewport height, effectively acting like a simplified modal-like view within the existing overlay.

name: Webinars
title: Webinars
description: Browse and filter upcoming webinars with configurable date range, time of day, topic type, and sort order, then navigate to individual webinar detail pages.

core_features: Fetch and display available filter and sort options for webinars using the getWebinarFilterOptions interface.
List upcoming webinars using the searchWebinars interface, driven entirely by URL parameters for date range, time of day, topic types, and sort order.
Allow users to change filters (date range presets, optional custom dates, time of day, topic types) and update the URL so results can be shared and revisited.
Allow users to change sort order and see the list of webinars update accordingly.
Handle empty, loading, and error states for the webinar listing without relying on static or hardcoded data.
Allow users to click a webinar result to navigate to webinar_detail.html with the corresponding webinar id in the URL.
user_workflows:
Initial page load and auto-search workflow:
1. User opens webinars.html, optionally with URL parameters (startDate, endDate, timeOfDay, topicTypes, sortBy, dateRangePreset, page, pageSize).
2. The page parses URL parameters into an internal filter state.
3. The page calls getWebinarFilterOptions with no parameters.
4. When filter options return, the page binds them to the filter controls (date range presets, time of day options, topic type options, sort options).
5. The page calls searchWebinars with startDate, endDate, timeOfDay, topicTypes, and sortBy derived from the URL (or defaults if absent).
6. While waiting for search results, a loading state is shown.
7. When results arrive, the page displays the webinar list for the current page (if pagination is implemented client-side), or an empty state if no webinars are returned.

Change date range preset workflow:
1. User opens the date range preset control and selects one of the options returned by getWebinarFilterOptions.dateRangePresets.
2. The selection updates a dateRangePreset value in the filter state and corresponding URL parameter (?dateRangePreset=selectedValue).
3. A date range computation utility derives startDate and endDate from the selected preset and the current date/time; these derived values are written to both filter state and URL parameters (?startDate=..., ?endDate=...).
4. Any existing page parameter is reset to the first page in the URL (e.g., ?page=1).
5. The page triggers a new searchWebinars call with the updated startDate and endDate (and existing timeOfDay, topicTypes, sortBy values from the URL).
6. The webinar list updates based on the new results.

Change time of day or topic type filters workflow:
1. User selects or deselects time of day and/or topic type filters using the options from getWebinarFilterOptions.timeOfDayOptions and getWebinarFilterOptions.topicTypeOptions.
2. The selected timeOfDay value and topicTypes collection are updated in filter state and mirrored in URL parameters (e.g., ?timeOfDay=value, ?topicTypes=value1,value2).
3. The page parameter is reset (e.g., ?page=1).
4. The page calls searchWebinars with the current startDate, endDate, timeOfDay, topicTypes, and sortBy values from the filter state/URL.
5. The webinar list updates to reflect the new filters.

Change sort order workflow:
1. User selects a sort option from the list returned by getWebinarFilterOptions.sortOptions.
2. The selected sortBy value is stored in filter state and in the URL parameter (?sortBy=selectedValue).
3. The page parameter is reset (e.g., ?page=1).
4. The page calls searchWebinars with the current startDate, endDate, timeOfDay, topicTypes, and the new sortBy value.
5. The webinar list re-renders using the new sort order.

Pagination workflow (client-side, state via URL only):
1. After a searchWebinars call returns an array of webinars, the page calculates total pages based on a pageSize parameter (from URL or a default) and the length of the results array.
2. User clicks a pagination control (Next, Previous, or a page number).
3. The page parameter (?page=n) is updated in the URL; the array returned by the most recent searchWebinars call is sliced client-side to show only the subset corresponding to the selected page.
4. If any filter/sort change occurs, page is reset to 1 in the URL and the list is recalculated from the new results.

Navigate to webinar detail workflow:
1. User clicks anywhere on a webinar card or on a dedicated "view details" control within the card.
2. The click handler reads the webinar.id from the bound Webinar object.
3. The browser navigates to webinar_detail.html with the corresponding id parameter (e.g., webinar_detail.html?id=<webinar.id>), without altering other filters on the current page.
4. User can use the browser back button to return to webinars.html, preserving the URL parameters and thus the previous filter, sort, and pagination state.

Share and revisit filters workflow:
1. After adjusting filters and sort options, the current filter/sort/pagination state is reflected in the URL (startDate, endDate, timeOfDay, topicTypes, sortBy, dateRangePreset, page, pageSize).
2. User copies the URL and shares it or bookmarks it.
3. Another visitor opens the shared URL.
4. On load, the page parses the URL parameters to reconstruct the exact filter and sort state.
5. The page calls searchWebinars using these parameters and repopulates the list, effectively restoring the shared state.
interactions: On page load, parse URL parameters into a filter state object and trigger calls to getWebinarFilterOptions and searchWebinars.
On URL parameter change (initiated by the page itself or via browser navigation history), re-derive filter state and, when relevant parameters differ, call searchWebinars again.
On selecting a date range preset, update the dateRangePreset URL parameter, compute and update startDate/endDate, reset page, and re-run searchWebinars.

On adjusting a custom date range (if provided as optional fields), update startDate/endDate URL parameters directly, clear or update dateRangePreset to match behavior, reset page, and re-run searchWebinars.
On selecting a time of day option, update the timeOfDay URL parameter and re-run searchWebinars.
On selecting or deselecting topic types (multi-select), update a topicTypes URL parameter using a URL-safe list format, then re-run searchWebinars.
On selecting a sort option, update the sortBy URL parameter and re-run searchWebinars.
On clicking a pagination control, update the page URL parameter and re-render the currently loaded webinars array subset without calling searchWebinars again.
On clicking a webinar card, navigate to webinar_detail.html with the webinar id passed as the id URL parameter.
On detecting an empty result set from searchWebinars, display an empty-state component with options to adjust or clear filters, and update filters (and URL) if the user chooses to clear them.
On encountering an error during getWebinarFilterOptions or searchWebinars, display an error-state component and provide a retry interaction that re-calls the appropriate interface.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is entirely driven by URL parameters and API responses.

URL parameters:
- startDate: ISO-like date or datetime string representing the start of the search window; used directly as the startDate argument for searchWebinars.
- endDate: ISO-like date or datetime string representing the end of the search window; used directly as the endDate argument for searchWebinars.
- timeOfDay: A single value matching the timeOfDay enum used by the API (e.g., one of the values exposed by getWebinarFilterOptions.timeOfDayOptions[].value); used directly as the timeOfDay argument for searchWebinars. If absent, the timeOfDay argument is passed as null or omitted according to implementation.
- topicTypes: A URL-safe list of topic type values (e.g., comma-separated values corresponding to getWebinarFilterOptions.topicTypeOptions[].value). Parsed into an array and passed as the topicTypes argument to searchWebinars. If absent or empty, topicTypes is treated as no filtering by topic.
- sortBy: One of the values from getWebinarFilterOptions.sortOptions[].value; passed directly as the sortBy argument to searchWebinars. If absent, a default sort value is taken from the filter options (for example, the first sort option) and written back to the URL.
- dateRangePreset: Matches one of getWebinarFilterOptions.dateRangePresets[].value; when present, the page computes startDate and endDate from this preset and the current date/time. If both dateRangePreset and explicit startDate/endDate are present, the implementation can either prioritize explicit dates or keep them synchronized; this rule should be defined consistently and applied before calling searchWebinars.
- page: Positive integer indicating the current page of client-side pagination. Used only for slicing the array returned by searchWebinars; does not affect API calls.
- pageSize: Positive integer specifying how many webinars to show per page. Used for client-side pagination; if absent, a default page size is used, and that default value may optionally be written to the URL.

State behavior:
- On initial load, the page reads all URL parameters, normalizes them (e.g., ensuring page and pageSize are valid integers, arrays are properly parsed from topicTypes), and creates an internal state object that mirrors the URL.
- Whenever a user changes filters or sort settings through the UI, the page updates the internal state, writes corresponding changes to the URL parameters (modifying the query string), and then calls searchWebinars with the updated parameters.
- The internal state is not persisted elsewhere (no localStorage or cookies); the URL is the single source of truth for filter, sort, and pagination state.
- Browser back/forward navigation is handled by listening to URL changes and re-synchronizing filter state and search results from the query parameters.
- The result set from searchWebinars is stored in memory only for the duration of the page session. The list of webinars is filtered for display using the page and pageSize values, without altering the original results array.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial loading of filter options and webinar results. Parses URL parameters into an internal filter state, requests filter options via getWebinarFilterOptions, then calls searchWebinars using values derived from the URL (and from dateRangePreset if present). Also re-synchronizes state when the URL changes (e.g., back/forward navigation).

Provides controls for date range (preset and optional custom dates), time of day, and topic type filters, as well as a clear/reset filters control. Uses filter options returned by getWebinarFilterOptions and writes all changes to URL parameters. Triggers searchWebinars via the page-init logic when filters change.

Displays and manages the sort order for webinar results using the options from getWebinarFilterOptions.sortOptions. Updates the sortBy URL parameter on selection and triggers a re-call to searchWebinars.

Handles client-side pagination of the webinars returned from searchWebinars. Uses page and pageSize from URL parameters to determine which slice of the webinar results to display. Updates the page URL parameter when users navigate between pages.

Displays the list of webinars returned by searchWebinars, restricted to the current page as determined by the pagination component. Each item is rendered as a card showing key attributes (e.g., title, start date/time, topic type, time of day) directly from the Webinar model. Provides click interaction for navigating to webinar_detail.html using the webinar id.

Displays when searchWebinars returns an empty array. Reads current filters from the URL and optionally provides actions such as clearing filters or reverting to broader defaults. When actions are used, updates URL parameters and indirectly triggers searchWebinars via the page-init logic.
Shows a loading state while getWebinarFilterOptions or searchWebinars are in progress. Appears and disappears based on pending state flags managed by the page-init component.
Displays error messages when calls to getWebinarFilterOptions or searchWebinars fail. Provides a retry mechanism that re-invokes the failed interface using the current URL-derived parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, vertically stacked content column housed inside a tall, dark translucent panel that sits centered over a full-bleed lifestyle photo (e.g., beach). All components are arranged in a linear top-to-bottom flow within this panel: page-init messaging at the top; filters below; then sort control; then the main webinars results area; finally pagination at the bottom. Empty, loading, and error states occupy the same visual territory as the results list, swapping into that region as needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, vertically stacked content column housed inside a tall, dark translucent panel that sits centered over a full-bleed lifestyle photo (e.g., beach). All components are arranged in a linear top-to-bottom flow within this panel: page-init messaging at the top; filters below; then sort control; then the main webinars results area; finally pagination at the bottom. Empty, loading, and error states occupy the same visual territory as the results list, swapping into that region as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width ~640px), vertically spanning from near the top margin down toward the bottom with generous breathing room against the background image. Within the panel, about the top 30–35% is devoted to messaging & configuration (page-init + filter bar), and the lower 65–70% is reserved for the dynamic content (sort control + results list or states + pagination). Each component spans nearly the full internal width of the panel, with consistent padding (around 24–32px horizontal, ~24px vertical) and 16–32px vertical gaps between major sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately dense and conversion-focused: text and controls are stacked with limited but sufficient vertical spacing to keep everything readable while creating a sense of urgency and focus on discovering webinars. There is no multi-column complexity; elements are mostly full-width and vertically aligned. The panel itself sits in a visually open, uncluttered photographic background which provides contrast and relief from the dense central content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey starts at the top of the panel with a bold, high-contrast H1 and short explanatory copy (page-init), then flows down to the filter bar that invites interaction. From there, the eye is led into the sort control just above the list, then into the vertical stack of webinar results. The bottom of the panel reinforces continuation with pagination controls. If there are no results, or the list is loading or errored, that state fills the results region but keeps the same top-to-bottom flow: headline → filters → state message → (optional) retry or change-filters prompt → pagination region (if applicable).

layout.overall_description: The Webinars page appears as a dramatic, conversion-focused hero overlay on top of a full-bleed beach photo. Centered in the viewport is a tall, dark, semi-transparent panel with soft edges and subtle drop shadow, occupying around half the page width and framed by the colorful background on both sides. At the top of this panel, a bold, gradient-tinted headline announces the Webinars section, followed by concise supporting copy that explains the value of upcoming trainings. Directly beneath, a stacked filter bar lets users refine by date range, time of day, and topic in an intuitive, full-width block. As the user scrolls further down within the panel, a compact sort control sits aligned near the top-right of the results region, leading into a vertically stacked list of webinar cards, each spanning the panel width. The bottom of the panel holds centered pagination controls, visually anchoring the page and inviting the user to explore more sessions. When there are no webinars, a loading phase, or an error, a high-contrast message and icon replace the list in the same area, but the overall structure—hero text at top, filters in the middle, dynamic results region below, navigation at the bottom—remains intact, all within a single, focused vertical funnel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component sits at the very top of the central dark panel, acting as the hero messaging block. It is horizontally centered and spans roughly 90–100% of the panel’s internal width, with about 24px of padding to the left and right edges of the panel. There is a vertical top margin of approximately 32–48px inside the panel before the H1 appears, allowing the hero message to breathe against the beach background. The main headline (e.g., “Live Webinars”) is large and bold, occupying a single or double line centered at the top, consuming roughly the top 10–15% of the panel height on desktop. Immediately below, with 8–16px vertical spacing, sits 2–3 lines of supporting copy in a lighter color, still center-aligned, explaining what the webinars help users achieve (e.g., growing their work-from-home business). All text is stacked in a linear block, without side-by-side layout, reinforcing the single-column flow. The bottom of this component ends with a 24–32px gap before the filter bar begins, clearly separating hero messaging from interactive controls while maintaining a tight, promotional feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable panel; it remains at the top of the content flow as the user scrolls the rest of the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is positioned directly beneath the page-init hero copy, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It reads as the main interaction module for configuring webinars, occupying the full internal width of the panel (minus consistent left/right padding of about 24–32px). The filter bar itself appears as a slightly darker band within the panel, with its own internal padding of around 16–24px on all sides. Inside this band, controls for date range, time of day, topic type, and any reset/apply actions are arranged in a vertical stack on narrow viewports (mobile/small) and can tighten into two rows on wider widths, but remain visually perceived as a single block. Labels sit above each input, maintaining the top-to-bottom reading order: label, then control, with 8px spacing between them and 16–24px between groups. The overall height of the filter bar accounts for roughly 15–25% of the panel height when all filters are visible, making it a substantial, highly noticeable section. There is a clear vertical gap of about 24px between the bottom of the filter bar and the sort control/results region below, reinforcing the step from configuring filters to seeing results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow. Optionally, on very tall lists, the filter bar can become sticky relative to the top of the viewport within the panel area so that it remains visible as the user scrolls results, but it does not detach from the panel or become a global page header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control component sits immediately below the filter bar, separated by approximately 16–24px of vertical space, marking the transition from configuration to results. It occupies a single horizontal line near the top of the results region. The entire sort control area spans the full internal width of the panel, but the actual sort UI (label + dropdown or segmented button) is right-aligned within this width, occupying roughly 40–50% of the row on desktop. On narrow screens, the sort control reflows to center alignment with full-width control, but still remains above the list. There is minimal vertical height—about 40–56px total—making it visually lighter than the filter bar. Below the sort-control row is a 16px vertical gap before the first item in the results list, keeping them visually connected but distinct. The sort label is smaller and less visually bold than the hero and filter labels, signaling its secondary importance while still being immediately discoverable when the user’s eye moves downward toward the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row anchored at the top of the results region; it scrolls away as the user moves far down the list and reappears when scrolling back up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-list component occupies the main body of the lower portion of the panel, beginning just below the sort control with roughly 16px of vertical spacing. It spans nearly the full width of the panel (respecting the same 24–32px left/right padding), and its height expands as needed, becoming the primary scrollable region within the page. Each webinar entry is displayed as a vertically stacked card, full-width within this column, with 16–24px vertical spacing between cards. Cards themselves are moderately dense, with internal padding of about 16–24px, and can feature a primary line (webinar title), secondary lines (date, time, topic), and a CTA (e.g., “View details”) aligned consistently—typically title at the top, meta information below, and a right-aligned or bottom-aligned CTA button. The list aims to fill roughly the lower 50–60% of the panel height before needing to scroll on a standard laptop viewport, maintaining the sense that the user is exploring a curated set of opportunities. When content exceeds the initial viewport height, the background image remains largely visible beyond the panel edges, while the panel itself scrolls normally. If there are no results, or if loading/error states apply, this entire region is replaced by the relevant state component while retaining the same spatial footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flexible-height scrollable content within the central panel; it grows and shrinks based on number of results, pushing the pagination control downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-control component sits at the very bottom of the central panel’s content stack, directly underneath the results list (or empty/error state) with about 24–32px of vertical spacing. It is horizontally centered and spans approximately 60–80% of the panel’s internal width, leaving some space on the sides to preserve the single-column feel. The pagination elements (previous/next buttons, page numbers) are arranged on a single horizontal line, with the primary page number region centered; the entire control has a modest vertical height (around 40–56px). There is a final bottom padding inside the panel of about 24–32px below the pagination, ensuring the controls don’t touch the panel’s edge and providing a comfortable end to the scroll. Visually, pagination is less bold than the headline or filter bar, but uses high-contrast buttons (e.g., vivid colored active page, clear hover states) consistent with the direct-response style, signaling that continuing exploration is encouraged.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the natural bottom of the content; it moves further down as more results are loaded or when the viewport height is small, and is reached through normal scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state component occupies the same spatial region as the results list, appearing whenever filters yield no matching webinars. It is vertically centered within the space that would normally be filled by results, starting just below the sort control and ending above the pagination. Horizontally, it uses the same width as the list (full panel width minus 24–32px side padding). The content is stacked in a simple, vertical arrangement: at the top, a subtle icon or illustration in a muted yet brand-aligned color; below that, a bold primary line (e.g., “No webinars match your filters”), then a smaller secondary line explaining what the user can try (e.g., “Adjust the date range or topic type to see more options”), with about 8–16px between lines. Optionally, a small, high-contrast inline link or button to “Clear filters” can appear below the message, centered. The entire block is grouped into a tight vertical column, occupying roughly 30–40% of the visible panel height for clarity, with ample blank space above and below to preserve the clean, hero-like feel even when there’s no data.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the results list within the same layout region. It does not alter the position of the sort control above or the pagination area below, although pagination can be hidden when empty if desired.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator is displayed over the results region whenever webinars are being fetched. Spatially, it is centered both horizontally and vertically within the area normally occupied by the results list (between the sort control and the pagination). It may appear as a semi-opaque dark overlay that slightly dims any underlying content, with a bright spinner or animated progress element in the middle, respecting the bold, high-contrast style. The overlay respects the same left/right boundaries as the list (no full-screen takeover), reinforcing that only the data region is loading while the hero and filter bar remain visible and interactive (if appropriate). Vertical spacing relative to the sort control and pagination is effectively shared: the spinner sits midway in this zone, usually around the middle 30–40% of the panel height, leaving visible but subdued space above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient overlay anchored to the bounds of the results region; it scrolls with the panel content and disappears once loading completes, revealing the list or an empty/error state beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-state component occupies the results-list area when there is a failure fetching webinars. It is positioned starting just below the sort control, using the full internal width of the panel, and extends downward far enough to be immediately noticeable—typically filling at least 30–40% of the visible panel height. At the top of this block is a strong, high-contrast error headline (e.g., in red or alert-colored accent) aligned center, followed 8–16px below by a concise explanatory message in smaller text. Beneath that, separated by around 16px of spacing, a clear primary action (e.g., a button labeled “Retry” or “Try again”) is centered, consistent with the high-impact CTA styling used elsewhere (bold color, shadow, full-width or medium-width button). Additional guidance, such as “Check your internet connection,” may appear as a tertiary line below the button. Generous empty space surrounds this column, particularly above and below, ensuring the message stands out against the dark panel without feeling cramped. Pagination can be hidden or visually subdued in this state, keeping focus on resolving the error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the results list content while maintaining the same spatial anchor between sort control and pagination region. It scrolls as part of the normal panel flow if its content exceeds a single screen height.

name: Webinar Detail
title: Webinar Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected webinar and provides an embedded registration form that submits via the webinar registration interface.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific webinar based on the URL parameter `id` by calling `getWebinarDetails`.
Show webinar metadata including title, subtitle (if available), description, agenda items, date, time, time zone, and any time zone note returned by `getWebinarDetails`.

Display webinar scheduling details derived from the `Webinar` model, such as duration (from `duration_minutes` or `start_datetime`/`end_datetime`), time-of-day classification (`time_of_day`), and status (e.g., scheduled, canceled, completed).
Provide a prominent registration call-to-action button (e.g., labeled "Reserve my spot" or "Register now") that scrolls or jumps the user to the embedded registration form.

Render an embedded registration form bound to the current webinar, allowing entry of name, email, selection of preferred time zone, and reminder preference, using options that match the `WebinarRegistration.selected_time_zone` and `WebinarRegistration.reminder_preference` enums.
Submit the registration form via `registerForWebinar` using the current webinar id and form inputs, and handle the returned success state, message, and `WebinarRegistration` object.
Display an in-page confirmation state after successful registration, including a concise success message and any relevant timing or reminder information derived from the registration response.

Handle invalid or missing webinar ids and interface errors by showing functional error states (e.g., webinar not found, webinar canceled, registration closed) and disabling or hiding the registration form when appropriate.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Viewing webinar details:
1. User arrives on `webinar_detail.html?id={Webinar.id}` from another page.
2. On page initialization, the page reads the `id` URL parameter.
3. The `webinar-loader` component calls `getWebinarDetails` with `webinarId = id`.
4. While data is loading, a loading state is shown in the webinar detail area and registration section.
5. When the interface returns, webinar details (title, description, agenda, date and time, time zone note, presenter, duration) are populated into the webinar detail components.
6. If the webinar status is `scheduled`, the registration CTA button and form become enabled. If the webinar is `canceled` or `completed`, the page shows an appropriate status message and disables the registration form.

Workflow 2 – Registering for a webinar from the CTA button:
1. After reviewing details, the user clicks the registration CTA button (e.g., "Reserve my spot").
2. The page scrolls or jumps to the embedded registration form section.
3. The form is pre-associated with the current webinar id and may preselect the time zone using the webinar's `time_zone` value.
4. The user enters name and email and chooses their preferred time zone and reminder preference from the available enum-based options.
5. The user submits the form.
6. The `webinar-registration-form` component validates required fields on the client (non-empty name, valid email format, selected time zone, selected reminder preference).
7. If validation passes, `registerForWebinar` is called with `webinarId = id` and the entered fields.
8. While the request is pending, the submit button shows a loading/disabled state to prevent duplicate submissions.
9. When the interface returns a successful result (`success = true`), the form area switches to a confirmation state using the returned `message` and any relevant details from the `registration` object.
10. If the interface returns `success = false` or the call fails, an error message is shown, and the form remains visible so the user can correct and resubmit.

Workflow 3 – Handling missing or invalid webinar id:
1. User attempts to access `webinar_detail.html` without an `id` parameter or with an id that does not correspond to an existing webinar.
2. On initialization, the `webinar-loader` component detects a missing `id` or receives an error/empty result from `getWebinarDetails`.
3. The page shows a functional error message indicating that the webinar could not be loaded and hides or disables the registration components.
4. No calls to `registerForWebinar` are allowed while webinar details are unavailable.

Workflow 4 – Revisiting the page with a different webinar id:
1. User navigates to another webinar detail from a list or link, which changes the URL to `webinar_detail.html?id={anotherWebinarId}`.
2. On page load (or route change if in a SPA context), the `webinar-loader` component detects the new `id` value.
3. Any previous webinar information and registration states are cleared or reset.
4. `getWebinarDetails` is called again with the new `webinarId`.
5. The page displays the new webinar's details and an empty registration form ready for fresh input.
interactions: On page initialization, the system reads the `id` parameter from the URL query string and triggers `getWebinarDetails(id)`.
If the URL `id` parameter is missing, the page displays a "webinar not found" state and prevents registration interactions.
While webinar details are loading, the detail and form components show a loading state (e.g., disabled form fields and placeholder text).

Once webinar details load successfully, the webinar title, schedule, time zone, presenter, description, agenda list, and time zone note are populated from the `Webinar` object and `agendaItems` array.
Clicking the registration CTA button (e.g., "Reserve my spot" or "Register now") scrolls the page to the embedded registration form section and focuses the first input field.
In the registration form, the user can type into the name and email fields; changes are stored in the component's local form state.

The time zone dropdown in the registration form is populated with options derived from `WebinarRegistration.selected_time_zone` enum values and defaults to either the webinar's `time_zone` or a previously selected value within the current session.
The reminder preference dropdown or radio group is populated with options derived from `WebinarRegistration.reminder_preference` enum values.
Submitting the form triggers client-side validation: verifying that name and email are non-empty, email matches a basic email pattern, and that both a time zone and reminder preference are selected.
If validation fails, validation messages are displayed near the relevant fields, and `registerForWebinar` is not called.
If validation passes, the form component calls `registerForWebinar` with `webinarId = id`, name, email, selectedTimeZone, and reminderPreference.
During the `registerForWebinar` call, the submit button is disabled, and a submitting state is indicated to prevent duplicate clicks.

If `registerForWebinar` returns `success = true`, the form container hides or locks the input fields and displays a concise success message using the returned `message`, optionally including confirmation details derived from the `registration` object.
If `registerForWebinar` returns `success = false`, the error `message` is shown above the form, and the fields remain editable so the user can adjust inputs and resubmit.
If the webinar status is `canceled` or `completed` (derived from `Webinar.status`), the registration CTA button and form are disabled or hidden, and a status notice is shown instead.

Navigation away from the page is handled by the browser (e.g., back button or external links); any such navigation will cause the page to be reinitialized with the new URL and associated `id` parameter if present.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is determined by the URL query parameter `id`, which maps directly to `Webinar.id`. On load, the page reads `id`; if present, it calls `getWebinarDetails` with `webinarId = id`. The results populate the in-memory state for the current webinar (Webinar data, agendaItems, and timeZoneNote). Derived states include: (1) loading state while the interface call is pending; (2) error state if `id` is missing or the interface fails; (3) unavailable state if the returned webinar has a status that disallows registration (e.g., `canceled` or `completed`); and (4) ready state when a scheduled webinar is loaded. Registration state is managed per page load and is not persisted via URL or localStorage: before form submission, the registration section is in an "editable" state; once `registerForWebinar` returns success, the section transitions to a "registered" state showing a confirmation message and disabling further submissions. If the URL `id` value changes (for example, by the user directly editing the URL or navigating from another page to a different webinar detail URL), the page should treat this as a new initialization: clear any prior webinar and registration states, re-call `getWebinarDetails` with the new `id`, and rebuild the page based on the new response.

components.functionality: Reads the `id` parameter from the URL, calls `getWebinarDetails` with that id, and manages loading and error states for the entire page.
event_handlers: onInit: Read `id` from URL and, if present, call `getWebinarDetails(webinarId=id)`.
onLoadSuccess: Store returned `webinar`, `agendaItems`, and `timeZoneNote` in shared page state and signal child components to render.
onLoadError: Set a global error state indicating that the webinar could not be loaded and notify dependent components to show an error message.
onMissingId: If `id` is absent from the URL, immediately set a not-found state and skip calling `getWebinarDetails`.

components.functionality: Determines whether registration is allowed based on the loaded webinar's status and controls visibility and interactivity of registration-related components.
event_handlers: onWebinarLoaded: Inspect `webinar.status` and set flags such as `isRegistrationOpen` and `isWebinarViewable`.
onStatusChange: If `webinar.status` indicates `canceled` or `completed`, trigger display of a status message and disable the registration CTA button and form.
onStatusScheduled: If `webinar.status` indicates a scheduled webinar, enable the registration CTA button and form.

components.functionality: Displays the top-level webinar information including title, optional subtitle, and key schedule details like date, time, time zone, and duration.
event_handlers: onWebinarDataChange: When `Webinar` data is updated, format and display the webinar title, subtitle, start date/time, end date/time or duration, time zone, time-of-day classification, and `timeZoneNote`.

components.functionality: Shows the main webinar description and an agenda list derived from the `agendaItems` returned by `getWebinarDetails`.
event_handlers: onWebinarDataChange: Render the webinar description using `webinar.description` if available.
onAgendaItemsChange: Render a structured list of agenda items using each item's `title` and `description`.

components.functionality: Provides a prominent entry point for registration with a clear label such as "Reserve my spot" or "Register now" and scrolls the user to the registration form.
event_handlers: onRender: Set button visibility and disabled state based on webinar availability and `webinar-status-gate` flags.
onClick: Smooth scroll or jump to the `webinar-registration-form` component and focus the first input field if registration is open.
onDisabledClick: If registration is closed (e.g., webinar canceled or completed), prevent scrolling and optionally show a tooltip or message indicating registration is unavailable.

components.functionality: Embedded form that allows the user to register for the currently viewed webinar by submitting name, email, preferred time zone, and reminder preference via `registerForWebinar`.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize empty form fields (name, email) and default selection values. Preselect the time zone using `webinar.time_zone` if available.
onChangeName: Update local form state with the user's entered name.
onChangeEmail: Update local form state with the user's entered email address.
onChangeTimeZone: Update the selected time zone in local form state using one of the allowed `WebinarRegistration.selected_time_zone` enum values.
onChangeReminderPreference: Update the reminder preference in local form state using one of the allowed `WebinarRegistration.reminder_preference` enum values.
onValidate: Ensure name and email are not empty, email has a valid pattern, and both a time zone and reminder preference are selected; set field-level error states if validation fails.

onSubmit: If validation passes and registration is open, call `registerForWebinar` with `webinarId = webinar.id`, name, email, selectedTimeZone, and reminderPreference. While awaiting response, disable the form and show a submitting state.

onSubmitSuccess: When `registerForWebinar` returns with `success = true`, store the returned `registration` object and trigger the `registration-confirmation` component to display a success message. Optionally lock the form inputs or replace them with confirmation content.
onSubmitError: When `registerForWebinar` returns with `success = false` or a network error occurs, display the error `message`, re-enable the form, and allow the user to adjust inputs and resubmit.
onWebinarStatusChange: If the webinar becomes unavailable (e.g., status not scheduled), disable the form and prevent new submissions.

components.functionality: Displays a concise confirmation or error message after a registration attempt, using the response from `registerForWebinar`.
event_handlers: onRegistrationSuccess: When a `WebinarRegistration` object is returned with `success = true`, display a success banner that uses the returned `message` and may reference details like `registration.selected_time_zone` and `registration.reminder_preference`.
onRegistrationError: Display an error banner with the error `message` returned by `registerForWebinar` without hiding the form.
onReset: If the page is reloaded or the URL `id` changes, reset the confirmation state so that it reflects only the current webinar’s registration state.

components.functionality: Provides feedback when the webinar cannot be loaded due to a missing or invalid `id` or a failure in `getWebinarDetails`.
event_handlers: onMissingId: Show a message indicating that no webinar was specified; hide the registration-related components.
onLoadError: Show a message indicating that the webinar could not be loaded and suggest navigating back or selecting another webinar; hide the registration form and CTA button.
onRetryRequest: If a retry control is present, re-trigger `getWebinarDetails` using the current URL `id`.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: All webinar detail content sits inside a single, tall, centered hero panel that floats above a full-bleed lifestyle background image. Inside that panel, components are stacked in a strict top-to-bottom order: header at the top, then description/agenda, then registration instructions and form (including the CTA button), then any confirmation or support messaging. Error and load states appear in-line or as overlays on this same central column rather than in separate regions of the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All webinar detail content sits inside a single, tall, centered hero panel that floats above a full-bleed lifestyle background image. Inside that panel, components are stacked in a strict top-to-bottom order: header at the top, then description/agenda, then registration instructions and form (including the CTA button), then any confirmation or support messaging. Error and load states appear in-line or as overlays on this same central column rather than in separate regions of the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The centered panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width with a max-width around 480–640px, and it is vertically padded so it feels like a modal hovering in the middle of the screen. Within the panel, approximately the top 60–65% of vertical space (as content allows) is reserved for the webinar title and descriptive copy (header + description/agenda), while the bottom 35–40% is reserved for conversion elements (registration instruction, form, CTA button, and confirmation messaging). Loaders and critical errors temporarily take over most of the panel area to keep focus.
layout_strategies.content_density: Inside the panel, text and controls follow a moderately dense marketing layout: small vertical gaps (8–16px) between related lines (title, subtitle, meta info) and larger gaps (24–32px) between major blocks (header → description/agenda → form → confirmation). Inputs and the CTA button are stacked with tight but comfortable spacing, echoing a high-urgency landing page while avoiding visual clutter. Outside the panel, the beach background is left visually open to provide breathing room and contrast.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the top-center of the panel with a high-contrast headline in the webinar-detail-header, moves down into key supporting details in the description/agenda, then naturally funnels into the registration area where an instruction line leads the eye directly to the stacked form fields and bold registration CTA button. After submission, the user’s attention is pulled to a prominent confirmation banner in the same lower region. Loaders and error banners interrupt this flow intentionally by appearing in the central or top portion of the panel, ensuring state changes are immediately noticed.

layout.overall_description: The page is dominated by a full-bleed beach photograph that fills the entire viewport, saturated and slightly darkened to serve as a cinematic backdrop. At the center of the screen sits a tall, dark, semi-transparent panel with soft edges and a subtle drop shadow, occupying about half the viewport width and framed by the background image on both sides. All content lives inside this single panel in a vertical stack. At the top, a large, bold webinar title and key details draw immediate attention, followed by a concise description and agenda section that builds interest without overwhelming the user. Below, the layout transitions into a focused registration area: a short, directive line leads into the registration form fields, which are stacked full-width, and culminate in a wide, high-contrast CTA button anchored in the lower third of the panel. Status banners for confirmation or errors appear in-line within the panel, replacing or pushing aside the form while maintaining the same central alignment. A loading state gently overlays the panel with a centered indicator when data is being fetched. The overall effect is a high-contrast, single-column funnel that feels like a promotional hero module specifically designed to drive webinar signups.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the webinar-loader appears as a semi-opaque dark overlay that covers the entire central hero panel from edge to edge, matching the panel’s width and height and slightly dimming any underlying content. At the exact center of this overlay (both vertically and horizontally), a spinner or loading indicator is placed, approximately 48–64px in diameter, with a brief status line directly beneath it. The overlay aligns with the panel’s boundaries rather than the full viewport, so the beach background remains visible around the panel. All other panel content is visually subdued beneath this overlay but not reflowed, reinforcing that loading is a temporary state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a modal-style overlay fixed to the hero panel’s bounds, centered within the viewport. It sits above all other components in z-order while active and prevents interaction with underlying content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The webinar-status-gate is an invisible logical wrapper that conceptually covers the full interior of the central hero panel, from the top padding margin under the panel’s top edge down to the bottom padding above the panel’s lower edge. Within this gated space, either the normal content stack (header, description, form) or fallback states (error, empty, or loading) are rendered, but never both simultaneously. Visually it does not introduce extra space or borders; instead, it simply determines which set of components occupies the single vertical column inside the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that manages which components are rendered inside the central panel. It does not affect layout directly beyond switching between content sets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The webinar-detail-header sits at the top of the central hero panel’s content area, starting just below a generous top padding band of approximately 24–32px. It spans the full internal width of the panel (minus side padding) and forms the first, most prominent block in the vertical stack. The main webinar title appears in large, bold type centered horizontally within this block, with any key metadata (date, time, host) either directly below it or on the next line, separated by a compact 8–12px gap. The header block itself occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the panel’s content height before transitioning downward into the description, establishing a strong, high-contrast anchor at the top of the funnel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the top of the content stack inside the panel, scrolling off-screen only when the entire page scrolls and the panel’s content exceeds the viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The webinar-description-and-agenda section is placed directly below the webinar-detail-header, separated from it by a medium vertical gap of about 24–32px to clearly distinguish it as a new content block. It also spans the full internal width of the panel, following the same side padding. The first lines of this section are short, persuasive descriptions that align with the mockup’s supportive copy, typically rendered in a slightly smaller but still bright, high-contrast type. Beneath that, the agenda details are presented as compact paragraphs or a concise bullet list, with 8–12px spacing between items to maintain the moderately dense promotional feel. Overall, this section occupies roughly the next 30–35% of the panel’s content area, ending with a noticeable 24–32px gap before the registration area begins, creating a clear break between “information” above and “action” below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block in the middle of the vertical stack. It grows vertically based on content but always pushes the registration area down while maintaining consistent top and section spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-cta-button is positioned inside the lower conversion area of the panel as the culminating element of the webinar-registration-form. It sits directly beneath the last form field (typically email or contact details), with a tight but clear vertical gap of about 12–16px. The button is full-width relative to the panel’s internal content column, aligning flush with the left and right edges of the input fields. Visually, it is large and high-contrast—using a saturated accent color (e.g., bright yellow) with a subtle drop shadow—to mirror the strong CTA button from the mockup. It occupies roughly the final 15–20% of the lower panel region above the bottom padding, giving it a grounded, anchor-like position in the user’s visual path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control anchored within the form block. It scrolls with the rest of the panel content; there is no sticky behavior, ensuring it remains contextually tied to the fields above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The webinar-registration-form begins just below the webinar-description-and-agenda section, separated by a larger gap of approximately 24–32px to mark the transition from reading to action. It occupies the lower third to lower two-fifths of the panel, depending on field count. At the top of the form block, a short instructional line (e.g., “Reserve your seat below”) is centered or left-aligned within the same content column and placed with about 8–12px of space above the first input. Below this, all form fields are stacked vertically and span the full width of the content column, each with 12–16px internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, matching the input/button sizing from the mockup. Vertical spacing between fields is compact (8–12px) to maintain a focused, conversion-oriented density. The registration-cta-button sits immediately beneath the final field, visually integrated as part of the form. A modest 24px padding remains between the button and the bottom edge of the panel, preserving breathing room while keeping the CTA visually anchored at the bottom of the conversion zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the lower portion of the panel. Its vertical size expands with additional fields, pushing the bottom of the panel’s content downward; if the content exceeds viewport height, the panel’s contents scroll normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-confirmation status banner is displayed in the same lower region of the panel where the registration form normally appears, effectively replacing the form block when registration succeeds. It is centered within the content column and spans the full internal width, with a height sufficient to hold 1–3 lines of text. The banner is styled with a high-contrast success color (e.g., teal or cyan) and a subtle glow or shadow, echoing the mockup’s payoff statement area. It is separated from the description/agenda section above by approximately 24–32px of vertical space, mirroring the form’s previous offset, and includes internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides. The text is large enough to command attention and is typically centered horizontally, becoming the new focal element in the lower third of the panel while leaving the header and description intact above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally in place of the form, occupying the same vertical slot in the stack. It does not overlay other content; instead, it pushes or replaces the form so that the layout remains a clean, linear column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The webinar-load-error banner appears within the central hero panel, just below the top padding and, if applicable, directly beneath any minimal title shell, effectively occupying the upper portion of the content area. It spans the full internal width and is rendered with a bold error color (e.g., bright red) and strong contrast to immediately stand out against the dark panel background. The banner has 16–24px of internal padding and may support 1–2 lines of explanatory text. When shown, it either pushes the rest of the content down or, in cases of fatal load failure, is the only visible block inside the panel aside from the background, leaving generous empty space below it. A small 16–24px gap separates it from any content that appears beneath, preserving hierarchy while making the error impossible to miss.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in-line banner within the panel’s vertical stack. It occupies the top portion of the content column when active and reflows other components around it; it does not float or overlay beyond the panel bounds.

name: Earnings Calculator
title: Earnings Calculator
description: Page for configuring, estimating, and saving a personalized work-from-home earnings plan, with optional continuation to the application flow.

core_features: Fetch earnings calculator default values and constraints using getEarningsCalculatorDefaults and initialize the input form.
Allow users to input or adjust hours per week, average sale value, monthly leads, conversion rate, and an optional monthly income goal.
Calculate an estimated monthly income using calculateEarningsEstimate based on the current inputs.
Display the estimated monthly income together with goal comparison (goalMonthlyIncomeTarget and meetsTarget) returned from calculateEarningsEstimate.
Provide a "Save my estimate" action that opens a form to capture name and email, then saves the current configuration via saveEarningsPlan.
After an estimate is available (and optionally after saving), provide a contextual CTA to navigate to application.html with relevant calculator state in the URL parameters.
Persist and restore calculator state via URL parameters instead of localStorage, allowing deep-linking and back/forward navigation.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: First-time visitor calculates an estimate
1. User navigates to earnings_calculator.html (e.g., from header, footer, or homepage link) without specific query parameters.
2. On page load, the calculator-initializer component calls getEarningsCalculatorDefaults.
3. The page merges any existing URL parameters (if present) with the returned defaults, then populates hours per week, average sale value, monthly leads, conversion rate, and optional goal input fields.
4. The page automatically triggers calculateEarningsEstimate with the initial values or waits for the user to click a "Calculate" button (depending on configuration), passing: hoursPerWeek, averageSaleValue, monthlyLeads, conversionRatePercent, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget.
5. When the response returns, the earnings-result-panel displays estimatedMonthlyIncome, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget, and meetsTarget.
6. The url-state-manager updates the current URL query parameters to reflect the latest inputs and goal value, enabling the user to bookmark or share the configured calculator URL.

Workflow 2: Visitor adjusts inputs and refines their plan
1. User modifies one or more calculator fields (hours per week, average sale value, monthly leads, conversion rate, or monthly goal).
2. On each relevant change or on an explicit "Recalculate" action, the earnings-calculator-form triggers calculateEarningsEstimate with the updated values.
3. The earnings-result-panel updates to show the new estimatedMonthlyIncome and whether meetsTarget is true or false.
4. The url-state-manager synchronizes the URL query parameters with the latest input values (e.g., hoursPerWeek, averageSaleValue, monthlyLeads, conversionRatePercent, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget).
5. The user can use the browser back/forward buttons; on navigation, the calculator-initializer reads the updated URL parameters and restores the corresponding calculator state, then triggers calculateEarningsEstimate again to refresh the displayed estimate.

Workflow 3: Visitor saves their earnings plan
1. After viewing a calculated estimate, user clicks the "Save my estimate" button.
2. The save-plan-modal-form opens, pre-populated (internally) with the current calculator inputs and goal; the user is prompted to enter their name and email.
3. User submits the save-plan form; front-end validation ensures name, email, and all required calculator inputs are present.
4. The save-plan-modal-form calls saveEarningsPlan with: name, email, hoursPerWeek, averageSaleValue, monthlyLeads, conversionRatePercent, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget (values taken from the current calculator state and the form inputs).
5. When saveEarningsPlan returns, the component reads the success flag, message, and the returned earningsPlan object (including its id, estimated_monthly_income, meets_target, and timestamps).
6. On success, the page displays a non-promotional confirmation state (e.g., text message indicating the plan is saved) and updates the URL parameters to include a reference to the saved plan (e.g., earningsPlanId derived from earningsPlan.id) alongside the current calculator parameters.
7. If the call fails (success = false), the component displays the message returned by the interface and allows the user to correct inputs or retry.

Workflow 4: Visitor continues to application with current plan
1. After an estimate has been calculated (and optionally saved), the user sees an "Apply now with this earnings plan" CTA.
2. When the user clicks the CTA, the apply-with-plan-cta component constructs a URL pointing to application.html.
3. The component appends relevant query parameters to application.html (e.g., hoursPerWeek, averageSaleValue, monthlyLeads, conversionRatePercent, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget, estimatedMonthlyIncome, meetsTarget, and optionally earningsPlanId if a plan has been saved).
4. The user is navigated to application.html with these parameters, allowing the application page to read and pre-contextualize the user’s intent and plan (implementation on that page is out of scope here).

Workflow 5: Returning visitor opens a shared or saved link
1. User opens earnings_calculator.html with query parameters already present (for example, from a shared link or bookmark).
2. On page load, the calculator-initializer calls getEarningsCalculatorDefaults, then merges URL parameter values on top of the returned defaults, honoring constraints (e.g., ensuring parameters do not exceed min/max values returned from the API).
3. The earnings-calculator-form fields are populated using the merged values.
4. The page triggers calculateEarningsEstimate with the effective inputs to show an up-to-date estimatedMonthlyIncome and goal status.
5. If an earningsPlanId is present in the URL, the page does not directly fetch from other interfaces (not assigned); instead, it uses the ID only for display or navigation context (e.g., marking that the estimate has been saved previously) and continues to rely on the current URL-bound values for calculation.
interactions: On page load: calculator-initializer triggers getEarningsCalculatorDefaults, then populates the calculator form and constraints; after initialization, it may trigger calculateEarningsEstimate with effective values.

On URL change (initial load or browser navigation): url-state-manager parses query parameters (for calculator inputs, goal, and optional earningsPlanId), merges them with the default values, updates the calculator form state, and triggers calculateEarningsEstimate for the current configuration.

On calculator input change: earnings-calculator-form updates its internal state for the changed field and either (a) immediately calls calculateEarningsEstimate, or (b) waits until the user clicks a dedicated "Calculate" or "Update estimate" button, then calls calculateEarningsEstimate with all current values.

On estimate calculation response: earnings-result-panel displays estimatedMonthlyIncome, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget, and meetsTarget; if meetsTarget is false, it may display a functional hint to adjust inputs (without prescribing static values).

On any successful estimate update: url-state-manager updates the browser URL query parameters (using pushState/replaceState) with the current calculator input values and goalMonthlyIncomeTarget, ensuring they are encoded as strings suitable for use in URLs.

On clicking "Save my estimate": save-plan-trigger opens the save-plan-modal-form and passes the current calculator state values (hoursPerWeek, averageSaleValue, monthlyLeads, conversionRatePercent, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget) into that form’s context.

On submitting the save-plan-modal-form: the component validates name and email, then calls saveEarningsPlan with name, email, and the current calculator configuration; the form is temporarily disabled during the request to prevent duplicate submissions.

On saveEarningsPlan success: the page shows a confirmation state using the returned message, optionally closes the modal (depending on configuration), and updates the URL with earningsPlanId and possibly recalculates using calculateEarningsEstimate if the backend adjusted inputs or goal.
On saveEarningsPlan failure: the form re-enables inputs and displays the error message returned; the user can correct the name/email or adjust calculator values and resubmit.

On clicking "Apply now with this earnings plan": apply-with-plan-cta reads the current calculator state and the latest estimate results, builds a navigation URL to application.html with these values in query parameters, and triggers a location change.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for shareable state. Supported parameters (names are indicative and may be adapted consistently across components) include: hoursPerWeek, averageSaleValue, monthlyLeads, conversionRatePercent, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget, estimatedMonthlyIncome, meetsTarget, and earningsPlanId. On initial page load, the calculator-initializer calls getEarningsCalculatorDefaults to obtain default values and constraints (minHoursPerWeek, maxHoursPerWeek, minConversionRatePercent, maxConversionRatePercent). It then parses the current URL parameters and merges them onto the defaults. Any parameter that falls outside the constraints returned by getEarningsCalculatorDefaults is clamped or ignored according to the constraints, avoiding reliance on any hardcoded numeric limits. These effective values are used to populate the calculator form, and calculateEarningsEstimate is then called to show the current estimate.

Whenever the user changes inputs and triggers a recalculation, the url-state-manager updates the query parameters to reflect the new inputs and goal. This enables back/forward navigation to restore previous states: when the URL changes due to history navigation, the page re-parses the parameters, re-applies constraints, repopulates the form, and re-calls calculateEarningsEstimate. If a plan is successfully saved via saveEarningsPlan, the returned earningsPlan.id is written into the earningsPlanId query parameter so that a shared link can indicate that the configuration corresponds to a saved plan. No data is persisted in localStorage or other client storage; all restorable state is encoded in the URL, and all numeric boundaries and default values come exclusively from getEarningsCalculatorDefaults.

components.functionality: On page load (and on explicit refresh events), this component initializes the calculator by calling getEarningsCalculatorDefaults, reading URL parameters, merging them with the defaults under the provided constraints, and then populating the earnings-calculator-form with the resulting values. After initialization, it optionally triggers an initial call to calculateEarningsEstimate so the user sees an estimate without any extra action.

Provides input fields for hours per week, average sale value, monthly leads, conversion rate, and an optional goalMonthlyIncomeTarget. It validates user input according to constraints (provided by getEarningsCalculatorDefaults via calculator-initializer) and triggers calculateEarningsEstimate when the user requests an updated estimate. It also exposes its current values to other components (e.g., save-plan-modal-form, apply-with-plan-cta).

Displays the latest estimation details returned from calculateEarningsEstimate, including estimatedMonthlyIncome, the goalMonthlyIncomeTarget echoed back by the interface, and meetsTarget. It updates whenever a new estimate is successfully fetched. It can also expose a simple derived state (e.g., whether an estimate is currently available) that other components use to enable or disable actions such as the save-plan-trigger and apply-with-plan-cta.

Synchronizes calculator-related state with the browser URL query parameters. After each successful user-initiated update (input changes and recalculation, or a successful saveEarningsPlan call), it updates the URL with the latest calculator values, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget, and optionally earningsPlanId. It also listens for browser history events or direct URL changes and notifies the calculator-initializer/earnings-calculator-form to reload state based on the new parameters.

Provides the entry point for saving the current earnings plan. It remains disabled until at least one successful estimate is available. When activated, it opens the save-plan-modal-form and passes the current calculator values and goal into that modal’s context.

Collects the user’s name and email while reusing the current calculator configuration (hoursPerWeek, averageSaleValue, monthlyLeads, conversionRatePercent, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget) supplied by earnings-calculator-form. On submit, it calls saveEarningsPlan with these values. On success, it receives the resulting earningsPlan object and informs url-state-manager to update the earningsPlanId query parameter, and informs earnings-result-panel if the backend’s persisted values differ from the current view.

When an estimate is available (and optionally when a plan has been saved), this component constructs a navigation URL to application.html, injecting the current calculator inputs, goalMonthlyIncomeTarget, estimatedMonthlyIncome, meetsTarget, and earningsPlanId (if present) as query parameters. It then navigates the user to that URL when clicked.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: All interactive content is organized in a single, vertically stacked column inside a central, dark translucent panel that sits over a full-bleed lifestyle background image. The stack order from top to bottom is: calculator initializer (headline + framing copy), earnings calculator form (inputs), earnings result panel (calculated output), save plan trigger (primary CTA), apply-with-plan CTA (secondary navigation). The URL state manager is non-visual. A separate save-plan modal form appears as a secondary, smaller overlay card centered over this main panel when invoked.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All interactive content is organized in a single, vertically stacked column inside a central, dark translucent panel that sits over a full-bleed lifestyle background image. The stack order from top to bottom is: calculator initializer (headline + framing copy), earnings calculator form (inputs), earnings result panel (calculated output), save plan trigger (primary CTA), apply-with-plan CTA (secondary navigation). The URL state manager is non-visual. A separate save-plan modal form appears as a secondary, smaller overlay card centered over this main panel when invoked.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper ~60–65% of the main panel is dedicated to framing and configuring the calculation (initializer + main form), and the lower ~35–40% is dedicated to outcomes and action (earnings result, save plan CTA, apply with plan CTA). Horizontally, the main panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width around 560–640px) and is centered, with the beach background visible on both sides. The modal overlay is slightly narrower (~70–80% of the main panel width) to feel like a focused secondary step.
layout_strategies.content_density: Within the panel, content is moderately dense and promotional: text and interactive elements are stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing between related elements and 24–32px between major sections (initializer → form → results → CTAs). There is consistent internal padding of ~24–32px on all sides of the panels, ensuring the dense content still feels contained and intentional against the busy photographic background.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey is a strong top-to-bottom funnel. The eye first hits the bold, gradient-style headline and framing copy in the calculator initializer at the top of the panel, then moves down into the detailed earnings-calculator-form inputs. Once inputs are in focus, attention naturally drops to the earnings-result-panel, which uses contrast and slightly larger type to highlight the estimated earnings. From there, the bright, full-width save-plan-trigger button pulls attention as the primary conversion action, and finally the apply-with-plan-cta link at the very bottom offers the continuation path. When opened, the save-plan-modal-form temporarily takes over as the highest visual priority, centered with a darkened backdrop to isolate the save action.

layout.overall_description: The page is a full-height hero section with a saturated, high-contrast beach photograph covering the entire viewport. Centered on the screen is a tall, dark, translucent panel that feels like a promotional card or modal overlay, occupying about half the viewport width on larger screens and most of the width on mobile. Inside this panel, content flows in a single vertical column following a direct-response rhythm: a bold earnings-calculator initializer headline and short supporting copy at the top, then the main earnings calculator inputs, then a visually distinct earnings estimate, and finally strong CTAs at the bottom. Generous but deliberate spacing separates these sections, using 24–32px gaps for major breaks and 8–16px for related items. The primary save-plan button sits just below the result, spanning nearly the full panel width with a bright, contrasting color and strong drop shadow, echoing the dramatic marketing style of the mockup. Below that, a lighter-weight "apply with plan" link provides a secondary path. When users choose to save, a smaller, more focused modal form appears centered over everything with the background dimmed, creating a clear, layered hierarchy of attention while still echoing the same dark, glossy panel aesthetic. Overall, the layout feels like a high-impact, conversion-oriented funnel staged within a single, visually rich hero experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calculator initializer sits at the very top of the central dark panel, acting as the entry point. It is aligned to the horizontal center of the panel, with top padding of about 24–32px from the panel’s top edge. The main headline spans nearly the full internal width (around 90% of the panel’s width), centered or slightly left-aligned, using large, bold, high-contrast typography. Beneath the headline, 8–16px of vertical space separates it from 2–3 lines of supporting copy that explain what the earnings calculator does and how it simplifies planning. Overall, the initializer content occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the panel’s height, establishing context but leaving ample space below for the calculator itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupies the top section of the main central panel and scrolls with the page when content exceeds viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The earnings-calculator-form is placed directly below the initializer, separated by a clear section gap of roughly 24–32px to signal the transition from explanation to interaction. It spans the full available width inside the panel minus internal padding (about 24–32px on each side), creating a stack of full-width input fields.
