Each input row has 12–16px vertical padding inside and 12–16px of space between rows, matching the moderately dense, promotional feel. Any labels or helper text are aligned left within the same column, with 4–8px spacing above their respective inputs. The form section as a whole occupies approximately the next 30–40% of the panel’s height, keeping it visually central and clearly the main interactive area of the page. The bottom of this form is separated from the earnings-result-panel by a slightly larger gap (around 24px) to differentiate configuration from outcome.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked immediately below the initializer within the central panel; adapts to content height and pushes subsequent sections downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The earnings-result-panel is positioned directly beneath the calculator form, with about 24px of vertical space separating it from the last form field. It is visually grouped as a distinct block that spans the same internal width as the form but may have a subtly different background treatment (slightly lighter or with a subtle glow) to draw attention. Inside this block, the estimated earnings figure is centered and uses larger, bright text (white or bold accent color) with 8–16px of margin above and below, making it the focal point of this section. Supporting explanatory text (e.g., timeframe, assumptions) appears below the main number with 8–12px spacing, in a slightly smaller type size. The result panel takes up around 15–20% of the panel’s height, leaving enough room below for strong CTAs while still feeling prominent. A 16–24px gap below this panel separates it from the save-plan-trigger button, so the CTA clearly follows the outcome.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned directly below the form within the central panel; height adjusts to the amount of result and explanation content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is a non-visual, purely functional component. It does not render any visible UI and therefore occupies no explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it is associated with the central panel, updating URL parameters in response to calculator inputs and saved plans, but it does not affect the spatial arrangement of any visible elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; operates in the background without altering the physical layout or flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-plan-trigger button is placed immediately below the earnings-result-panel, forming the primary conversion action. It is centered within the panel and spans roughly 90–100% of the internal width, with 16–18px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, echoing the bold, full-width CTA button from the mockup. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space above it (to the result panel) and 16–24px below it (before the apply-with-plan-cta). The button uses a bright, saturated color (e.g., yellow) with a strong drop shadow, making it one of the most visually prominent elements in the bottom third of the panel. Together with the earnings result above, this section occupies about 20–25% of the panel height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the central panel’s content column; remains in the same relative position but may move vertically with the page on smaller screens as content scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-plan-modal-form appears only after the save-plan-trigger button is activated. It is rendered as a secondary dialog layered above the main central panel, centered both horizontally and vertically in the viewport. The modal has a slightly smaller footprint than the main panel, occupying about 70–80% of the main panel’s width and roughly 35–45% of the viewport height, with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. Its background is a slightly lighter or more opaque dark surface to distinguish it as a separate card, while the rest of the page (including the main panel and background photo) is dimmed with a translucent black overlay. Inside the modal, content follows a tight vertical stack: a concise headline at the top, 8–16px below that a brief explanatory line, followed by vertically stacked input fields (e.g., name, email) with 12–16px spacing, and finally a full-width confirmation button at the bottom with 16–18px vertical padding. The total vertical spacing inside the modal mirrors the main panel but is slightly tighter to emphasize focus and urgency.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport with a full-screen, semi-opaque backdrop. It floats above all other content and locks the underlying page scroll while open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The apply-with-plan-cta is positioned at the very bottom of the main central panel, directly beneath the save-plan-trigger button. It is separated from the button by about 16–24px of vertical space to signal that it is a secondary, follow-up action rather than the main conversion. The CTA is horizontally centered and may appear as an underlined text link or a low-emphasis button with no heavy fill, spanning only as wide as its text content or up to about 50–60% of the panel width. This keeps it visually lighter and less dominant than the save-plan-trigger. A bottom padding of about 24–32px from the panel’s bottom edge ensures breathing room, echoing the top padding above the initializer and balancing the panel vertically.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored at the bottom of the main panel’s content stack; scrolls with the rest of the panel when the page content exceeds viewport height.

name: Programs
title: Programs
description: Programs page for browsing, filtering, and sorting work-from-home business programs by time commitment, live sessions, and average monthly earnings, with navigation to individual program detail pages.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options for programs using getProgramFilterOptions and bind them to the filter controls (time commitment, live sessions, and sorting).

Allow users to filter programs by weekly time commitment using values based on timeCommitmentOptions from getProgramFilterOptions, including low-hour options such as those with a small maxHoursPerWeek.
Allow users to filter programs by whether they include live group sessions using a boolean control mapped to includesLiveGroupSessions.
Allow users to filter programs by a minimum number of live sessions per month using options from liveSessionsPerMonthOptions returned by getProgramFilterOptions.
Allow users to sort program results using sortOptions returned by getProgramFilterOptions, including sorting by average monthly earnings from high to low.
Retrieve and display a list of active programs that match the current filters and sort order using searchPrograms.
Sync all filter and sort selections with URL query parameters so that the state is shareable and preserved on refresh and back/forward navigation.
Enable navigation from each program card in the results list to its program detail page (program_detail.html) using the program id as a URL parameter.
user_workflows: Browse all programs with default sort: User opens programs.html (from header, footer, or direct link) > Page reads URL parameters (if any) > Page calls getProgramFilterOptions to obtain available filter and sort options > Page determines default sortBy (e.g., first option from sortOptions) when none is present in URL > Page calls searchPrograms with null or default filter parameters and the resolved sortBy > User sees a list of programs and can scroll through them.

Filter by time commitment and live sessions: User opens programs.html or is already on the page > User selects a time commitment option derived from timeCommitmentOptions (for example, an option whose maxHours is suitable for low weekly hours) > Filter component updates the maxHoursPerWeek query parameter in the URL and notifies the page controller > Page controller calls searchPrograms with updated maxHoursPerWeek and existing filter/sort parameters > User optionally toggles the 'includes live group sessions' control, which updates includesLiveGroupSessions in the URL and triggers searchPrograms again > User optionally selects a minimum live sessions per month option from liveSessionsPerMonthOptions, updating minLiveSessionsPerMonth in the URL and triggering another searchPrograms call > Results list updates after each change.

Sort programs by average monthly earnings: User opens programs.html (with or without filters) > User interacts with the sort control bound to sortOptions and selects the option whose value corresponds to average_monthly_earnings_high_to_low > Sort control updates the sortBy query parameter in the URL > Page controller calls searchPrograms with the unchanged filters and updated sortBy > Results list re-renders in the new order.

Share or revisit a filtered program list: User adjusts filters and sort order on programs.html until satisfied > URL now encodes current state with maxHoursPerWeek, includesLiveGroupSessions, minLiveSessionsPerMonth, and sortBy parameters > User copies URL and shares it, or bookmarks it > Another visitor (or the same user later) opens that URL > On load, page controller reads the query parameters, pre-selects matching filter and sort options, then calls searchPrograms with those values > The recipient sees the same filtered and sorted list without additional interaction.

Navigate to a specific program detail: User scans the results list produced by searchPrograms > User clicks a program card > Card click handler navigates to program_detail.html with the selected Program.id passed as the id query parameter (e.g., program_detail.html?id={Program.id}) > Program detail page can then use this id to load and display program-specific information.
interactions: On page load, the page controller reads URLSearchParams (maxHoursPerWeek, includesLiveGroupSessions, minLiveSessionsPerMonth, sortBy), calls getProgramFilterOptions, resolves any missing defaults (such as sortBy from sortOptions), then calls searchPrograms with the effective parameters.

When the user changes the time commitment selection, the filter form converts the selected timeCommitmentOptions item into a maxHoursPerWeek value, updates the maxHoursPerWeek URL parameter, and notifies the page controller to call searchPrograms with the new maxHoursPerWeek.

When the user toggles the 'includes live group sessions' control, the filter form updates the includesLiveGroupSessions URL parameter to "true" or "false" (or removes it for an "any" state), and the page controller calls searchPrograms with the new boolean value.

When the user selects a minimum live sessions per month option, the filter form updates the minLiveSessionsPerMonth URL parameter based on the chosen liveSessionsPerMonthOptions item (mapping its minSessions to the parameter) and triggers a call to searchPrograms.

When the user clears all filters, the filter form removes maxHoursPerWeek, includesLiveGroupSessions, and minLiveSessionsPerMonth from the URL, keeps or resets sortBy as defined by the page logic, and the page controller calls searchPrograms with unfiltered parameters.

When the user changes the sort selection, the sort control updates the sortBy query parameter with the selected sortOptions.value and triggers searchPrograms with the same filter values but the new sortBy.
When the user clicks a program card, the results list component reads the bound Program.id and navigates the browser to program_detail.html?id={Program.id}, without altering other query parameters.

When the user uses the browser back or forward buttons, the page controller listens for URL changes (e.g., popstate), re-reads the updated query parameters, syncs the filter and sort component selections, and re-calls searchPrograms so the visible list always reflects the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The URL query parameters are the single source of truth for the page state. On initial load, the page reads the parameters maxHoursPerWeek, includesLiveGroupSessions, minLiveSessionsPerMonth, and sortBy from programs.html. If sortBy is absent or does not match any item in sortOptions returned by getProgramFilterOptions, the page selects a default sortBy based on the available sortOptions (for example, the first option) and writes it to the URL. If maxHoursPerWeek, includesLiveGroupSessions, or minLiveSessionsPerMonth are missing, the page treats them as unfiltered (null) and does not include them in the searchPrograms call. After resolving parameters, the page calls searchPrograms(maxHoursPerWeek, includesLiveGroupSessions, minLiveSessionsPerMonth, sortBy) and renders the returned Program array. Each user interaction that changes filters or sort order updates the corresponding URL parameter(s) using the History API (e.g., pushState or replaceState) and triggers a new searchPrograms call with values derived directly from the URL. The filter and sort components derive their currently selected options by matching URL values to the result of getProgramFilterOptions (e.g., matching maxHoursPerWeek to a timeCommitmentOptions.maxHours property, and matching sortBy to a sortOptions.value). When the browser back or forward buttons change the URL, a listener on URL changes re-runs this resolution logic and re-calls searchPrograms, ensuring that the visible list and UI controls always reflect the URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL state, and communication between filter, sort, and results components for the Programs page. On initialization, it calls getProgramFilterOptions with no parameters to obtain available filter and sort options. It then reads current query parameters (maxHoursPerWeek, includesLiveGroupSessions, minLiveSessionsPerMonth, sortBy) and resolves a valid sortBy using the sortOptions from getProgramFilterOptions. It calls searchPrograms with the resolved parameters and stores the returned Program array in its internal state. Whenever filters or sort order change (as notified by child components), it updates the URL, calls searchPrograms again, and passes updated results and selected values down to the children. It also listens to browser history events to re-sync state when the URL changes externally.

Provides controls for filtering the program list by time commitment, whether programs include live group sessions, and minimum live sessions per month. It receives timeCommitmentOptions and liveSessionsPerMonthOptions from the page controller (which originated from getProgramFilterOptions). It maps user selections to URL parameter values: selected time commitment option sets maxHoursPerWeek using its hours range, the 'includes live group sessions' control sets includesLiveGroupSessions to true or false (or clears it for 'any'), and the minimum live sessions control sets minLiveSessionsPerMonth based on the selected option's minSessions. On initialization, it reads the current URL parameters via the page controller to pre-select the matching options. On changes, it notifies the page controller so that the URL can be updated and searchPrograms can be re-run.

Displays a sort selection control populated from sortOptions returned by getProgramFilterOptions. It includes options whose values correspond to sortBy values supported by searchPrograms, such as average_monthly_earnings_high_to_low. On initialization, it uses URLSearchParams and the sortOptions list to choose the currently active sort option. When the user selects a new sort option, it sends the chosen sortOptions.value to the page controller, which updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and triggers a new searchPrograms call.

Renders the current set of programs returned by searchPrograms and manages empty or loading states. It receives an array of Program entities and renders a list of program-card components, one per Program. If the Program array is empty, it invokes the programs-empty-state component behavior (for example, showing an option to clear filters). It exposes an event when a program card is clicked so navigation can be handled centrally, though navigation can also be triggered directly in the card component.

Represents a single program in the list. It receives a Program object and displays its core attributes such as name, tagline, time commitment range (time_commitment_hours_per_week_min and time_commitment_hours_per_week_max), includes_live_group_sessions, live_sessions_per_month, and average_monthly_earnings. The entire card (or a specific action on it) is clickable. When clicked, it uses the Program.id to navigate the browser to program_detail.html?id={Program.id}, enabling the user to view detailed information about that program.

Handles the behavior when searchPrograms returns no Program results for the current filters. It is triggered by the programs-results-list component when the bound Program array is empty. It can present a control to reset or relax filters (for example, clear maxHoursPerWeek, includesLiveGroupSessions, and minLiveSessionsPerMonth from the URL) by delegating to the filter form and page controller. After filters are reset, the page controller updates URLSearchParams and calls searchPrograms again to repopulate the list.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the hero-style, single-column, central panel from the mockup. All components (page controller, filters, sort, results, and empty state) are stacked in a linear top-to-bottom order inside one tall, dark, semi-translucent panel that floats over a full-bleed lifestyle background image. There are no true sidebars or multi-column splits; the 'filter-sidebar' behaves like a stacked filter block within the column. The panel itself sits centered in the viewport and reads like a focused conversion funnel for browsing and selecting programs.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the hero-style, single-column, central panel from the mockup. All components (page controller, filters, sort, results, and empty state) are stacked in a linear top-to-bottom order inside one tall, dark, semi-translucent panel that floats over a full-bleed lifestyle background image. There are no true sidebars or multi-column splits; the 'filter-sidebar' behaves like a stacked filter block within the column. The panel itself sits centered in the viewport and reads like a focused conversion funnel for browsing and selecting programs.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (max-width around 560–640px) and is vertically centered with generous top and bottom breathing room from the beach-style background. Within the panel, the top 30–35% is dedicated to page-level messaging and controls (page title, description, filter instructions, and filter form), and the lower 65–70% to dynamic content (sort control and results list or empty state). The results list area is allowed to grow and scroll, while the background remains visible around the panel to preserve the immersive, promotional feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density mirrors the mockup’s promotional, slightly compressed feel: text and controls are stacked with clear but not overly generous vertical spacing. Small typographic blocks (labels, helper text) are separated by 8–16px, and larger blocks (title, description, filter block, results list) by 24–32px. Cards within the results list maintain moderate vertical gaps to feel energetic and conversion-focused, without becoming cluttered thanks to the large, uncluttered background around the central panel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a top-to-bottom funnel inside the central panel. The eye is first caught by the high-contrast Programs page title at the top center, then steps down to the brief explainer copy. From there, attention moves into the filter instructions and the filter form, which highlights how to narrow down options. The sort control directly below acts as a pivot into the results area, drawing the eye toward the first program card. Each card’s CTA at the bottom continues the downward momentum. When no results exist, the empty state message occupies the results region and mirrors the bold, payoff-style lines from the original hero, creating a strong endpoint to the flow.

layout.overall_description: The Programs page appears as a full-bleed lifestyle backdrop (e.g., someone working on a laptop by the beach) with a dark, translucent, vertically oriented panel centered in the viewport. This panel feels like a tall modal or promotional card, spanning roughly half the screen width and rising to occupy most of the height, with a subtle gradient and drop shadow lifting it off the background. At the top of the panel, a bold Programs headline and short description establish context, echoing the hero headline from the mockup. Directly beneath, a stacked filter block lets users narrow programs by commitment and earnings, followed by a slim sort bar that spans the panel width, setting up the results area. Below this, the results list fills the lower portion of the panel with vertically stacked program cards, each full-width and high-contrast, reading like mini landing cards with their own title, benefits, and CTA. If no programs match the filters, the cards are replaced with a centered, two-line empty-state message that uses the same payoff styling as the hero’s bottom statement. The entire experience feels like a focused conversion funnel embedded in a cinematic background, with a strong vertical narrative guiding users from understanding to refining to choosing a program.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller anchors the very top of the central dark panel and acts like the hero header. The panel itself is horizontally centered in the viewport, occupying approximately 45–50% of the viewport width (max-width around 600px), with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Inside this panel, the page controller content begins about 8–16px from the top inner edge. A large, bold Programs heading is centered or slightly left-aligned within the panel and spans most of the panel width, with line lengths constrained to 2–3 lines maximum. Directly below the heading, separated by roughly 8–12px, a medium-weight descriptive paragraph (1–3 short lines) explains what users can do on this page. If there is any brief highlight metric (e.g., number of programs available), it sits as a small, high-contrast line below the description with another 8px gap. Altogether, this block consumes the top 20–25% of the panel height on initial load, clearly distinguished from the functional filter area below by a 24–32px vertical gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The page controller scrolls away with the rest of the panel content; it is not sticky. The central panel itself stays fixed in the horizontal center while the page scrolls. On very small screens, the panel effectively becomes full-width, but the linear top-to-bottom ordering of the controller content remains the same.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter form sits directly below the page controller inside the same panel, maintaining the single-column flow. A vertical gap of about 24–32px separates it from the bottom of the page controller block, visually marking a transition from messaging to controls. The filter block spans the full internal width of the panel, with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides and a slightly darker or subtly outlined sub-panel feel (e.g., a faint border or inner shadow) to give it structure. At the top of the filter form, a short label or instruction line (such as Filter programs) is aligned left and separated from the controller above but visually connected through consistent typography and color accents. Beneath this label, individual filter groups (time commitment, live sessions, earnings range, etc.) are stacked vertically with 16px gaps between groups. Each group arranges its label at the top and its control (selects, checkboxes, sliders) directly under it, occupying nearly the full width of the panel minus padding. This creates a vertical ladder of filters that is easy to scan from top to bottom. The overall filter block height is flexible: it expands as more criteria are added but remains visually contained within the central panel, occupying roughly 15–25% of panel height on initial load. A 24px vertical gap separates the bottom of the filter block from the sort control below, preserving a clear section break while maintaining the moderately dense feel from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filter form scrolls with the rest of the panel content. On taller screens, most of the filter block remains visible alongside the first few results. On very narrow screens, any horizontally aligned filter inputs stack into full-width rows, but the overall vertical order and single-column structure remain unchanged.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned immediately beneath the filter form, separated by approximately 16–24px of vertical space, and acts like a compact, horizontal control bar that transitions the eye from filtering into the results. It spans the full internal width of the panel but is shorter in height than the filter block, giving it a sleek, toolbar-like appearance. The bar has 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding. Within this bar, a small, muted label (such as Sort by) is aligned to the left, and the primary sort dropdown or segmented control is aligned to the right, creating a left-right tension within the otherwise single-column composition. On smaller screens, the label and control can stack vertically (label on top, dropdown below) with an 8px gap while still staying within the same horizontal bounds. A 16–24px gap separates the bottom edge of this sort bar from the first item in the programs results list. Visually, it feels like a trim control strip sitting at the top edge of the results area, similar in weight to the instruction line above the form in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sort control can optionally become sticky relative to the top of the panel: as the user scrolls the results list, the sort bar stays pinned at the top inner edge of the panel while content beneath it scrolls. On implementations without stickiness, it scrolls naturally with the panel content but remains the first element users see above the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the entire vertical space below the sort control and forms the lower, content-heavy portion of the central panel. A vertical gap of 16–24px separates the top of the list from the bottom of the sort control, so the first program card feels clearly connected yet distinct. The list itself is a single column of program cards that spans the full available width inside the panel, with consistent left and right padding inherited from the panel (24–32px). Each program card is stacked one after another with 16–24px vertical spacing between card edges, matching the moderately dense stack seen in the hero’s text blocks. On initial load, the results list area consumes roughly 60–65% of the panel height, making two to three program cards visible above the fold, depending on viewport height. As the user scrolls, the list extends downward while the dark panel maintains the same width, creating a focused scrollable column of offers. The background image remains visible around the panel edges, framing the scrolling content and echoing the hero-like feel of the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The results list scrolls as part of the main page scroll; it is not contained within a separate inner scroll container to avoid double scrollbars. On larger viewports, the beach background can remain fixed (parallax-like) while the results list and central panel move over it, reinforcing the overlay card metaphor from the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each program card is a full-width item within the results list, visually similar to a mini landing card. It extends nearly the entire width of the central panel, leaving only the panel’s internal padding (24–32px) as side margins. The card has a solid dark background, a noticeable drop shadow, and possibly a subtle gradient to echo the bold hero styling. Internal padding is approximately 24px on all sides. Content inside the card is arranged in a top-to-bottom structure: 1) At the top, a bold program title spans the full width, with 8–12px of space below; 2) A compact metadata row (e.g., time commitment, live sessions badge, estimated monthly earnings) either sits on a single horizontal line with icons aligned left and right or stacks into 2–3 short lines with 8px spacing; 3) Below this, a 2–3 line descriptive paragraph explains the program’s benefit, separated by 12–16px of vertical space; 4) At the bottom, a prominent CTA button (e.g., View Program or Learn More) spans most of the card width (80–100%), centered horizontally or aligned left, with 12–16px vertical padding. The CTA button uses the same high-contrast accent color (e.g., bright yellow or red) and slight shadow as the main hero CTA from the mockup, pulling focus to the bottom of each card. There is 16–24px of space between the bottom of the descriptive text and the CTA, and an additional 8–12px between the CTA and the bottom edge of the card. Cards are separated from each other by 16–24px vertical gaps within the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Program cards are standard block elements within the results list and scroll vertically with the page. On hover-capable devices, the card can slightly elevate (stronger shadow, subtle scale-up) to enhance the high-contrast, direct-response feel without changing its layout footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state message block replaces the programs results list area whenever filters produce no matches. It occupies the same horizontal bounds as the results list inside the central panel, starting at the same vertical position just below the sort control, with a 16–24px gap from the sort bar. Instead of stacked cards, the empty state forms a centered typographic composition that echoes the payoff statement at the bottom of the original hero. The block has 24–32px of internal padding and a slightly lighter or more transparent dark background than the cards, visually setting it apart as informational rather than clickable. Inside, a smaller, high-contrast accent line (e.g., in teal or cyan) appears at the top, centered or left-aligned, stating something like No programs found. Below that, separated by 8–12px, a larger, bold white line communicates the payoff or suggestion (such as Relax your filters to discover more opportunities), spanning 1–3 lines. If there is a recovery action (e.g., a button to clear filters), it sits below the main line with 16px of spacing and is centered horizontally. The entire message block is vertically centered within the remaining panel height beneath the sort control when space allows, giving it a strong, calm presence, similar to the bottom reinforcement statement in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty-state block scrolls with the panel content but, when it is the only element in the results region, it will typically appear fully above the fold, requiring little to no scrolling. On smaller screens, its text wraps naturally but remains stacked in the same top-to-bottom order.

name: Program Detail
title: Program Detail
description: Displays full details for a single work-from-home program identified by URL parameter and allows visitors to save the program to favorites by creating a lightweight account in-flow, then continue to an application page.

core_features: Load and display detailed program information for the program specified by the URL parameter `id` using the `getProgramDetails` interface.
Show key program metrics such as time commitment range per week, live group session availability and count, and average monthly earnings based on the `Program` entity returned by `getProgramDetails`.
Display a structured curriculum outline using the `curriculumOutline` array from `getProgramDetails`, with interactions to expand or collapse module details.
Present support information using the `supportDetails` field from `getProgramDetails` to clarify what help the user receives during the program.
List expected outcomes using the `expectedOutcomes` array from `getProgramDetails` to help visitors understand what they can achieve.
Provide a primary action to save the program to favorites via an inline or popup form that collects name, email, and password, and calls `favoriteProgramWithAccount`.
Create a lightweight account and favorite record in one step by submitting the form to `favoriteProgramWithAccount`, and display feedback based on the `success` and `message` fields returned.
Update in-page state after a successful favorite so the primary action reflects that the program is saved during the current session (e.g., disabled or relabeled) without additional log-in flows.
Offer a secondary call-to-action to navigate to `application.html` after reviewing details or favoriting, passing the current program identifier via URL parameters if needed.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: View program details from listing: (1) User clicks a program on programs.html or index.html which links to program_detail.html with the appropriate `id` parameter; (2) Program Detail page reads `id` from the URL; (3) Page calls `getProgramDetails` with that `id`; (4) On success, page displays program title, tagline, metrics, curriculum, support details, and expected outcomes; (5) User can scroll through sections and optionally click to expand curriculum modules; (6) User may leave the page or navigate elsewhere without saving.

Workflow 2: Save a program to favorites and create account inline: (1) User lands on program_detail.html?id={Program.id}; (2) `getProgramDetails` loads and populates program content; (3) User clicks the primary 'Save this program' action; (4) Page opens the inline or popup favorite form, optionally updating the URL to include `favoriteForm=open`; (5) User enters name, email, and password; (6) On form submit, page validates required fields; (7) If valid, page calls `favoriteProgramWithAccount` with `programId` from `id` plus the entered name, email, and password; (8) On success, page shows a success notification using `message`, updates in-page state so the program appears saved and the favorite button is adjusted, and may close the form; (9) On error, page shows an error notification using `message` and keeps the form open for correction.

Workflow 3: Apply after reviewing or favoriting: (1) User reviews program details (and optionally completes the favorite flow); (2) User clicks an 'Apply now for this program' CTA; (3) Page navigates to application.html, optionally appending the current program id as a URL parameter (such as `application.html?programId={id}`) so the application flow can know which program the user came from; (4) User completes the application process on application.html (out of scope for this page).

Workflow 4: Handle invalid or missing program id: (1) User attempts to open program_detail.html without an `id` or with an invalid `id`; (2) Page attempts to call `getProgramDetails` with the provided `id`; (3) If the call fails or returns no valid program, page shows an error state explaining that the program cannot be loaded and hides or disables favorite and apply actions; (4) User may navigate back to the listing page or update the URL to a valid `id`.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the `id` URL parameter and triggers a `getProgramDetails` call; while loading, a loading state is shown and all dependent actions (favorite, apply) are disabled.
If `getProgramDetails` resolves successfully, the program header, metrics, curriculum, support details, and expected outcomes components are populated with the returned data and become interactive.
If `getProgramDetails` fails (e.g., network error or invalid program), an error message component is displayed and interactive CTAs that rely on a valid program are disabled or hidden.

When the user clicks the 'Save this program' trigger, the page opens the favorite account form inline under the header or in a popup, and may update the URL (e.g., adding `favoriteForm=open`) so the open-state can be restored if the user reloads or shares the link.

Within the favorite form, the user interacts with input fields for name, email, and password; on each change, the component updates its local form state and may run basic validation (such as required fields and email format).

On submitting the favorite form, the form component validates the inputs; if validation passes, it disables the submit button and calls `favoriteProgramWithAccount` with the current program id and form values.

When `favoriteProgramWithAccount` returns, the form component reads `success`, `message`, and `programFavorite`; if `success` is true, the page shows a success notification, updates state to mark the program as saved in this session, and can optionally close or collapse the form; if `success` is false, the page displays the error `message` and keeps the form open for editing.

After a successful favorite, subsequent clicks on the save/favorite trigger either do nothing, show a simple informational tooltip or message indicating the program is already saved for this session, or reopen the form in a read-only state depending on the chosen interaction model.

When the user clicks the 'Apply now for this program' CTA, the click handler reads the current `id` and navigates to application.html, preserving the id via a URL parameter so the next page can associate the application with this program.

If a `favoriteForm=open` or similar parameter is present in the URL on initial load, the favorite form component automatically starts in an open state once `getProgramDetails` has successfully loaded, without requiring the user to click the favorite trigger.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the URL parameter `id`, which must match a `Program.id`. On page initialization, the `program-detail-loader` component reads `id` from the query string (e.g., program_detail.html?id={Program.id}). If `id` is absent, null, or invalid, the loader does not call `getProgramDetails` and instead sets an error state that is rendered by an error component and disables favorite and application CTAs. If `id` is present, the loader calls `getProgramDetails(programId=id)` and, on success, populates the program-related components. Additional transient UI state is controlled via optional URL parameters rather than localStorage where persistent or shareable state is helpful. For example, a parameter such as `favoriteForm=open` can be appended when the user opens the favorite form, enabling the form to auto-open after a reload or when the URL is shared; when the form is closed, the component can update the URL (e.g., via history API) to remove this parameter. A parameter such as `section={sectionName}` may be used to indicate which content section (metrics, curriculum, support, outcomes) should be scrolled into view after load; the page reads `section` after `getProgramDetails` resolves and scrolls to the matching component. The result of `favoriteProgramWithAccount` is stored only in in-memory page state (e.g., a boolean flag like `isFavorite` and a last `favoriteMessage`) during the current session; this state is not persisted beyond the page and is not encoded in localStorage. However, the page may optionally reflect a successful favorite in the URL by adding a parameter like `favoriteStatus=success` to allow the success notification to re-render correctly after a reload. All dynamic content is driven exclusively by API responses (no hardcoded program, curriculum, or metrics values), and all navigations to application.html include the current `id` value as a URL parameter so downstream flows can reconstruct the association.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the `id` URL parameter, calling `getProgramDetails` with that id, managing loading and error states, and passing loaded data to child components.

Displays high-level program information, including program name and optional tagline or focus, and contains primary actions such as 'Save this program' and a secondary 'Apply now for this program' CTA.

Shows numeric and boolean metrics for the program such as the weekly time commitment range, whether live group sessions are included, how many live sessions occur per month, and the average monthly earnings estimate.
Lists the modules returned in `curriculumOutline` and allows users to expand or collapse individual modules to view `moduleSummary` details for each module.
Displays the `supportDetails` text from `getProgramDetails` so the user understands what support (such as coaching, group calls, or resources) is available in the program.
Renders a list of expected outcomes using the `expectedOutcomes` array from `getProgramDetails` to outline what the user may achieve by completing the program.

Primary action control that initiates the favorite flow; when clicked, opens the favorite account form (inline or popup) and may update the URL (for example, adding `favoriteForm=open`). After a successful favorite, it updates its appearance and behavior based on in-page state.

Collects the visitor’s name, email, and chosen password to create a lightweight account and favorite the current program in a single step by calling `favoriteProgramWithAccount`. Handles client-side validation, submission state, and response handling based on `success`, `message`, and `programFavorite`.
Displays success or error messages after the favorite form is submitted, using the `message` returned from `favoriteProgramWithAccount` and internal state indicating the last favorite result.

Provides navigation from the Program Detail page to application.html when the user is ready to apply, passing the current program id through URL parameters so the application page knows which program the user selected.
Renders when `getProgramDetails` fails or when no valid `id` URL parameter is present, instructing the user that the program could not be loaded and preventing access to favorite and apply actions.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered vertical column inside a tall, dark translucent panel that sits over a full-bleed lifestyle (beach) background. All components are stacked top-to-bottom within this panel, following a linear funnel: high-impact program summary at the top, key proof (metrics + outcomes) just below, then conversion actions (favorite + account form + apply CTA), and finally deeper detail sections (curriculum and support) further down the same column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered vertical column inside a tall, dark translucent panel that sits over a full-bleed lifestyle (beach) background. All components are stacked top-to-bottom within this panel, following a linear funnel: high-impact program summary at the top, key proof (metrics + outcomes) just below, then conversion actions (favorite + account form + apply CTA), and finally deeper detail sections (curriculum and support) further down the same column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (max-width around 560–640px), centered with visible background on both sides. Within the initial viewport height, about the upper 60–65% is given to program messaging (header, metrics, outcomes), with the lower 35–40% reserved for the favorite/account conversion UI and the main application CTA. As the user scrolls, the panel extends downward to accommodate curriculum and support details, maintaining consistent panel width and padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content in the panel is moderately dense and conversion-focused: typographic blocks and CTAs are stacked with compact but comfortable vertical spacing. Section-to-section gaps are roughly 24–32px, while related text within a section uses tighter 8–16px spacing. Inputs and buttons have visibly generous padding and drop shadows that match the bold, direct-response style, but elements never feel cramped due to the breathing room provided by the surrounding full-bleed background.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow is strongly vertical and funnel-like. The eye lands first on the bold program title and hero summary at the top center of the panel, moves down into a high-contrast metrics panel and outcomes list as proof, then is led naturally into the favorite-program trigger and inline sign-up form. The form visually anchors attention toward the large, bright application CTA button near the bottom of the panel. Notification and error states appear in-line in this flow, just above or in place of the CTAs, so users clearly see next steps without breaking the vertical reading pattern.

layout.overall_description: The page shows a full-bleed, high-saturation beach photograph spanning the entire viewport, with a single tall, dark translucent panel centered horizontally. This panel feels like a glossy, promotional card overlay, around 40–50% of the viewport width, with soft drop shadow and 24–32px internal padding. At the top of the panel, a bold, high-contrast program headline and short summary hook the visitor. Directly beneath, a compact metrics band and a clear expected-outcomes block stack in a tight vertical rhythm, filling most of the upper portion of the panel. Below this proof area, the flow transitions into conversion: a bright favorite/save button invites commitment, expanding an inline sign-up form right underneath when needed. A feedback banner and a large, full-width application CTA button occupy the lower third of the panel, echoing the dramatic, promotional color palette. As users scroll, the panel extends downward to reveal an accordion-style curriculum outline followed by a text-focused support section, both still centered and contained within the same single column. Loading and error states temporarily replace the card’s inner content but preserve the central panel frame and the immersive background, maintaining the high-impact hero experience while focusing attention on either progress or remediation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader appears inside the same central dark translucent panel, replacing the normal inner content. The panel itself remains centered and sized as usual (about 40–50% of viewport width, max-width around 560–640px) with consistent internal padding. The loader (spinner and/or text) is vertically and horizontally centered within this panel, occupying roughly the middle third of the panel height. There is generous empty space around it, allowing the motion and message to stand out against the dark overlay while the beach background is still visible around the panel edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces all other components within the central panel while loading; fixed to the panel’s center and scrolls with the page if the viewport is shorter than the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program header section sits at the very top of the central panel’s content column. It starts about 24–32px from the inside top edge of the panel and spans the full internal width. The main program title is large and bold, occupying roughly the upper 15–20% of the panel’s initial viewport height. Directly beneath the title, a short subheadline and/or one to two lines of supporting copy are stacked with tight 8–16px spacing, creating a compact but impactful hero message block. Left and right, the header content is centered within the panel width, with equal padding on both sides. Vertically below this header, a 24–32px gap leads into the metrics panel, maintaining a clear separation between the headline block and the proof section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the central column, scrolling naturally with the page within the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metrics panel is positioned directly under the program header section, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space. It spans the full internal width of the central panel and reads as a compact, high-contrast band of information—either as stacked rows or a tight list—occupying around 10–15% of the initial panel height. Individual metrics are arranged vertically with 8–12px between them, or in two short rows, but always maintaining the single-column feel. The background of this section can be slightly darker or accented (e.g., subtle gradient) to visually distinguish it from the rest of the panel, while still blending into the same dark overlay. Below it, a 24–32px gap separates the metrics from the expected outcomes list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow section within the panel; no sticky behavior. Resizes vertically based on content but maintains fixed horizontal padding consistent with the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The curriculum accordion lives lower in the column, below the primary conversion area, so it becomes available as the user scrolls. It is positioned after the application CTA and separated from it by a clear 32px gap to signal a shift from action to deeper detail. The accordion spans the full width of the central panel, with each accordion header running edge-to-edge inside the panel padding. Initially collapsed sections stack vertically with about 8–12px between headers, forming a clean list that likely occupies 20–30% of the panel height when in view. When one or more sections expand, the accordion extends downward, pushing subsequent content further down while remaining within the centered column. The overall block keeps the same 24–32px padding top and bottom as other major sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow content below the fold of the initial view; expands and contracts vertically within the central column, pushing other components down but never overlapping them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support details section appears directly below the curriculum accordion, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full internal width of the panel and is laid out as a text-forward block: a bold subheading at the top, followed by short paragraphs or bullet points stacked with 8–16px spacing. This section typically occupies the lower portion of the scrollable area and may take up 20–30% of the panel height when fully in view. Compared to the conversion elements, the typography here is slightly less dramatic but still high-contrast for readability on the dark overlay. The section ends with a comfortable 32px bottom padding before the panel’s bottom edge, ensuring the content does not feel cramped against the card boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow section near the bottom of the overall page content within the panel; scrolls normally with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The expected outcomes list is positioned just below the program-metrics-panel, separated by about 24–32px. It spans the full panel width and serves as a high-impact proof area occupying roughly 15–20% of the initial panel height. A short, bold heading introduces the outcomes, followed by a compact list of 3–6 key outcomes. Each outcome line is stacked vertically with tight 8–12px spacing, creating a dense but readable list. The section is visually framed as an important summary of benefits before conversion, and a slightly accentuated color (e.g., teal or bright white text) can be used for outcome labels or icons to echo the promotional style. Below this list, another 24–32px gap leads into the favorite-program-trigger CTA, clearly transitioning from benefits to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow section in the upper-middle of the panel; scrolls normally while anchoring the proof area of the funnel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-program-trigger is placed immediately under the expected-outcomes-list, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space to signal a move from information into commitment. It appears as a full-width, high-contrast button within the central panel, aligning with the left and right padding of other content. Vertically, it occupies a prominent slice of the lower half of the initial viewport, roughly 8–12% of panel height due to its generous padding and bold typography. The button sits centered horizontally, with slight drop shadow and saturated color (e.g., bright yellow or red) to stand out against the dark background. When the user scrolls, it remains part of the natural flow, with the favorite-program-form expanding directly beneath it when triggered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow CTA; when activated, it controls the expansion of the favorite-program-form immediately below but does not itself move or become sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-program-form is positioned directly beneath the favorite-program-trigger, with a tight 16px gap between the button and the top of the form when visible. In its collapsed state, it effectively occupies no vertical space, keeping the layout compact. When expanded, the form appears as a contained sub-panel spanning the full width of the central card, with 24px internal padding on left and right and 16–24px spacing between fields. It typically includes one or two input fields (e.g., email and password or code) stacked vertically with labels, each input having comfortable 12–16px internal padding. The form block occupies roughly 15–20% of the panel height when fully visible. The bottom of the form sits about 16–24px above the favorite-feedback-banner or, if the banner is not present, about 24–32px above the application-cta, visually tying the account creation step to the primary action below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Collapsible, in-flow section directly controlled by the favorite-program-trigger. Expands and collapses vertically, pushing the feedback banner and application CTA down or pulling them up, but always within the same single column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-feedback-banner appears just below the favorite-program-form, separated by approximately 16–24px to keep it visually connected to the form’s outcome. When the form is not visible, the banner can appear directly under the favorite-program-trigger with the same gap. The banner spans the full internal width of the panel as a low-profile horizontal strip, about the height of one to two lines of text plus 12–16px vertical padding. It uses a bright, contrasting background color (e.g., teal or yellow) to clearly indicate success or error while still fitting the bold promotional style. The banner typically occupies only about 5–8% of the panel height and is followed by a 24–32px gap leading into the application-cta, so users see the feedback and are then guided immediately to the main action below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient, in-flow element that appears or disappears without changing its horizontal position. Its vertical presence pushes the application CTA down slightly when visible, maintaining the linear vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application-cta is the visually dominant button placed near the bottom of the initially visible portion of the panel. It sits below the favorite-feedback-banner (or directly under the favorite-program-form if no banner is shown), separated by 24–32px of vertical space. The button spans the entire internal width of the central panel, aligning with the column’s padding. It has generous vertical padding (14–18px), large bold typography, and a strong saturated color with noticeable drop shadow, making it one of the most attention-grabbing elements on the page. In the initial viewport, this CTA occupies roughly the lower 15–20% of the panel height, anchoring the visual funnel. As the user scrolls down to see curriculum and support content, this button scrolls out of view normally, reinforcing that the primary action is available before reading deeper details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow primary CTA; not sticky. It remains within the central column and scrolls out of view as users move into the lower informational sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When triggered, the program-error-state takes over the inner content area of the central panel, similar to the loader. The dark translucent panel remains centered on the full-bleed background, but instead of the standard layout, a single error block is displayed. This block is centered vertically and horizontally within the panel, occupying roughly the middle 30–40% of the panel height. It typically includes an error icon, a bold error headline, and one to two lines of explanatory text, stacked with 8–16px spacing. A secondary retry or back CTA may appear beneath the message, centered, with 16–24px spacing above and below. There is ample negative space around the error content so it feels clear and focused, not cluttered with other UI elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces normal content inside the central panel. Static within the card, centered, and scrolling with the page if the panel exceeds viewport height.

name: Success Stories
title: Success Stories
description: Browse, filter, and sort success stories based on lifestyle, monthly earnings, and hours worked per week, with links to detailed story pages.

core_features: Load dynamic filter and sort options for lifestyle, minimum monthly earnings, hours worked per week ranges, and sort order using getSuccessStoryFilterOptions.
Allow users to select a lifestyle filter (including one with value "parents"), a minimum monthly earnings value, and an hours-per-week range.
Allow users to choose a sort order, including an option with value "most_recent" so that newer stories appear first.
Search and retrieve a list of success stories based on the selected filters and sort order using searchSuccessStories.
Display success stories as interactive cards that show key summary information from the SuccessStory model and link to a dedicated detail page.
Persist and share the current filter and sort state using URL query parameters, so that the listing can be deep-linked and reloaded with the same criteria.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load
1) User navigates to success_stories.html (possibly with URL query parameters).
2) The page initializes a URL state manager that reads any query parameters (e.g., lifestyle, minMonthlyEarnings, minHoursPerWeek, maxHoursPerWeek, sortBy).
3) The page calls getSuccessStoryFilterOptions with no parameters to obtain lifestyleOptions, minMonthlyEarningsPresets, hoursPerWeekRangeOptions, and sortOptions.
4) The filter form component is populated with these options and pre-selects values based on URL parameters where present; if sortBy is missing or invalid, it defaults to the option whose value is "most_recent".
5) The page then calls searchSuccessStories using the effective filter and sort values derived from the URL (or defaults).
6) The story list component displays the returned SuccessStory items as cards, ready for interaction.

Workflow: Adjusting filters and sorting
1) User changes one or more filter controls (e.g., selects a lifestyle option, adjusts the minimum monthly earnings control, chooses an hours-per-week range, or changes the sort option).
2) On filter form submission (e.g., clicking an apply/update button), the filter form sends the current control values to the URL state manager.
3) The URL state manager updates the browser URL query parameters (e.g., via pushState/replaceState) to reflect the new lifestyle, minMonthlyEarnings, minHoursPerWeek, maxHoursPerWeek, and sortBy values.
4) After updating the URL, the page calls searchSuccessStories with the new parameter values.
5) The story list component re-renders with the new results from searchSuccessStories.

Workflow: Following a deep link with predefined filters
1) User arrives at success_stories.html with predefined query parameters (for example, a link targeting a specific lifestyle value such as "parents").
2) The URL state manager parses the query parameters and passes them as initial values to the filter form.
3) The filter form initializes its controls to match these values.
4) The page calls searchSuccessStories with the parsed parameters.
5) The story list shows stories that match the predefined criteria, allowing the user to further adjust filters if desired.
6) Any subsequent changes update the URL and refresh the results as described in the filter workflow.

Workflow: Opening a story detail
1) User reviews the list of success story cards.
2) User clicks on a specific story card or a dedicated link within it.
3) The story list component reads the selected story's id field from the bound SuccessStory object.
4) The page navigates to story_detail.html with a query parameter id equal to that story's id (e.g., story_detail.html?id=<selectedId>), using normal navigation.
5) The user views the full story on the detail page (outside of this page's scope).
interactions: On page load, the page calls getSuccessStoryFilterOptions (no arguments) to load available filter and sort options.
On page load (after filter options are available and URL parameters are parsed), the page calls searchSuccessStories with parameters derived from the current URL state.
When the user changes the lifestyle selection control, the filter form updates its internal state; the change is applied to the search only when the user submits the filter form.

When the user adjusts the minimum monthly earnings control (using presets, a slider, or an input bound to minMonthlyEarningsPresets), the selected value is stored in the filter form state and later passed as minMonthlyEarnings on form submission.

When the user selects an hours-per-week range option (based on hoursPerWeekRangeOptions), the corresponding minHours and maxHours from that option are stored in the filter form state and later used as minHoursPerWeek and maxHoursPerWeek in searchSuccessStories.
When the user changes the sort option (based on sortOptions), the selected value is updated in the filter form state; on form submission, this is passed as sortBy to searchSuccessStories.

When the user submits the filter form (e.g., clicks an apply or update action), the event handler prevents default page reload, updates the URL query parameters via the URL state manager, and triggers a searchSuccessStories call with the new parameters.
When the user clicks a story card, the card's click handler uses the bound SuccessStory.id value to navigate the browser to story_detail.html?id=<id>.

If the user uses browser navigation (back/forward) changing URL query parameters, the URL state manager detects the change, updates the filter form state, and triggers a new searchSuccessStories call using the new URL-derived parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter and sort state. Supported parameters include:
- lifestyle: optional; if present and matches one of the lifestyleOptions.value entries (including the value "parents"), it is passed directly as the lifestyle argument to searchSuccessStories. If missing or invalid, lifestyle is passed as null/undefined.
- minMonthlyEarnings: optional numeric; if present and a valid number, it is passed as minMonthlyEarnings to searchSuccessStories. The filter form initializes its minimum earnings control to this value if it matches a preset or uses it as a custom value; if absent, the control initializes to an unselected or default state.
- minHoursPerWeek and maxHoursPerWeek: optional numerics; if both are present and valid, they are passed as minHoursPerWeek and maxHoursPerWeek to searchSuccessStories. The filter form attempts to match this pair to one of the hoursPerWeekRangeOptions; if matched, the corresponding option is selected; if not, the form may show a custom or unselected state.
- sortBy: optional; if present and equal to a value found in sortOptions (e.g., "most_recent" or another supported sort value), it is passed as sortBy to searchSuccessStories. If sortBy is missing or invalid, the page selects the sortOptions entry whose value is "most_recent" as the default and passes that to searchSuccessStories, and also updates the URL to reflect this default if desired.

On any filter form submission, the filter component constructs a new set of query parameters from its current state and instructs the URL state manager to update the URL. After the URL is updated, the same values are used to call searchSuccessStories. When navigation events (such as browser back/forward or external links into the page) change the query parameters, the URL state manager re-derives the effective filter state from the URL, updates the filter form binding, and triggers searchSuccessStories with those values. No filter or sort state is persisted in localStorage; only the URL (and the currently displayed in-memory state) is used.

components.functionality: Loads lifestyle, minimum monthly earnings presets, hours-per-week range options, and sort options for the page using getSuccessStoryFilterOptions, and exposes them to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getSuccessStoryFilterOptions with no parameters when the page loads.
onSuccess: Store the returned options in a shared state (e.g., in-memory store or props) used by the filter form and sort components.
onError: Expose an error state that other components can use to display a functional error message (without hardcoded text).

components.functionality: Parses and updates URL query parameters for lifestyle, minMonthlyEarnings, minHoursPerWeek, maxHoursPerWeek, and sortBy, and synchronizes them with the filter form and search logic.
event_handlers: onInit: Read current URL query parameters and provide them as initial state values for the filter form and search loader.
onFilterChange: When the filter form submits new values, construct a new query string and update the URL using browser history APIs without reloading the page.
onPopState: Listen for browser navigation events; when detected, re-parse query parameters and notify dependent components (filter form, search-results-loader) of the updated values.

components.functionality: Provides controls for users to select lifestyle, minimum monthly earnings, and hours-per-week ranges, and to choose a sort order. On submission, it passes the selected values to the URL state manager and triggers a search.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize each control's selected value from the URL state (provided by url-state-manager). If sortBy is not present or invalid, default to the option whose value is "most_recent" from sortOptions.
onLifestyleChange: Update internal form state when the user selects a different lifestyle option, ensuring that an option with value "parents" is available when provided by lifestyleOptions.
onMinMonthlyEarningsChange: Update internal form state with the selected or entered minimum earnings value.
onHoursRangeChange: When a range option is selected, update internal form state with the corresponding minHours and maxHours values from hoursPerWeekRangeOptions.
onSortByChange: Update internal form state with the chosen sort option value.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission, send the current form state (lifestyle, minMonthlyEarnings, minHoursPerWeek, maxHoursPerWeek, sortBy) to url-state-manager to update URL query parameters, and then trigger the search-results-loader to call searchSuccessStories.
onReset: Optionally provide a reset action that clears form state to defaults (e.g., no filters, default sort), informs url-state-manager to clear related query parameters, and triggers a new search.

components.functionality: Calls searchSuccessStories with parameters taken from the current URL state and provides the resulting list of SuccessStory records to the story list component.
event_handlers: onInit: After filter-options-loader and url-state-manager have established initial state, call searchSuccessStories with the current URL-derived parameters.
onUrlStateChange: Whenever url-state-manager reports that URL query parameters have changed, call searchSuccessStories again with the updated parameters.
onSuccess: Provide the resulting SuccessStory array to the success-stories-list component and update any result summary state.
onError: Expose an error state that can be handled by the list component or a simple error-message component.

components.functionality: Displays the list of success stories returned by searchSuccessStories as clickable cards, each showing key summary fields and linking to the story detail page.
event_handlers: onCardClick: When a story card is clicked, read the bound SuccessStory.id and navigate to story_detail.html?id=<id> using standard navigation.
onEmptyResults: If the bound SuccessStory array is empty, expose a state that can be used to render a functional empty-state message (without hardcoded content).
onResultsUpdate: Re-render the list whenever the bound SuccessStory array changes (e.g., after a new search).

components.functionality: Shows functional summary information about the current search, such as the number of stories returned and which filters are active, based on the latest search results and URL state.
event_handlers: onResultsChange: Update displayed counts or summary based on the latest SuccessStory[] length and active filters.

onFilterTagClick (optional): If active filters are shown as removable tags, handle clicks to remove a given filter (e.g., clearing lifestyle), delegate this action to success-stories-filter-form, which then updates URL via url-state-manager and triggers a new search.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, tall, centrally aligned panel floating over a full-bleed lifestyle (beach) background. All interactive and textual content is stacked in a strict top-to-bottom sequence inside this panel. The top half of the panel is reserved for narrative messaging about success stories (page title, supporting copy, guidance), while the lower half transitions into the functional layer (filters, summary bar, then the stories list). There are no side columns; everything lives in one vertical column that is visually treated like a modal card.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, tall, centrally aligned panel floating over a full-bleed lifestyle (beach) background. All interactive and textual content is stacked in a strict top-to-bottom sequence inside this panel. The top half of the panel is reserved for narrative messaging about success stories (page title, supporting copy, guidance), while the lower half transitions into the functional layer (filters, summary bar, then the stories list). There are no side columns; everything lives in one vertical column that is visually treated like a modal card.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (max-width around 560–640px), centered horizontally with generous background showing on both sides. Vertically, the panel initially fills most of the viewport height. The upper ~60–65% of the panel is focused on messaging about browsing and personalizing success stories, culminating in a short instruction line. The lower ~35–40% of the initial viewport is dedicated to the interactive area: the filter form at the top of this zone, followed immediately by the compact results summary bar and then the beginning of the success story list, which continues below the fold as the page scrolls.
layout_strategies.content_density: Inside the panel, content is moderately dense: text blocks, filters, and results are stacked with vertical spacing following the 8px system. Small typographic gaps (8–16px) separate related lines of text, while larger gaps (24–32px) separate major sections (headline block → simplifying statement → filter instruction → filter form → summary bar → story list). The density suggests a direct-response, results-focused feel without becoming cramped, and the uncluttered photographic background outside the panel provides breathing room around this dense central column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is a strong, top-to-bottom funnel centered on the panel. The user’s eye first lands on the high-contrast page headline at the top of the panel, then drops to a short block of supporting copy, then a bold, simplifying statement explaining that stories can be matched to lifestyle or earnings. A concise instruction line points down toward the filter form. The full-width, high-contrast primary action in the filter form (e.g., an ‘Update Stories’ button) draws the eye next. Immediately beneath, the results summary bar confirms the outcome of the chosen filters and acts as a visual bridge into the success stories list, which then becomes the focus as the user scrolls. Throughout, the eye is guided downward in a controlled, linear sequence with no lateral distractions.

layout.overall_description: The Success Stories page presents a dramatic, conversion-oriented hero experience anchored by a single, tall, dark translucent panel centered over a full-bleed beach photograph. The panel feels like a promotional modal card: it’s about 45–50% of the viewport’s width, with soft, high-contrast gradients, subtle drop shadows, and saturated accent colors echoing the original mockup. At the very top of this panel, a large, bold headline announces the Success Stories section, followed closely by a few lines of supporting copy and a prominent simplifying statement explaining that you can instantly find stories that match your lifestyle, income goals, and hours. A short instructional line then orients the user toward using the filters below. Directly underneath, the filter form spans the full internal width of the panel, with stacked filter controls and a bright, full-width action button that visually reads like the primary CTA. Just below the button, a compact but bold results summary bar runs edge-to-edge within the panel, confirming how many stories match the current filters and providing quick clear/reset affordances. As you scroll, the success stories list continues downward in the same centered column, with each story presented as a full-width card inside the panel’s effective width, separated by consistent vertical spacing. The beach background stays visible to the left and right, keeping the page immersive while the dark, translucent central strip carries the entire narrative and interactive experience in a clear, vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-options-loader is a non-visual, background component that executes before and alongside the success-stories-filter-form. Conceptually, it sits just above the filter form in the document hierarchy, ensuring filter dropdowns and controls are fully populated before the user interacts. It occupies no visible space and does not alter the dimensions of the central panel. When loading states are needed, they are expressed inside the filter form itself (e.g., disabled controls or inline skeletons), so the loader behaves like an invisible layer anchored to the top-middle portion of the panel where the filter section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; operates logically above the filter form without taking up layout space or affecting the flow of visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is entirely non-visual and exists as an invisible behavioral layer spanning the whole page. It conceptually wraps the central panel, synchronizing the filter form, summary bar, and success stories list with the URL. It does not occupy any width or height and does not introduce gaps or offsets; instead, it simply ensures that as the user scrolls, filters, and navigates, the state is preserved and reflected in the address bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered and global; attaches to the page shell and central panel logically, with no physical footprint or impact on spatial flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The success-stories-filter-form sits in the lower portion of the hero panel’s initial viewport area, directly beneath the textual instruction line that tells users how to refine stories. Within the centered dark translucent panel (about 45–50% of viewport width, max-width ~560–640px), the filter form spans the full internal width minus equal left/right padding of roughly 24–32px. There is a clear vertical gap of about 24px between the instruction text above and the top edge of the form, signaling a transition from messaging to interaction. The form’s controls (e.g., lifestyle selector, monthly earnings range, hours per week) are stacked vertically in a single column, each control taking the full available width, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between them following the 8px grid. At the bottom of the form, a high-contrast, full-width primary action button (e.g., ‘Update Stories’ or ‘See My Matches’) stretches from left padding to right padding, with 12–16px of vertical padding inside to give it a strong, tappable presence. The bottom of the form ends roughly 16–24px above the results-summary-bar, visually connecting the form to the feedback the user will see next. Overall, the form occupies roughly the upper half of the functional zone of the panel and acts as the immediate focal point once the user’s eye moves past the headline and explanatory copy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the central panel’s content in the normal document flow; it is not sticky or fixed, but is initially fully visible within the first viewport along with the hero messaging above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-loader is positioned logically just above the success-stories-list and directly below the results-summary-bar in the content flow. Under normal conditions, it is invisible and occupies no space. When results are being fetched or refreshed (e.g., after changing filters), it temporarily claims the vertical space where the first few story cards would appear. In that state, it spans the same internal width as the story list (matching the central panel’s width minus 24–32px horizontal padding) and is centered within that column. It may present as a subtle animated loading bar or a centered spinner with a short status line, with 24–32px of vertical height, respecting the same top and bottom margins (16–24px) used between list items. Once loading completes, this space is seamlessly replaced by the first story cards, ensuring the overall vertical rhythm remains consistent.
