component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline within the column where the story list begins; participates in normal scrolling and does not fix or overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The success-stories-list sits immediately below the results-summary-bar (and the transient search-results-loader, when active) within the same centered panel column. It occupies the full effective width of the panel content, aligned flush with the form and summary bar above, with 24–32px of horizontal padding on both sides. A vertical gap of about 24px separates the summary bar’s bottom edge from the top of the first story card, clearly delineating the control/feedback zone from the content zone. Each success story appears as a full-width card stacked in a single column, with 24–32px of vertical spacing between cards, giving the list a moderately dense but readable rhythm. The list extends downward beyond the initial viewport, so users scroll through the stories while the beach background still frames the page edges. The cards themselves inherit the high-contrast, direct-response aesthetic (dark backgrounds with bright accents, bold headings), but structurally they simply follow one after another in a linear feed, occupying the lower segment of the central panel and continuing indefinitely as more stories load or paginate.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block flow: the list scrolls with the page, anchored below the summary bar. It does not use fixed or sticky behavior; instead, it creates a long, vertically scrollable content area within the centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-summary-bar is positioned directly beneath the success-stories-filter-form and serves as the visual hinge between the interactive filters and the story content below. It spans the full internal width of the central panel (matching the form and list widths), with equal left and right padding of roughly 16–24px. There is a compact vertical gap of about 16–20px between the bottom edge of the form’s primary button and the top edge of the summary bar, making the bar feel closely tied to the filter action. The bar itself is a low-profile horizontal band, about 48–64px tall, with a strong contrast background (for example, a teal or dark gradient strip) that stands out against the darker panel but remains secondary to the CTA button above. Within this band, the total number of matching stories and key filter highlights are left-aligned, while optional quick actions (like ‘Clear filters’) are right-aligned, all on a single visual line or two tightly stacked lines. As the user scrolls down into the list, the bar remains visually the top of the results area, helping orient the user and confirming that they are seeing content personalized to their selections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows directly beneath the filter form within the panel. Optionally, it can become sticky at the top edge of the viewport once the user scrolls past the hero messaging, maintaining a thin band across the top of the central column while the story list scrolls underneath; when not sticky, it simply scrolls away in normal document flow.

name: Story Detail
title: Story Detail
description: Page for viewing the full details of a single success story and allowing guests to bookmark it to their reading list using an email address.

core_features: Load and display a single success story based on the 'id' URL parameter using getSuccessStoryDetails.
Show complete story information including title, headline, person name (when present), lifestyle type, publication date, and full narrative content.
Display key outcome and effort statistics such as monthly earnings and weekly hours using SuccessStory fields.
When available, show the associated Program and Plan names as navigable links to program_detail.html and plan_detail.html using their IDs from getSuccessStoryDetails.
Provide a 'Save to reading list' / 'Bookmark this story' action that opens a simple inline or popup form for guests to enter an email address.
On bookmark form submission, call bookmarkStoryAsGuest with storyId and email, then show success or error feedback based on the returned result.
Handle missing or invalid story IDs by showing an error state and suppressing story content and bookmark actions.
user_workflows:
View a story from the Success Stories list or homepage:
1) User clicks a story on success_stories.html or index.html.
2) Browser navigates to story_detail.html?id={SuccessStory.id}.
3) On page load, the Story Detail page reads the 'id' URL parameter.
4) The page calls getSuccessStoryDetails(storyId = id).
5) While loading, the page shows a loading state.
6) When data is returned, the page displays the story header, stats, full content, and any available associated Program and Plan links.
7) If the story cannot be loaded, the page shows an error message and hides the story and bookmark components.

Navigate to associated program or plan from a story:
1) User scrolls through the story content and sees the associated Program and/or Plan displayed (when present in the story data).
2) User clicks the Program name link.
3) The page navigates to program_detail.html?id={associatedProgram.id} using the Program ID from getSuccessStoryDetails.
4) Alternatively, the user clicks the Plan name link.
5) The page navigates to plan_detail.html?id={associatedPlan.id} using the Plan ID from getSuccessStoryDetails.

Bookmark a story as a guest:
1) User is viewing a story on story_detail.html?id={SuccessStory.id}.
2) User clicks the 'Save to reading list' / 'Bookmark this story' button.
3) The page reveals an inline or popup bookmark form with an email input field and submit button.
4) User enters an email address and submits the form.
5) The page validates that the email field is not empty and has a valid email format; if invalid, it shows a validation message and does not call the API.
6) On valid input, the page calls bookmarkStoryAsGuest(storyId = current SuccessStory.id, email = entered email).
7) While the request is in progress, the form shows a submitting state and disables repeated submissions.
8) If bookmarkStoryAsGuest returns success = true, the page shows the success message from the response and may update the bookmark button state (for example, indicating that the story is saved for this email during the current view).
9) If success = false or the call fails, the page shows the error message from the response and allows the user to correct the email or retry submission.
interactions: On page load: read the 'id' URL parameter; if present, trigger getSuccessStoryDetails. If 'id' is missing or empty, show an error state with no API call.

On successful response from getSuccessStoryDetails: bind the returned SuccessStory to the story header, stats, and content components; bind associatedProgram and associatedPlan (when provided) to the related links component.
On click of the Program name (when associatedProgram is present and has an id): navigate the browser to program_detail.html?id={associatedProgram.id}.
On click of the Plan name (when associatedPlan is present and has an id): navigate the browser to plan_detail.html?id={associatedPlan.id}.

On click of the bookmark button: toggle visibility of the bookmark form (inline below the button or in a simple popup); set internal state to track that the user intends to bookmark this specific SuccessStory.id.
On change in the bookmark email input field: update internal form state with the current email value and clear validation/error messages when the user edits.
On bookmark form submission: validate the email input; if invalid, display validation errors; if valid, call bookmarkStoryAsGuest(storyId = current SuccessStory.id, email = current input).

On success from bookmarkStoryAsGuest: display the returned message, optionally disable or relabel the bookmark button for the current session, and keep the form visible or collapse it depending on the design; no additional navigation is required.
On error from bookmarkStoryAsGuest (success = false or call failure): display the error message from the API or a generic error message, allow the user to adjust the email and resubmit.

On change of the 'id' URL parameter (for example, via navigation or internal routing): re-trigger the loading sequence by calling getSuccessStoryDetails with the new id and resetting bookmark-related state for the new story.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from the 'id' URL parameter: story_detail.html?id={SuccessStory.id}. When the page loads or when this parameter changes, the page calls getSuccessStoryDetails(storyId = id). If 'id' is missing, empty, or invalid (e.g., the API returns no story), the page enters an error state where story content and bookmark actions are not rendered. The current SuccessStory.id from the getSuccessStoryDetails response is also used for bookmarkStoryAsGuest calls. Navigation to related pages uses URL parameters only, constructing program_detail.html?id={associatedProgram.id} and plan_detail.html?id={associatedPlan.id} without any local storage. Any transient UI state (such as loading indicators, validation errors, bookmark success state, and bookmark form visibility) is kept in page or component memory and is reset when the 'id' parameter changes.

components.functionality: Reads the 'id' URL parameter and calls getSuccessStoryDetails to fetch the story, associated Program, and associated Plan. Manages loading and error states and exposes the fetched data to other components.
Displays high-level information about the story, such as title, headline, person_name, lifestyle, and story_date, using the loaded SuccessStory.
Shows key metrics from the SuccessStory, including monthly_earnings and the hours_per_week_min/hours_per_week_max range. This gives users a quick overview of results and time investment.
Renders the full narrative of the success story using the SuccessStory.content field, optionally preceded by the summary if available.

When associatedProgram and/or associatedPlan are available from getSuccessStoryDetails, displays their names as links. Clicking these links navigates to program_detail.html or plan_detail.html with the appropriate 'id' URL parameters.

Provides a 'Save to reading list' / 'Bookmark this story' button that is enabled when a SuccessStory is loaded. Clicking the button toggles visibility of the bookmark form component for the current story.

Inline or popup form that appears when the bookmark CTA is activated. Collects a guest email address and, upon submission, calls bookmarkStoryAsGuest with the current storyId and email. Handles validation, loading state, and displays success or error messages based on the response.

Displays global feedback such as loading indicators, errors from getSuccessStoryDetails, and success or error messages from bookmarkStoryAsGuest, using the message data returned by the interfaces where available.

Handles situations where the 'id' URL parameter is missing, empty, or when getSuccessStoryDetails returns no valid story. Shows an appropriate error state and hides story content and bookmark components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit inside a single, tall, dark translucent panel that is vertically and horizontally centered over a full-bleed beach background. Within this panel, content flows in a strictly top-to-bottom, single-column stack: story header at the top, then stats, then full story content, then associated links, and finally the bookmarking CTA section (CTA line, form, and feedback messages) near the bottom. Loader and fallback state temporarily replace or overlay this central stack.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit inside a single, tall, dark translucent panel that is vertically and horizontally centered over a full-bleed beach background. Within this panel, content flows in a strictly top-to-bottom, single-column stack: story header at the top, then stats, then full story content, then associated links, and finally the bookmarking CTA section (CTA line, form, and feedback messages) near the bottom. Loader and fallback state temporarily replace or overlay this central stack.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (max-width around 540–640px), with the beach photo clearly visible on both sides. Vertically, the panel can extend beyond the viewport and scroll if needed. Approximately the upper 60–65% of the panel height is devoted to the story itself (header, stats, full content, related links), while the lower 35–40% is dedicated to the bookmarking funnel (bookmark CTA, email form, feedback). Internal padding is about 24–32px on the sides and 24px top and bottom, with 24–32px between major blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout follows a moderately dense promotional rhythm: text blocks are stacked with 8–16px between related lines and 24–32px between major sections. The story content area is text-heavy but constrained by a narrow column to keep lines readable. The bookmarking section is visually denser and more high-contrast (bold CTA text, bright button, tight stacking) to create urgency, while the surrounding photographic background provides visual breathing room.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the bold story title at the top center of the panel, then down to the concise stats summary that quickly frames the success. From there, the reader flows into the full story content. When they reach the bottom of the narrative, their attention is caught by a bold, high-contrast bookmark CTA line, followed by a short instruction, then the email input and bright full-width bookmark button. Any feedback message appears immediately around this form, and the section anchors the bottom of the panel as the final conversion action.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a dramatic, full-bleed beach photograph that fills the entire viewport, with a slightly dimmed overlay to ensure legibility. Centered in the middle of the screen is a tall, dark translucent panel with soft edges and subtle drop shadow, visually similar to a modal card. This panel contains a single, vertically stacked column of content. At the top, a large, bold story title in high-contrast typography sets the tone, with a compact stats strip beneath it summarizing the key outcomes of the story. Below that, the full story text flows down the panel in a comfortably narrow column, followed by a small section of associated links for further exploration. As the reader scrolls toward the bottom of the panel, the storytelling transitions into a conversion-focused area: a bold bookmark headline and one-line benefit statement, followed by a short instruction, then a stacked email input and a bright, full-width bookmark button that stands out in yellow or red against the dark background. Any success or error messages appear tightly associated with this form. Loader and fallback states temporarily replace or overlay this central panel content but maintain the same centered, modal-like presence. The overall effect is a focused, promotional reading and conversion experience, framed by lifestyle imagery and driven by a strong top-to-bottom funnel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader appears as a semi-opaque dark overlay that covers the entire central panel and softly extends to the edges of the viewport, dimming both the panel and the beach background. At the center of the viewport, aligned with the center of the panel, a compact loading indicator and short status line appear, occupying roughly 20–25% of the panel’s width. This overlay sits above all other components, preventing interaction with the story content and bookmark form while data is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay relative to the viewport and central panel; centered both horizontally and vertically, covering all underlying components until loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The story header is positioned at the very top inside the dark panel, starting immediately after a 24–32px top padding. It spans almost the full width of the panel, leaving about 24–32px padding on the left and right. The main story title is a large, high-contrast H1 (occupying roughly 2–3 lines), centered or slightly left-aligned, and takes up about the top 15–20% of the panel’s content area. If there is a subtitle or byline, it appears directly beneath the title with 8–12px vertical spacing, in smaller, lighter text. This block visually anchors the page and sets the tone for all subsequent content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, participating in normal document flow at the top of the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stats summary sits directly below the story header, separated by roughly 12–16px of vertical space. It spans the same content width as the header, aligned to the left edge of the text. The stats appear as a compact band occupying about one line to one-and-a-half lines in height, using a slightly brighter accent color (e.g., yellow or teal) for key numbers. On larger screens, individual stats may be arranged in a shallow horizontal row, but they stay visually grouped as a single strip under the header. This section occupies about 8–10% of the panel’s content height and creates a quick-glance snapshot before the reader dives into the full story.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in normal vertical flow, tightly coupled to the header as its immediate follower.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The full story content sits directly beneath the stats summary with a more generous 24px vertical gap, marking the transition from teaser information into the main narrative. It occupies the full text width inside the panel (minus 24–32px side padding) and extends vertically as long as needed, forming the dominant middle section of the page. Paragraphs are stacked with 8–12px spacing, while section breaks or subheadings get 16–24px above them, maintaining the moderately dense promotion-style rhythm. This component is expected to consume the majority of the vertical space—roughly 45–55% of the panel content area before the user reaches the bookmarking section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable along with the rest of the panel; it grows in height based on content and pushes subsequent sections downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The associated links block appears immediately after the last paragraph of the story content, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space to denote it as a new, supporting section. It is narrower in perceived visual weight, occupying the same width but only about 10–15% of the panel’s content height. A small heading or label sits at the top of this block, with related links listed beneath in a single vertical column with 8–12px between items. The styling is lighter and less saturated than the primary CTA elements, keeping this section clearly secondary and not competing with the bookmark funnel below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, placed in the normal vertical flow between the main story content and the bookmark CTA section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark CTA block begins the conversion-focused lower section of the panel and is positioned directly below the associated links, with a clear 24–32px vertical gap to mark a new intent. It spans the full text width and occupies roughly 10–15% of the panel content height. At the top of this block is a bold, high-contrast statement such as “Bookmark this success story to revisit later” in large, white or gradient-enhanced text. Directly underneath, with about 8–12px spacing, a shorter supporting line communicates the benefit (e.g., teal accent text reinforcing the convenience or outcome). This area visually echoes the strong promotional style of the original hero headline and acts as the attention-grabbing entry point into the bookmark form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the top of the bottom funnel section; it pushes the form and messages downward as it grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark form sits immediately below the bookmark CTA block, separated by 12–16px to maintain tight, conversion-focused density. It is centered within the panel and spans nearly the full usable width, leaving 24–32px padding on each side. The email input appears first, with a label or placeholder aligned left and the input field width filling about 100% of the content column. Vertically, the input field has 12–16px internal padding and about 8–12px of space below it. Below the input, a bright, full-width button—typically in a saturated yellow or red—extends edge-to-edge within the text column, with 12–16px vertical padding inside and slightly larger top margin (12–16px) to emphasize it. Together, the input and button block occupy around 15–20% of the panel content height and form the visual and functional center of the conversion area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked within the lower portion of the panel; elements expand vertically but remain constrained to a single column. On small screens, the button remains full-width and pinned under the input, preserving the vertical funnel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: System feedback messages appear in close proximity to the bookmark form to make the cause-and-effect relationship obvious. By default, this component sits directly beneath the bookmark button, with a small 8–12px vertical gap. It spans the same width as the form and supports one or two lines of text. Success messages use a positive accent (e.g., teal or green on the dark panel) with a compact background pill or subtle border, while error messages use a more urgent accent (e.g., red). The messages may expand vertically to accommodate multiple lines but remain visually compact so as not to overpower the CTA, occupying roughly 5–8% of the panel content height when present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appearing and disappearing in place under the form; when shown, it pushes any following content (if present) downward. In states where global issues must be shown (e.g., story loading failure), this component can be reused higher in the panel, but its primary placement is directly under the bookmark form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the story is missing or invalid, the normal stack of header, stats, content, links, and bookmark section is replaced by a simplified fallback block centered inside the same dark translucent panel. The panel remains centered over the beach background as usual. Inside, the fallback message occupies the central 40–50% of the panel’s height, aligned both horizontally and vertically. A bold headline stating that the story cannot be found appears at the top of this block, followed by 8–12px of space, then a brief explanatory line and possibly a subtle action (e.g., a link to go back). This message block spans about 60–70% of the panel width, with generous side padding to maintain readability, and sits alone in the panel to remove distractions and emphasize the state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central panel but conditionally rendered: it replaces the regular content stack when triggered. It remains vertically centered if the panel is shorter than the viewport and scrolls with the panel if additional information is included.

name: Application
title: Application
description: Page for collecting and submitting work-from-home program applications using a dynamic form.

core_features: Load dynamic application form options (availability, areas of interest, budget range) via getApplicationFormOptions when the page initializes or reloads.
Render an application form that collects availability, monthly budget, preferred start date, areas of interest, full name, email, phone, and biggest goal/intent text.
Enforce required fields, numeric constraints for monthly budget based on getApplicationFormOptions, and a required Terms & Conditions agreement checkbox.
Allow URL parameters to prefill form fields (e.g., availability, areasOfInterest, email) and control the page state (e.g., editing vs. submitted confirmation).
Submit the completed application to submitApplication and handle the response to show success, failure, or retry states without leaving the page.
Display a confirmation and next-steps message based on the submitApplication response while keeping the user on application.html.
user_workflows: Initial visit and form completion: 1) User navigates to application.html from the site. 2) The page reads URL parameters to determine initial field values and mode (e.g., editing or submitted). 3) The page calls getApplicationFormOptions and populates availability and areas-of-interest inputs and budget constraints. 4) User fills in all required fields (availability, monthly budget, preferred start date, areas of interest, full name, email, phone, biggest goal) and checks the Terms & Conditions checkbox. 5) User clicks the form submit control. 6) Client-side validation runs; if valid, submitApplication is called with current form values. 7) On a successful response, the form switches to a confirmation state, the success message from submitApplication is displayed, and the URL is updated to indicate status=submitted and reference the created Application.

Returning from another page with prefilled preferences: 1) User clicks an "Apply" link elsewhere on the site that navigates to application.html with query parameters such as availability, areasOfInterest, and email. 2) application.html parses URL parameters and sets initial form field values accordingly. 3) The page calls getApplicationFormOptions and merges dynamic option data (e.g., option labels and ranges) with any prefilled values from the URL. 4) User reviews and adjusts prefilled values. 5) User submits the form, which triggers submitApplication and then displays a confirmation or error state as appropriate.

Viewing the confirmation state after submission: 1) After a successful submitApplication call, application.html updates the URL to include a status parameter (e.g., status=submitted) and an identifier for the created Application. 2) If the user reloads the page or returns to the same URL, the page reads the status parameter. 3) When status indicates a submitted state, the page initializes directly in confirmation mode (no new submitApplication call is made). 4) The user sees a non-editable confirmation panel that uses submitApplication.message (if available from navigation state or a prior call) or a generic confirmation state and optional next steps instructions.
interactions: On page load, the page triggers a getApplicationFormOptions call and updates form controls when the data is loaded.
User selects an availability option from dynamically loaded choices; the selection updates the internal form state and the URL parameter for availability.
User selects one or more areas of interest from dynamically loaded multi-select options; selections update internal state and the areasOfInterest-related URL parameters.
User enters a numeric monthly budget; client-side validation ensures the value is within the minMonthlyBudget and maxMonthlyBudget range from getApplicationFormOptions.
User chooses a preferred start date using a date input control; the selected date is stored in state and optionally reflected as a URL parameter.

User types their full name, email, phone, and biggest goal text in corresponding inputs; each onChange event updates the internal form state and, for selected fields (such as email), synchronizes values to URL parameters.
User checks the Terms & Conditions agreement checkbox; the checkbox state determines whether form submission is allowed and can be synchronized to a URL parameter only as a non-sensitive flag.
User clicks the "Submit Application" control; onSubmit triggers client-side validation, and if validation passes, the page calls submitApplication with current form values.

If submitApplication returns success, the page disables the editable form inputs, displays a confirmation panel using the returned message and application data, and updates the URL status parameter to represent a submitted state.
If submitApplication returns a non-success response or a network error occurs, the page displays an error banner, keeps the form editable, and allows the user to modify fields and resubmit.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL query parameters and in-memory component state, without using localStorage. On initial load and on each URL change, application.html parses the querystring to derive: (1) mode: a status parameter determines the high-level mode, such as editing (no or non-submitted status) vs. submitted (status value indicating that an application has already been submitted). In submitted mode the page displays a confirmation view and disables the main form. In editing mode the form is enabled and ready to accept input. (2) initial field values: query parameters corresponding to form fields (e.g., availability, monthlyBudget, preferredStartDate, areasOfInterest, fullName, email, phone, biggestGoalText) are used to pre-populate the form's internal state. For multi-select areasOfInterest, multiple query parameters or a delimited value can be parsed to pre-select the relevant options. On page mount, getApplicationFormOptions is always called in editing mode; when its response arrives, the availabilityOptions and areasOfInterestOptions arrays and budget range values are bound to the form controls. If a query parameter refers to an option that is not present in the current getApplicationFormOptions response, that value is ignored to avoid invalid selections. As the user edits key fields (such as availability, areasOfInterest, and email), the URL is updated to reflect the latest values, enabling deep linking and preserving partial progress on reload without persisting to localStorage. On a successful submitApplication call, the handler updates the URL to include a status parameter indicating a submitted state and an application identifier parameter derived from the returned Application.id, while omitting sensitive personal data from the URL. Subsequent loads of the same URL detect this status and immediately present the confirmation view instead of an editable form, without re-submitting the application.

components.functionality: Initializes the Application page by parsing URL parameters, determining whether the page is in editing or submitted mode, and requesting dynamic form options via getApplicationFormOptions when in editing mode.

Synchronizes selected form field values and the overall page mode with URL query parameters so that the user's in-progress application is partially encoded in the URL and the page can restore state on reload.

Renders and manages the main application form fields (availability, monthly budget, preferred start date, areas of interest, full name, email, phone, biggest goal) and the Terms & Conditions agreement checkbox, enabling or disabling fields based on the current page mode and validation state.
Displays the list of availability options returned by getApplicationFormOptions and allows the user to select one, updating the Application.availability field and reflecting the selection in the URL.
Displays the list of areas of interest returned by getApplicationFormOptions as a multi-select control; selections update Application.areas_of_interest and synchronized URL parameters.
Captures the applicant's monthly budget value, validates it against the minMonthlyBudget and maxMonthlyBudget values from getApplicationFormOptions, and updates Application.monthly_budget when valid.

Collects the preferred start date as a date/time value, ensures it conforms to the expected datetime format for Application.preferred_start_date, and optionally serializes it to and from a URL parameter.

Manages the full name, email, and phone inputs, keeping them synchronized with the Application.full_name, Application.email, and Application.phone fields and, for selected fields like email, reflecting values in URL parameters for prefill support.
Captures the applicant’s biggest goal or intent as free text, mapping it to Application.biggest_goal_text and enforcing the required status on submission.
Represents the mandatory Terms & Conditions agreement; its checked state updates Application.accepted_terms and is used by validation logic to allow or block submission.

Aggregates and displays validation errors for required fields and invalid values (e.g., budget outside the dynamic range, missing terms acceptance), based on the current Application data and constraints from getApplicationFormOptions.

Coordinates final validation of the Application form data and calls submitApplication with the mapped parameters when the user submits; it manages loading, success, and error states and updates the Application.status and URL parameters based on the submitApplication response.

Displays the result of the submitApplication call, including success or error messages, and shows any message content returned by submitApplication.message; in submitted mode it also reflects the current Application.status.

Shows a confirmation and next-steps view after the application has been successfully submitted; it uses Application.status, submitApplication.message, and the presence of a submitted state in the URL to render a non-editable summary of the submission state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, vertically stacked column of content inside a dark, translucent central panel that sits over a full-bleed lifestyle hero background (e.g., beach image). All user-facing components (status banner, form, validation messages, confirmation view) are arranged in a top-to-bottom funnel within this panel. Non-visual components (data loader, URL state manager, submit controller) do not occupy layout space. Inside the panel, the top zone is reserved for status and high-level instructions, the middle zone for the application fields, and the bottom zone for consent + CTA, mirroring the mockup’s linear, conversion-focused stack.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, vertically stacked column of content inside a dark, translucent central panel that sits over a full-bleed lifestyle hero background (e.g., beach image). All user-facing components (status banner, form, validation messages, confirmation view) are arranged in a top-to-bottom funnel within this panel. Non-visual components (data loader, URL state manager, submit controller) do not occupy layout space. Inside the panel, the top zone is reserved for status and high-level instructions, the middle zone for the application fields, and the bottom zone for consent + CTA, mirroring the mockup’s linear, conversion-focused stack.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Horizontally, the central panel (the application form container) occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop, with a max-width around 560–640px, centered, with visible background on both sides. On smaller screens it collapses toward 100% minus safe margins. Vertically, approximately the upper 25–30% of the panel is dedicated to status messaging and introductory text (within the form component), the middle 50–55% to the main field groups (availability, interest, budget, dates, contact, goals), and the lower 15–20% to the terms checkbox, submit action, and any final reassurance or feedback. The confirmation panel later reuses this same central area. Internal padding follows a 24–32px rhythm on all sides, with 16–24px between major component blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately dense and promotional, echoing direct-response style. Within the panel, fields and copy are stacked with 12–16px vertical spacing between related items (labels and fields) and 24–32px between major groups (status area, selector cluster, contact info, goals, consent + submit). There is enough breathing room to keep the form scannable, but not so much that it feels leisurely; the compact vertical stacking keeps the conversion-focused, urgent feel from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is a strong, top-to-bottom funnel focused on completion. The eye first hits the top of the panel where the submission status banner (if present) and the form’s heading/instructions sit, supported by high-contrast backgrounds and bold type. From there, attention moves down through the field groups in logical order: availability and interest selection (framing fit), then budget and start date (commitment), then contact info and goals (qualification). The validation errors list, when visible, appears high in the stack so it’s immediately noticed, but visually subordinate to the title. Finally, the terms checkbox and the bold, full-width CTA button form the visual climax at the bottom, with any success/confirmation view replacing the form entirely while keeping its prominent central position.

layout.overall_description: The page opens on a dramatic, full-bleed lifestyle photograph that fills the entire viewport, with saturated colors and a subtle gradient overlay that darkens toward the center. Floating in the middle is a tall, dark, translucent panel that visually resembles a modal card: softly rounded corners, strong drop shadow, and vivid accent edges that echo the red, yellow, and cyan palette. This panel is vertically biased slightly above center, so there’s a bit more breathing room below than above, and it occupies about 45% of the viewport width on desktop, never exceeding roughly 600px. Inside this panel, content flows in a straight vertical column: at the very top, an optional status banner spans the full width, followed by the application form’s heading and a brief instruction line. Below this, any validation errors appear in a highlighted strip, and then the main form fields stack in logical sections with modest spacing—availability and interest selectors first, then budget and start date, followed by contact details and a goal textarea. Toward the bottom, the terms consent checkbox hugs the left edge of the column, and just beneath it the full-width, high-contrast submit button anchors the layout as the brightest focal point. Upon successful submission, the entire form area is replaced by a confirmation panel that uses the same central card but with more open spacing and a strong success message. Throughout, the single-column, center-focused panel structure keeps the eye moving top-to-bottom, while the expansive, colorful background image frames the experience and reinforces the promotional, high-impact feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual data-loader that initializes the application state before any UI appears. It does not render any visible elements or occupy space within the central panel or the background. From a layout perspective, it operates entirely behind the scenes, completing its work before the central panel and form are displayed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it does not participate in the DOM layout or take up any width or height on the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This URL state manager is an invisible behavioral layer that syncs the form state with the URL. It has no visual representation, no box, and no reserved space in or around the central panel; it simply augments the behavior of the visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it is effectively layered outside the visual layout with no spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application form is the main visual and spatial anchor inside the dark translucent central panel. It defines the panel itself: a vertically oriented card centered horizontally in the viewport, occupying about 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width ~600px) and dropping to nearly full width on small screens with 16–24px horizontal margins. Vertically, the form content fills most of the panel from just below its top edge to just above the bottom edge, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Within this form container, all field groups and status indicators are stacked in a single column, aligned left, and constrained to the same internal width. The top portion of the form (roughly the upper quarter to third of the panel’s internal height) is reserved for the heading copy and instructions (part of this component’s content), followed by the validation-errors-list and then the sequence of field subcomponents. The bottom 15–20% of the form is reserved for the terms consent checkbox and the full-width CTA button, keeping the most important action anchored visually at the lower center of the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a central, scrollable card on smaller screens and a vertically centered panel on larger screens. It is not fixed; if the content exceeds viewport height, the page scrolls while the panel stays centered horizontally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the application form, the availability selector appears near the top of the main field area, immediately after any introductory heading/description text and, if present, the validation errors list. It spans the full usable width of the form’s inner column (100% of the panel width minus padding), aligned to the left edge. A label and any helper text sit directly above the input control with about 8–12px vertical spacing. This selector occupies a vertical slice of roughly 10–12% of the form’s internal height, leaving 16–24px of space below it before the next field group, maintaining the linear, stacked rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form’s single column; it scrolls naturally with the rest of the form content and maintains its position relative to neighboring fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The areas-of-interest multi-select is placed directly beneath the availability selector, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space. It also spans the full internal width of the form column. The label sits left-aligned at the top of this block, with any helper text or subheading directly below it, followed by the multi-select options. If presented as chips or checkboxes, they wrap within the same column but stay visually grouped as a single block. This section occupies a slightly larger vertical area than a single input—around 12–15% of the panel’s internal height—maintaining the dense, promotional feel without appearing crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the central column; its height can grow with content but it always stays stacked immediately after the availability selector and before the budget input.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget input sits directly below the areas-of-interest selector, separated by approximately 16–24px. It is a single, full-width numeric field with its label aligned to the left margin of the column. The input itself uses generous horizontal padding (around 16–20px inside the field) and moderate vertical padding (12–14px), echoing the mockup’s input styling. This block takes roughly 8–10% of the form’s internal height, visually balancing with the start-date picker that follows. A small gap of 16–20px is maintained below it to preserve the clear step-by-step scan pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static field within the vertical stack; expands only minimally with error messages or helper text while maintaining its relative position in the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The start-date picker is positioned directly underneath the budget input, with about 16–20px of vertical separation. It spans the same full internal width of the panel and aligns to the same left column edge, reinforcing the single-column rhythm. The label appears above the date input or calendar trigger, and the control itself uses similar vertical height and padding as the budget input so the two look balanced as a pair. This section occupies roughly 8–10% of the form’s internal height, forming the final part of the ‘commitment’ cluster before moving into contact info.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; scrolls with the rest of the content and maintains consistent spacing to both the budget input above and the contact-info-fields below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info fields group appears directly below the start-date picker, separated by 24–32px to signal a new logical section. This block contains several individual fields (e.g., name, email, phone), all stacked vertically within the same central column and aligned left. Each field spans the full internal width, with 8–12px between labels and inputs and 8–12px between each consecutive field, resulting in a compact but readable group. Overall, this group occupies a noticeable portion of the panel—around 15–20% of the internal height—forming a dense middle section of the form prior to the goal textarea. There is 24px of vertical space below this group before the next component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grouped block that scrolls with the form. It behaves as a cohesive section, maintaining uniform spacing between its internal fields and its neighboring sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The goal textarea is placed directly under the contact-info fields group with a slightly larger gap of about 24px, helping it read as a distinct, more narrative section. It spans the full width of the inner column and is taller than the other inputs, taking up roughly 12–15% of the form’s internal height to allow for multiple lines of text. The label and any short prompt sit above the textarea with 8–12px spacing. This block fills much of the remaining middle-lower portion of the panel, with around 24–32px of space beneath it leading into the terms consent and CTA cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical stack; its height is fixed or minimally resizable but always anchored directly above the terms-consent-checkbox and below the contact info group.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms consent checkbox occupies the beginning of the conversion cluster near the bottom of the form. It is positioned directly beneath the goal textarea, separated by 24–32px, and left-aligned with the rest of the column. The checkbox icon appears on the far left with the terms text extending to the right, potentially wrapping onto multiple lines but staying within the same width as the inputs above. This section takes up a relatively shallow vertical area (about 6–8% of the internal height), leaving a clear visual path to the CTA button immediately below, which reinforces the sense that consent is the final step before submission.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored near the bottom of the form; it scrolls normally and remains directly above the submit action regardless of content length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation errors list appears near the top of the form content inside the central panel, directly below the form’s heading/instruction line and above the first field group (availability-selector). It spans the full width of the inner column and is presented as a compact vertical list or a bold bar with stacked items, using a high-contrast treatment (e.g., red accent) to attract attention while still being subordinate to the top-level heading. Vertical padding within this block is around 12–16px, with 8–12px between individual error messages if listed. There is about 16–20px of space between the bottom of this block and the availability selector, keeping the flow continuous but visually clear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally and pushes subsequent form content downward when present. It scrolls with the panel content and does not remain fixed; when there are no errors, the space collapses entirely.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit controller is an invisible behavioral component that manages submission logic, loading states, and transitions between the form and the confirmation panel. It does not render any visible UI or occupy any space within the central panel or the page layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; operates purely as a controller without affecting layout dimensions or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission status banner occupies a prominent horizontal strip at the very top of the content inside the central panel, above the form’s heading and all other fields. It spans the full width of the panel’s inner area and is relatively shallow, using strong accent colors (e.g., teal or yellow) to indicate progress or success while contrasting with the darker panel background. Vertical padding inside the banner is around 12–16px, giving it enough presence to be immediately noticed. When present, it takes up roughly 6–8% of the panel’s internal height and pushes the rest of the form content downward so the user sees it first upon arrival or after submission.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally and is anchored to the top inside the central panel; it scrolls with the panel content rather than being fixed to the viewport. When not needed, the banner is completely absent and the form’s heading moves up to occupy its space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation panel takes over the same central card space as the application form after a successful submission. It is positioned in the exact center of the viewport, inheriting the same width (around 40–50% of the viewport on desktop, with a max-width near 600px) and the same dark translucent background, shadow, and padding as the form panel. Within this panel, content is vertically centered slightly above the midline to mirror the hero layout: a bold confirmation headline sits at the top, followed by one or two lines of supporting copy and any key details or next steps, all aligned to the same single column. Spacing between lines is generous—24–32px between major blocks—creating a more open, celebratory feel compared to the denser form state. The overall height of the confirmation content is shorter than the form, occupying perhaps 50–60% of the panel’s height, allowing the photographic background to frame the success message prominently.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the application form inside the same central panel region: when active, it replaces the form content entirely. It remains centered in the viewport and scrolls only if its content exceeds the viewport height, which is unlikely under normal circumstances.

name: Newsletter Preferences
title: Newsletter Preferences
description: Configure newsletter frequency, topics, delivery time, content types, and submit your email subscription preferences.

core_features: Load available newsletter preference options (frequencies, topics, best time of day, content types) using getNewsletterPreferenceOptions and bind them to the form controls.
Allow users to select a newsletter frequency from the dynamically loaded frequency options.
Provide a selectable list of topics from the dynamically loaded topic options, including a control to clear all topic selections at once and then re-select specific topics.
Allow users to choose the best time to receive emails from the dynamically loaded best-time-of-day options.
Allow users to select one or more content types from the dynamically loaded content type options.
Collect and validate required contact fields (email, and optionally first name) together with the selected preferences.
Submit the newsletter subscription and preferences using saveNewsletterSubscription and handle the response (success or error) while keeping the user on the same page.
Reflect form state and result status in the URL via parameters so the page can be reloaded or shared with state (e.g., preselected options or success indication) without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow: First-time visitor configures preferences and subscribes
1. User navigates to newsletter.html with no query parameters.
2. The page controller component initializes and calls getNewsletterPreferenceOptions.
3. While loading, inputs are disabled or not rendered until options are available.
4. Once options are loaded, the form component renders with:
   - Frequency select populated from frequencyOptions.
   - Topics multi-select populated from topicOptions.
   - Best time select populated from bestTimeOptions.
   - Content types multi-select populated from contentTypeOptions.
5. The user enters their email and, optionally, first name.
6. The user selects a frequency, one or more topics, a best time to receive emails, and one or more content types.
7. The user clicks the form submit button.
8. The form validates that:
   - Email is present and in valid format.
   - Frequency is selected.
   - At least one topic is selected.
   - A best time to receive is selected.
   - At least one content type is selected.
9. On passing validation, the form calls saveNewsletterSubscription with the current field values.
10. While the request is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a loading state is shown.
11. If saveNewsletterSubscription returns success = true, the controller:
    - Stores the returned subscription object in page state.
    - Switches the UI into a success state showing confirmation details.
    - Updates the URL query string to include a success indicator and the email (e.g., success=true&email=<encoded>) without reloading the page.
12. If saveNewsletterSubscription returns success = false or a transport error occurs, an error message is shown and the form remains visible for correction or retry without changing the URL success indicator.

Workflow: Visitor arrives via a pre-configured link (pre-filling form fields)
1. User opens newsletter.html with one or more query parameters such as:
   - email
   - firstName
   - frequency
   - topics (comma-separated values)
   - bestTime
   - contentTypes (comma-separated values)
2. The page controller parses the URL parameters but does not attempt to validate them until getNewsletterPreferenceOptions has returned.
3. The page calls getNewsletterPreferenceOptions and waits for the options.
4. After options are loaded, the controller:
   - If an email parameter exists, pre-fills the email input.
   - If a firstName parameter exists, pre-fills the first name input.
   - If a frequency parameter matches one of the loaded frequencyOptions values, pre-selects that option.
   - If a bestTime parameter matches one of the loaded bestTimeOptions values, pre-selects that option.
   - If topics parameters are present, splits them and pre-selects those items that match any topicOptions values, ignoring invalid ones.
   - If contentTypes parameters are present, splits them and pre-selects those items that match any contentTypeOptions values, ignoring invalid ones.
5. The user can adjust any pre-filled choice: change frequency, add/remove topics, change best time, change content types, or update their email/first name.
6. The user submits the form, triggering the same validation and saveNewsletterSubscription flow as described for first-time visitors.
7. On success, the URL query parameters are updated to reflect the actual saved preferences (e.g., frequency, topics, bestTime, contentTypes, email) plus success=true, allowing the user to refresh or share the link with their chosen configuration.

Workflow: User clears and re-selects specific topics
1. User is on the preferences form with one or more topics already selected (either manually, from URL prefill, or from a prior change).
2. The user clicks the 'Clear all topics' control in the topics selector component.
3. The topics selector component sets the topics selection state to an empty array and updates the bound topics field in the main form state.
4. The topics selector re-renders with no topics selected.
5. The user then clicks individual topic options to re-add them to the selection; each click toggles the corresponding topic value in the form's topics array.
6. When the user submits the form, validation ensures that at least one topic is selected; if none are selected, an inline validation error is shown and saveNewsletterSubscription is not called.

Workflow: Returning user following a success state link
1. User visits a URL such as newsletter.html?success=true&email=<encoded>&frequency=<value>&topics=<values>&bestTime=<value>&contentTypes=<values> (created by a previous successful submission).
2. The page controller parses the URL parameters, including success=true.
3. The page calls getNewsletterPreferenceOptions and waits for results.
4. After options are loaded, the controller pre-fills the form fields from the URL parameters as in the pre-configured link workflow.
5. Since success=true is present, the controller initializes the page in a success-visible state while still allowing access to the form for adjustments.
6. The success component displays a non-blocking confirmation message, using the current in-memory subscription-like state derived from URL plus any changes the user makes.
7. If the user changes any preferences and resubmits, saveNewsletterSubscription is called again. On successful response, the success message is updated to reflect the new subscription data, and the URL is updated again to the new preference values.
interactions: On page load, the controller component triggers an onInit event that calls getNewsletterPreferenceOptions, then binds the returned frequencyOptions, topicOptions, bestTimeOptions, and contentTypeOptions as data sources for the relevant form inputs.
When the user changes the frequency select input, an onChange event updates the form state frequency field to match the selected option's value.
When the user selects or deselects a topic, an onChange event in the topics selector toggles the corresponding topic value in the form state topics array.
When the user clicks the 'Clear all topics' control, an onClick event sets the topics array in form state to an empty array and re-renders the topics inputs with no selections.
When the user changes the best time select input, an onChange event updates the bestTimeToReceive field in the form state.
When the user selects or deselects a content type, an onChange event toggles the corresponding content type value in the form state contentTypes array.
When the user types into the email input, an onChange event updates the email field in the form state and clears any existing email-specific validation error.
When the user types into the first-name input, an onChange event updates the firstName field in the form state.

When the user submits the form, an onSubmit event:
  - Prevents default browser submission.
  - Runs client-side validation on email, frequency, topics, bestTimeToReceive, and contentTypes.
  - If validation passes, calls saveNewsletterSubscription with the current form state values.
  - If validation fails, sets validation error messages in state and prevents API calls.

When saveNewsletterSubscription resolves with success = true, an onSuccess handler:
  - Stores subscription (the returned NewsletterSubscription) in state.
  - Triggers display of the success component.
  - Calls the URL state sync component to update query parameters to include success=true and reflect the saved preference values without reloading.

When saveNewsletterSubscription resolves with success = false or fails, an onError handler sets an error message in state, ensures the success component is hidden or updated to indicate failure, and keeps the form enabled for retry.

When the URL changes due to internal state sync (e.g., history.pushState or history.replaceState), an onURLChange handler in the controller can re-derive page state if needed (for example, when the user navigates back/forward), ensuring the form fields and success indicator match the current URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters are the primary external mechanism for persisting and restoring page state across reloads and navigation, without using localStorage.

Supported URL parameters (all optional):
- email: Pre-fills the email field. On successful submission, this is updated to the confirmed subscription email.
- firstName: Pre-fills the first-name field. On successful submission, this is updated to the confirmed subscription first_name (if provided).
- frequency: Pre-selects the newsletter frequency, if it matches a value returned in frequencyOptions. On success, updated to the saved frequency value.
- topics: A comma-separated list of topic values. After getNewsletterPreferenceOptions returns, each value is matched against topicOptions; matches are selected, invalid values are ignored. On success, this is updated to reflect the topics saved in subscription.topics.
- bestTime: Pre-selects the best time to receive emails if it matches a value returned in bestTimeOptions. On success, updated to subscription.best_time_to_receive.
- contentTypes: A comma-separated list of content type values. Matched against contentTypeOptions; matches are selected, invalid values are ignored. On success, updated to subscription.content_types.
- success: A boolean-like flag (e.g., 'true' or '1') used to indicate that the page should initialize with the success component visible. This is set to a truthy value after a successful form submission and cleared or left absent when showing the form without a confirmed success.

State resolution rules:
1. On initial load, the controller parses URL parameters but does not immediately apply them to form state until getNewsletterPreferenceOptions has resolved, to ensure that all referenced option values can be validated against actual options.
2. After getNewsletterPreferenceOptions returns, the controller builds the initial in-memory form state in this precedence order for each field:
   - If the field has a value already in memory (e.g., when navigating back/forward within the same session), keep it.
   - Else, if a corresponding URL parameter is present and its value matches one of the loaded options (for select/multi-select fields), use it.
   - Else, for selectable fields, default to an unselected state or any appropriate neutral initial state determined by page requirements (e.g., no automatic selection), relying solely on runtime logic and not on hardcoded option values.
3. When the user makes changes in the form, the in-memory state is updated but URL parameters are not immediately changed to avoid excessive history entries; the URL is only updated after a successful save, or based on explicit controller logic (e.g., using replaceState on key changes if desired).
4. After a successful save, the controller uses the returned subscription object to set the source of truth for saved values and updates the URL parameters to match the subscription fields. This ensures that refreshing the page reconstructs the saved preferences from the URL.
5. If success is present in the URL on page load and evaluates to true, the success component is shown by default once the initial state is built; if it is absent or evaluates to false, the success component remains hidden until a successful save occurs.
6. Navigation actions like browser back/forward trigger onURLChange in the controller, which re-parses the URL, re-applies the above resolution rules, and updates the form and success states accordingly.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading of preference options, parsing and applying URL state, managing global form state, orchestrating calls to getNewsletterPreferenceOptions and saveNewsletterSubscription, and controlling the visibility of success and error states.

On initialization, calls getNewsletterPreferenceOptions with no parameters, manages loading and error status for this call, and passes frequencyOptions, topicOptions, bestTimeOptions, and contentTypeOptions to the page controller and form components.

Renders the newsletter preferences form fields (email, first name, frequency select, topics multi-select, best-time select, content-types multi-select), binds them to in-memory state, performs client-side validation, and triggers saveNewsletterSubscription on submission.

Provides a dedicated multi-select control for newsletter topics, rendering options from topicOptions, enabling selection/deselection of multiple topics, and exposing a 'Clear all topics' action that sets the topics selection to an empty array.
Manages selection of one or more content types from contentTypeOptions, updating the form's contentTypes array as items are toggled.
Displays a single-select control for frequency using frequencyOptions returned from getNewsletterPreferenceOptions and updates the form's frequency field when changed.
Displays a single-select control for best time to receive emails using bestTimeOptions and updates the form's bestTimeToReceive field when changed.

Receives validated form state (email, firstName, frequency, topics, bestTimeToReceive, contentTypes) from the form component, calls saveNewsletterSubscription, and returns the response to the controller for success or error handling.

Displays the success confirmation once a subscription has been successfully saved. Uses the NewsletterSubscription object to show user-specific details such as the confirmed email and selected preferences, and remains consistent with the URL success parameter.
Displays validation errors and API error messages when saveNewsletterSubscription fails or when client-side validation fails prior to the API call.

Encodes and decodes form and success state into URL query parameters. On load, parses parameters and passes them to the controller; on successful save, updates or replaces the current URL with parameters matching the saved NewsletterSubscription values and the success flag.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: All content sits inside a single, tall, vertically oriented central panel that floats over a full-bleed lifestyle background (beach photo). The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, single-column stack: at the top of the panel is the page title and short explanatory copy; below that is a compact cluster of preference controls (topics, content types, frequency, best time); at the bottom sit the primary Save Preferences button and any status messages. There are no sidebars or multi-column splits; all interactive elements are stacked and center-aligned within the panel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All content sits inside a single, tall, vertically oriented central panel that floats over a full-bleed lifestyle background (beach photo). The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, single-column stack: at the top of the panel is the page title and short explanatory copy; below that is a compact cluster of preference controls (topics, content types, frequency, best time); at the bottom sit the primary Save Preferences button and any status messages. There are no sidebars or multi-column splits; all interactive elements are stacked and center-aligned within the panel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width, centered horizontally with generous background visible on both sides, and extends vertically to feel dominant but not full-height (approx. 70–80% of viewport height on desktop). Within the panel, the top ~60–65% is dedicated to messaging and form context (headline, subcopy, short instruction above the controls), and the lower ~35–40% to the actual configuration controls and the Save Preferences CTA plus success/error messages. Padding inside the panel uses 24–32px on left/right and ~24px top/bottom, with consistent 8–16px vertical spacing between related fields and 24–32px between major sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content inside the panel is moderately dense and conversion-focused: the headline, supporting text, and controls sit relatively close together to emphasize action, separated by 8–16px gaps. Larger 24–32px gaps are reserved for transitions between logical sections (intro messaging → controls group → CTA/status area). Outside the panel, the large photographic background is intentionally free of other UI to provide breathing room and contrast, making the central content feel intense and focused.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the intense, high-contrast headline at the top of the panel, then moves directly down through a short supporting line and a bold simplification line that explains what these preferences achieve. The eye is then guided down to a concise instruction line above the form controls, into the stacked selectors (topics, content types, frequency, best time), and finally to the bright, full-width Save Preferences button. After submission, success or error messages appear just above or below the button, keeping the focus at the bottom of the funnel. The entire flow is a top-to-bottom funnel anchored at the center of the screen, echoing the original marketing-style mockup.

layout.overall_description: The page loads into a full-bleed beach photograph that fills the entire viewport, with a dark, semi-translucent vertical panel centered both horizontally and vertically. This panel feels like a tall, modal-style card hovering over the image, with a subtle glow and strong contrast against the background. Inside the panel, a bold, gradient headline at the very top announces the newsletter preferences, followed closely by a short, punchy line explaining that you’re tailoring your work-from-home updates to fit your schedule. A slightly larger gap leads into a clear instruction line introducing the preference controls. Directly beneath, a single-column stack of dropdowns and multi-select chips lets you choose topics and content types, followed by frequency and best time selectors, all spanning nearly the full width of the panel with comfortable padding. At the bottom of this stack, a bright, full-width Save Preferences button anchors the experience. Success or error messages appear inline near the button area, reinforcing the outcome without breaking the vertical flow. The center panel occupies about half the screen width, with the beach image visible on the left and right, creating a dramatic, focused composition that mirrors a high-impact promotional hero while housing a practical configuration form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual controller defines the full-page canvas. It stretches to cover 100% of the viewport width and height and applies the full-bleed beach photo as the fixed background layer. Over this background, it centers a single main content panel: on desktop, the panel is horizontally centered and vertically aligned slightly above the exact middle (so the top has a bit more breathing room), with a max-width of about 480–640px (roughly 40–50% of viewport width). On smaller screens, the controller lets the panel expand up to 100% width minus 16–24px side margins. The page-controller locks the overall layout into this single-column, modal-like hero section and ensures that any scrolling (if content exceeds height) happens inside the central panel rather than the background image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-viewport container with a fixed or parallax background image and a centered, non-sticky content panel layered above it. All other visual components are positioned within this panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as an overlay centered within the same central panel area, not over the entire viewport. When active (while preferences are being fetched or saved), it dims the contents of the panel with a dark translucent scrim and shows a spinner or progress indicator in the absolute center of the panel. The overlay respects the panel’s rounded corners and width, making it feel integrated rather than full-screen. Vertically, the loader content (spinner + short loading label) sits slightly above the vertical center to align visually with where the headline and form normally draw the eye. Once loading completes, the overlay fades out, revealing the panel’s headline and form underneath without shifting their positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily floats above all panel contents as an absolute-positioned overlay pinned to the panel’s top, left, right, and bottom edges; disables interaction with underlying form elements while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form occupies the full content area inside the central dark panel, following a tight, single-column, top-to-bottom stack. At the very top of this form area, there is a bold H1-style title aligned to the left but visually centered within the panel’s width, spanning roughly 80–90% of the panel width. There is a 16–24px margin above the headline to keep it from touching the top edge. Immediately below the H1, with about 8–12px spacing, lies a short supporting sentence in a slightly smaller, high-contrast style. Another 16–24px gap separates this from a concise, bold simplification line (e.g., ‘Tell us when and how you want updates’), which may be set in white for emphasis. After another 16–24px, a brief instruction line (e.g., ‘Choose your topics and schedule below’) appears directly above the first input control, guiding the eye downward. The form’s interactive controls (all the selectors) are then stacked with 12–16px vertical gaps between them, each spanning nearly the full inner width of the panel (leaving 24–32px padding on the left and right). At the bottom of the form’s interactive region, there is a 24–32px gap before the Save Preferences button to visually separate inputs from action. Any bottom payoff or confirmation copy is placed just below the button, remaining within the same form container. The entire form uses consistent left alignment for labels with the field inputs directly beneath, maintaining a tight, focused vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central panel; if content exceeds panel height on smaller screens, the form scrolls as a single column while the panel frame remains centered against the fixed background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topics selector is the first major interactive block in the form, placed directly under the form’s instructional line. It spans roughly 100% of the inner panel width (minus the side paddings), aligned left, with a bold label at the top and the multi-select control immediately below. Vertically, it sits about 8–12px beneath the instruction copy. The selector area is about 1.5–2 times the height of a standard input to accommodate multiple chips or checkboxes, but visually it still feels like a single unit in the vertical stack. There is a 12–16px gap below this component before the content-types selector, maintaining the dense but orderly stack. Its placement at the top of the input column emphasizes that choosing topics is the primary preference decision.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element; stretches with the panel width and wraps chips or options to multiple lines as needed while maintaining the overall single-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the topics selector, separated by a 12–16px vertical gap, the content-types selector mirrors the same width and alignment. A left-aligned label sits above the multi-select field; the control itself expands vertically to accommodate multiple options or chips, but is visually framed to feel like one compact module. It maintains the same left and right padding as the topics selector, reinforcing a tight column. The combined height of the topics and content-type selectors together occupies a noticeable but not overwhelming portion of the middle third of the panel, giving the sense of a compact configuration zone. Another 12–16px gap below this component leads into the frequency selector.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width form element within the stack; content wraps within the same column, preserving the single-column, top-to-bottom flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The frequency selector appears below the content-types selector, with about 12–16px of vertical spacing. It is a single-select dropdown styled as a bold, full-width field. A left-aligned label sits directly above the dropdown (4–8px spacing), and the dropdown itself spans nearly the full inner width of the panel, with the same transparent/dark-styled background as other inputs. The visual height is slightly taller than a standard text input to maintain the touch-friendly feel established in the mockup. This selector anchors the start of the lower section where timing-related settings live. Another 12–16px gap separates this from the best-time selector below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the vertical form flow; no special behavior beyond typical dropdown expansion overlaying the content directly below it when opened.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The best-time selector is stacked immediately under the frequency selector, with a 12–16px vertical gap. It uses the same visual width, label positioning, and dropdown styling as the frequency selector, reinforcing a pair-like relationship. Together, these two controls occupy the lower portion of the form’s control cluster, just above the main CTA. There is a larger gap—about 24–32px—beneath this selector before the Save Preferences button, clearly separating 'settings' from 'action.' This position ensures the user’s eye naturally moves from choosing frequency to choosing time, then down to the button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element; participates in the single linear stack and moves up or down as the content above expands or collapses.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save action manifests as a bold, high-contrast CTA button positioned near the bottom of the central panel, directly under the last selector, separated by a 24–32px gap. The button spans nearly the full inner width of the panel (roughly 90–100% of the content width), with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding internally, creating a tall, substantial tap target. It is centered horizontally but left-aligned text inside is acceptable, provided it reads as a strong bar across the panel. The button color is bright and saturated (e.g., yellow or red) with a subtle drop shadow, echoing the direct-response marketing style. There is a 16–24px gap below the button for status messages and any payoff line. This button visually anchors the panel and is the final focal point in the downwards funnel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; remains at the bottom of the form’s content stack, moving downward if additional fields are added, and scrolling into view on small screens as the user progresses.
