component_layouts.layout_narrative: The success state appears inline, just below the Save Preferences button, using the bottom message area of the panel. When visible, it occupies a compact block that spans the same width as the button. The message is center- or left-aligned within this width and uses a calm but high-contrast color accent (e.g., teal for an upper line and white for a main line), echoing the mockup’s payoff statement. A smaller, colored line of text (teal/cyan) sits above a slightly larger, brighter confirmation line (white or near-white), with 4–8px spacing between them. The block sits 8–12px below the button, and there is another 16–24px of padding between this message and the bottom edge of the panel, maintaining a grounded, finished feel. When not active, this area collapses, allowing the button to sit closer to the panel’s bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline without shifting the rest of the layout drastically; its height expands the panel content area and may create slight internal scrolling on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state occupies the same general region as the success message but takes visual precedence when present. It appears just above or immediately below the Save Preferences button—ideally just above, so users see the problem before the action they attempted. In this configuration, the error message block spans the full inner width and sits 8–12px above the button, with a saturated warning color (e.g., red or deep orange) and strong contrast text. The block is compact and may include a bold headline line (e.g., ‘We couldn’t save your preferences’) and a smaller clarifying line, with 4–8px spacing between lines. This placement keeps the central CTA visible while clearly flagging the issue. When no error exists, the space collapses back to the normal 24–32px gap between the last selector and the button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline and pushes the Save Preferences button slightly downward while visible; dismissed errors return the button to its default position without altering the panel’s overall centering.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual but conceptually tied to the form area. It aligns with the central panel and ensures that any preference state (e.g., selected topics or frequency) is synchronized with URL parameters. While it has no direct on-screen representation, its influence is spatially consistent: when the page is loaded with certain URL parameters, the central panel opens with those controls already set, preserving the vertical layout and avoiding any jumps or reflows. In essence, it keeps the initial form state aligned with the incoming URL without changing any of the visual structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible logical layer; does not occupy physical space but updates the state of visible components on initial load or when the URL changes, without altering their pixel positions.

name: Help Center / FAQ
title: Help Center / FAQ
description: A searchable Help Center where users can find FAQ articles, view full answers, and navigate to contact support for additional help.

core_features: Global FAQ search using a prominent search input tied to the searchFAQArticles(query) interface.
Search results list showing matching FAQ articles with question titles and expandable answer sections.
Full FAQ answer view for a selected article using getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId).
Deep-link support so specific searches and FAQ articles can be opened directly via URL parameters.

Contextual 'Still need help? Contact support' actions under each FAQ answer that navigate to the contact support page with appropriate URL parameters, including a specific configuration for the money-back guarantee article.
user_workflows:
Search for FAQs and read answers:
1. User navigates to help_center.html.
2. Page reads the 'query' URL parameter (if present) and pre-fills the search input.
3. If 'query' is present, the page calls searchFAQArticles(query) and renders the result list.
4. User types or edits their search (e.g., 'refund') in the search input.
5. User submits the search form.
6. The page updates the URL parameter 'query' with the submitted value (e.g., help_center.html?query=refund).
7. The page calls searchFAQArticles(query) with the updated query and displays the matching FAQArticle items (question titles, short previews).
8. User clicks on a result item.
9. The page calls getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId) for the clicked article and expands/displays the full answer inline.

Open a specific FAQ directly from a link:
1. User clicks a link on another page pointing to help_center.html with 'faqId' (and optionally 'query') set, e.g., help_center.html?faqId=<FAQArticle.id>.
2. On load, the page reads 'faqId' from the URL.
3. The page calls getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId) using the 'faqId' value.
4. The selected FAQ's full question and answer are displayed in the detail area or expanded in the results list.
5. If a 'query' parameter is also present, the page first calls searchFAQArticles(query) to populate the results list and then highlights/expands the FAQ with id=faqId within that list.

Refine search after viewing an answer:
1. User has an open FAQ answer from the current search.
2. User adjusts text in the search input and submits again.
3. Page updates the 'query' parameter in the URL and removes any existing 'faqId' from the URL so a fresh result set is shown.
4. Page calls searchFAQArticles(newQuery) and renders new results.
5. Any previously open detailed FAQ is cleared or collapsed unless the user re-selects it.

Go from a FAQ answer to the Contact Support page:
1. User expands or opens a FAQ answer (via search results or a direct 'faqId' URL).
2. Under the full answer, the user sees the 'Still need help? Contact support' link.
3. User clicks this link.
4. The page determines appropriate URL parameters for contact.html based on the FAQArticle (e.g., mapping FAQArticle.category and possibly tags to ContactMessage.topic and subject).
5. For the money-back guarantee FAQ, the page navigates specifically to contact.html with topic=billing_refunds and subject='Question about 30-day money-back guarantee', as defined.
6. Browser navigates to contact.html with the computed query parameters.

Use browser navigation with preserved state:
1. User performs a search and opens a FAQ answer (URL is help_center.html?query=<q>&faqId=<id>).
2. User clicks links or modifies search again (URL changes accordingly).
3. User presses the browser Back button.
4. onPopState handler reads the restored URL parameters and re-applies state: triggers searchFAQArticles(query) if 'query' is present and getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId) if 'faqId' is present, re-opening the appropriate article.
interactions: Typing in the search input updates the local search query state but does not trigger a search until the user submits the form (e.g., by pressing Enter or clicking a submit button).
Submitting the search form updates the 'query' URL parameter, clears 'faqId' from the URL, and triggers searchFAQArticles(query).
Clicking on a FAQ result item sets 'faqId' in the URL (help_center.html?query=<currentQuery>&faqId=<selectedId>) and calls getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId) to load the full answer for that article.
Clicking an already-open FAQ result item toggles its expanded state without changing data; if collapsed, the component may remove 'faqId' from the URL if that item was the selected one.

When search results are loaded, each item binds to an FAQArticle; clicking on its question area is handled by an onClick event that uses that article's id as the faqArticleId parameter for getFAQArticleDetails.

The 'Still need help? Contact support' link under each answer uses an onClick event (or simple anchor navigation) that constructs a contact.html URL with query parameters derived from the FAQArticle properties:
  - Maps FAQArticle.category to ContactMessage.topic (e.g., billing_refunds for refund-related FAQs).
  - Sets ContactMessage.subject based on the FAQArticle.question, unless special-case logic applies.
  - For the dedicated money-back guarantee article, overrides the subject to 'Question about 30-day money-back guarantee' and topic to billing_refunds as required.

On initial page load and on any URL change (including back/forward navigation), the page parses 'query' and 'faqId' parameters and:
  - If 'query' is present, calls searchFAQArticles(query).
  - If 'faqId' is present, calls getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId) after the search results load (if any) so the matching item can be expanded or shown in the detail view.
If both interfaces return errors or empty results, the page shows functional error or empty-state messages controlled by component logic without relying on any static FAQ data values.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for search and selection state. Supported parameters: 'query' (string) for the search term and 'faqId' (string) for the selected FAQArticle.id.
- On load: The page reads 'query' and 'faqId' from window.location. If 'query' exists and is non-empty, it calls searchFAQArticles(query) and populates the results. If 'faqId' exists, it calls getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId) and marks that article as selected. If both are present, the detail component ensures that the article with id=faqId is expanded or shown after results load.
- On search submit: The page updates the browser URL to include 'query' (URL-encoded value) and removes any existing 'faqId'. It uses the History API (pushState/replaceState) so browser navigation remains consistent. It then calls searchFAQArticles(query) and re-renders results.
- On FAQ selection: Clicking a result item sets 'faqId' in the URL while preserving the current 'query'. The page then calls getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId) and updates the view to show the selected FAQ's full answer.
- On FAQ collapse (if the selected FAQ is collapsed): The page removes 'faqId' from the URL using History API and updates the view accordingly.
- On contact link click: The Help Center page does not alter its own state; instead, it constructs a navigation URL to contact.html with appropriate 'topic' and 'subject' parameters based on the current FAQArticle. For the money-back guarantee article, the outgoing URL is specifically contact.html?topic=billing_refunds&subject=Question%20about%2030-day%20money-back%20guarantee as defined, without storing any additional state.
- On browser back/forward: An onPopState listener re-reads 'query' and 'faqId' and re-issues searchFAQArticles and/or getFAQArticleDetails as needed so the visual state always matches the URL.

components.functionality: Provides a single-line input for users to enter a FAQ search term and submit it. On submit, it updates the 'query' URL parameter and triggers a searchFAQArticles(query) call via higher-level page logic.
event_handlers: onLoad: Read 'query' from URL and pre-fill the input value.
onChange: Update local query state as the user types; does not call searchFAQArticles directly.
onSubmit: Prevent default form submission, update URL with 'query' and clear 'faqId', then invoke searchFAQArticles(query) and pass results to 'faq-search-results-list'.

components.functionality: Displays the list of FAQArticle search results returned from searchFAQArticles(query). Each result shows the question and a short answer preview. It is responsible for rendering a clickable item for each FAQArticle that can be expanded to show full content via getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId).
event_handlers: onSearchResultsReceived: Accepts an array of FAQArticle objects from searchFAQArticles(query) and stores them for rendering.
onResultClick: When a result item is clicked, reads its bound FAQArticle.id, updates the 'faqId' URL parameter, and triggers getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId).
onResultToggle: If the clicked result is already selected, toggles its expanded state and, when collapsing the selected item, removes 'faqId' from the URL.
onPopState: Re-applies selection highlighting based on the 'faqId' parameter after navigation events.

components.functionality: Shows the full question and answer content for a selected FAQArticle inline within the search results list (or in a dedicated detail area if needed). It receives the faqArticleId either from URL state or from a result item click, then uses getFAQArticleDetails to fetch the complete FAQArticle object and render the full answer.
event_handlers: onSelectFaq: Triggered when 'faqId' in the URL changes or when a result item is clicked. Calls getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId) with the selected id.
onDetailsLoaded: Receives a full FAQArticle object from getFAQArticleDetails and binds its question and answer fields into the expanded detail view.
onClose: When the user closes or collapses the detail view, removes 'faqId' from the URL using history.replaceState or pushState, and updates the component to hide the details.

components.functionality: Renders the 'Still need help? Contact support' action under every fully displayed FAQ answer. When activated, it navigates to contact.html with URL parameters derived from the current FAQArticle, including a specific configuration for the money-back guarantee article.
event_handlers: onRender: For each FAQArticle whose full answer is shown, display a 'Still need help? Contact support' link bound to that article.

onClick: Reads the bound FAQArticle properties (category, question, tags, guarantee_period_days) and constructs a contact.html URL. Maps FAQArticle.category to ContactMessage.topic where possible (e.g., 'billing_refunds' -> topic=billing_refunds). Uses the article.question as the default subject, unless the FAQArticle meets the criteria for the money-back guarantee article (for example, category='billing_refunds' and tags including a guarantee-related keyword or a non-null guarantee_period_days), in which case it sets topic=billing_refunds and subject='Question about 30-day money-back guarantee'. Navigates the browser to the constructed contact.html URL.
onKeyboardActivate: Supports keyboard activation (e.g., Enter/Space) to trigger the same navigation logic for accessibility.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL-based state, API calls (searchFAQArticles and getFAQArticleDetails), and communication between the search form, results list, and detail view. Ensures that the visual state matches the URL state at all times.
event_handlers: onLoad: Parse 'query' and 'faqId' from the URL. If 'query' exists, call searchFAQArticles(query) and send results to 'faq-search-results-list'. If 'faqId' exists, call getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId) and send the result to 'faq-article-detail-inline'.
onSearchSubmit: Receive search input from 'faq-search-form', update the URL with 'query', clear 'faqId', call searchFAQArticles(query), and update 'faq-search-results-list'.

onFaqSelectionChange: When a FAQArticle is selected from the results, update the URL with 'faqId' and call getFAQArticleDetails(faqArticleId), then pass the full article to 'faq-article-detail-inline'.

onPopState: Listen for browser back/forward events, re-parse URL parameters, and re-trigger searchFAQArticles and/or getFAQArticleDetails so that 'faq-search-form', 'faq-search-results-list', and 'faq-article-detail-inline' all reflect the restored state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, tall, centrally aligned hero panel over a full-bleed photographic background (e.g., a lifestyle beach image). All Help Center content is stacked in a strict top-to-bottom flow inside this dark, translucent panel, echoing the modal-like card from the mockup. From top to bottom inside the panel: a Help Center heading and brief support intro (page-level, not listed as components), then the faq-search-form as the primary interaction, followed by a slim state/breadcrumb area controlled by faq-page-state-controller, then the faq-search-results-list occupying the central section, the faq-article-detail-inline expanding inline below the relevant result or in a dedicated detail zone beneath the list, and finally the faq-contact-support-link anchored near the bottom of the panel as the secondary CTA for additional help.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, tall, centrally aligned hero panel over a full-bleed photographic background (e.g., a lifestyle beach image). All Help Center content is stacked in a strict top-to-bottom flow inside this dark, translucent panel, echoing the modal-like card from the mockup. From top to bottom inside the panel: a Help Center heading and brief support intro (page-level, not listed as components), then the faq-search-form as the primary interaction, followed by a slim state/breadcrumb area controlled by faq-page-state-controller, then the faq-search-results-list occupying the central section, the faq-article-detail-inline expanding inline below the relevant result or in a dedicated detail zone beneath the list, and finally the faq-contact-support-link anchored near the bottom of the panel as the secondary CTA for additional help.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop, centered with equal left and right background margins. Vertically it sits with generous top and bottom breathing room (about 10–15% of viewport height margin), but visually reads as a nearly full-height hero. Inside the panel, the upper 35–40% is devoted to orientation and self-service entry (heading + intro + faq-search-form + page-state/breadcrumb strip), the middle 40–45% to self-service discovery (faq-search-results-list as the main content body), and the lower 20–25% to deep reading and escalation (faq-article-detail-inline when open plus faq-contact-support-link and its supporting text). When content is long, the panel scrolls with the page, preserving the single-column flow.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content inside the panel is moderately dense and conversion-focused: components are stacked with 16–24px spacing between related sub-elements and 24–32px between major sections (search, results, detail, contact), mirroring the mockup. Elements feel close enough to create urgency and a sense of continuous flow, but each block has enough vertical separation to remain scannable. Outside the panel, the background image is fully visible with no competing UI, providing atmospheric breathing room and high contrast for the central content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is a strong vertical funnel: the eye first catches the bold Help Center heading and short support promise, then immediately drops to the high-contrast faq-search-form near the top center of the panel. Below that, any state indicator (faq-page-state-controller UI) subtly orients the user. The gaze then moves down into the faq-search-results-list, scanning question titles. Clicking a result draws attention further down to the expanded faq-article-detail-inline, which visually dominates the lower portion of the panel when open. At the very bottom, the faq-contact-support-link, styled as a clear escalation CTA, catches attention as the final step if answers above are insufficient.

layout.overall_description: The Help Center page sits on top of a full-bleed, high-contrast lifestyle photograph that fills the entire viewport. Centered on the screen is a tall, dark, semi-transparent panel with soft edges and a subtle glow or shadow, occupying roughly the middle 40–50% of the screen width and stretching vertically with comfortable margins above and below. Inside this panel, everything is arranged in a single, clean column. At the top, a bold, high-impact Help Center headline and a short line of supporting copy set the tone, echoing the dramatic typography of the original marketing hero. Immediately beneath, a wide, prominent search form spans nearly the full width of the panel, acting as the main entry point. Below the search, a slim horizontal area managed by the page state controller can show a breadcrumb or status line, helping users understand whether they are browsing all FAQs, viewing search results, or reading a specific article. The central band of the panel is reserved for the search results list: each FAQ result stacks vertically as a high-contrast row, with enough spacing and hover effects to feel tappable and action-oriented. When a result is opened, the answer expands inline as a darker, slightly inset section that occupies most of the lower part of the panel, pushing subsequent content downward without breaking the single column. Anchored towards the bottom is a small but visually clear block that asks whether the user still needs help, with the contact support link highlighted in brand accent color, acting like a final CTA. The entire composition preserves the strong vertical funnel and promotional feel of the original mockup while reorienting it toward self-service help and escalation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-search-form is positioned in the upper section of the central hero panel, directly below the page-level Help Center heading and introductory line. It is horizontally centered and spans approximately 90–95% of the panel’s internal width, leaving a slim 16–24px inset margin on both left and right sides to echo the mockup’s card padding. There is about 24–32px of vertical space between the intro text and the top edge of the search form, making it feel closely connected but still distinct as the primary interactive block. The search input itself is a single, full-width field with a noticeable height (equivalent to 12–16px vertical padding inside), visually akin to the bold email field in the original marketing layout. If a label or helper text is present, it sits directly above the input with 8–12px of spacing, aligned left but within the same column. The search button, if separate from the field, is attached to the right side or placed directly below as a full-width, high-contrast button, mirroring the bold CTA style; in either configuration, the entire search form area behaves like a compact module around 1/4 to 1/3 of the panel height when combined with heading and intro above. The component pushes all subsequent content downward in a linear flow and remains fixed to its spot within the panel as the page scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element within the central hero panel; it participates in the normal vertical document flow, expanding downward if helper text or validation messages appear but always maintaining its position in the upper third of the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-search-results-list occupies the central, content-heavy band of the hero panel, starting roughly 24–32px below the bottom edge of the search form (and any state strip above it). It spans the same usable width as the search area (about 90–95% of the panel width), left-aligned with the search input to preserve a strong vertical spine. Vertically, this list is designed to consume the majority of the middle 40–45% of the panel’s height on a typical viewport, expanding as results grow and pushing later content downward. Each individual result is rendered as a horizontally full-width row with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px side padding, separated by 8–12px vertical gaps to keep the stack readable but dense. The row background is slightly lighter or darker than the panel base, with subtle shadows or borders in accent colors (e.g., a slim yellow or teal bar on the left) to maintain the high-contrast, promotional visual language. When there are many results, the entire panel scrolls with the page rather than constraining the list to a separate scroll region, preserving the linear top-to-bottom flow. The list visually reads as a continuous column of tappable options leading the user toward one specific article, naturally guiding the eye downward to the location where the detail view will appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flexible-height block that grows based on the number of results, remaining anchored beneath the search area and above the article detail and contact-support section. It does not introduce its own scrolling container; instead, it contributes to the overall page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-article-detail-inline appears directly within the same central column as the results list, following the strict vertical stacking pattern. When an article is selected, its detail view expands inline either directly beneath the clicked result row (ideal for preserving context) or in a dedicated detail zone that begins 16–24px below the last visible result; in either case, it occupies the full width of the panel’s content area (90–95% of the panel width) and is aligned to the same left edge as the results and search field. Its container has more generous internal padding (24–32px all around), and a slightly more opaque or elevated background compared to the rest of the panel, giving it a modal-like feel while still remaining embedded in the flow. The article title is at the top of this block, followed by the answer content with 16–24px vertical rhythm between paragraphs and subheadings. Visually, the detail block tends to fill much of the lower third to lower half of the panel when expanded, pushing the faq-contact-support-link down toward the bottom. If the article is long, the page scrolls further, but the content stays within this single column. When no article is selected, this component effectively collapses to zero height, allowing the results and contact section to move upward.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual, expandable block that animates open within the main vertical flow. It expands and collapses in place without overlaying other content, pushing subsequent elements (including the contact-support link) up or down as needed. It does not float or fix to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-contact-support-link is positioned near the bottom of the hero panel as the final escalation CTA, echoing the payoff statement zone from the mockup. It sits 24–32px below the bottom edge of either the search results list (when no article is open) or the faq-article-detail-inline (when an article is open). The link is part of a small, centered block spanning about 70–80% of the panel’s width, aligned to the same central column but with text centered horizontally to differentiate it from the more functional content above. A brief line of supporting copy (e.g., "Still need help?") appears above the link in a secondary color like teal, with 8–12px spacing, and the contact link itself is styled in a bright accent (e.g., yellow or cyan) with underline or an arrow icon, making it stand out against the dark background while remaining visually lighter than the primary search and results. This block typically occupies the lower 15–20% of the panel’s content area, but its height is compact, with plenty of empty space below (24–32px) before the edge of the panel to preserve breathing room and echo the mockup’s bottom payoff section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored at the end of the main content column. It always appears after the results and article detail sections and moves up or down depending on their combined height. It does not stick to the viewport; it scrolls with the rest of the panel content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-page-state-controller occupies a slim horizontal band just beneath the faq-search-form and above the faq-search-results-list, functioning as an unobtrusive status and mode indicator within the same central column. Visually, when it produces UI, it appears as a single line or compact bar spanning about 90–95% of the panel width, aligned left with the search field. It sits 8–16px below the search form, maintaining close proximity so that users can quickly connect the state to their previous action. Typical content might include a small breadcrumb (e.g., "All FAQs > Search results"), a result count ("12 results for ‘payouts’"), or a subtle toggle to switch between views. The typography is smaller and less bold than the main headings and CTA elements, usually in a secondary color (muted gray or teal) to avoid distracting from the search and results while still providing clarity. In states where no explicit control or message is needed (e.g., idle with no query), this component collapses entirely, effectively becoming zero height and allowing the results list to slide up closer to the search form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lightweight informational layer that participates in the normal document flow, expanding to a slim bar when needed and collapsing to zero height when not in use. It never floats, overlays, or sticks; it simply reclaims vertical space between the search form and the results list.

name: Contact Support
title: Contact Support
description: Contact Support allows visitors to submit questions or issues (including billing and refund inquiries) by filling out a form with topic, subject, message, and contact details. The page can pre-fill the topic and subject from URL parameters and sends the submission to support using the submitContactMessage interface, then shows an inline confirmation state.

core_features: Contact form with required fields: Topic (dropdown including the billing_refunds option), Subject, Message, Name, and Email, mapped to the ContactMessage data model.
Topic preselection based on the 'topic' URL parameter, using enum values from ContactMessage.topic (e.g., 'billing_refunds') so that Help Center links can deep-link into a specific topic.
Subject pre-filling based on the 'subject' URL parameter, allowing Help Center FAQ entries to pass a suggested subject line into the form.
Client-side validation to ensure all required fields are populated and that the email field has a valid format before submitContactMessage is called.
Form submission via the submitContactMessage interface using the current form state (topic, subject, message, name, email) and handling the returned success flag and message.
Inline confirmation and error feedback states based on the submitContactMessage response, using the returned 'success' boolean and 'message' string.
Ability for the user to modify or override any prefilled topic or subject before submitting, without changing the URL parameters.
Non-destructive page reload behavior where a reload will reapply URL-based presets (topic and subject) but not auto-resubmit the form.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Access from footer without presets:
1) User clicks the "Contact Support" link in the site footer (no query parameters).
2) Browser navigates to contact.html.
3) On page load, the page controller parses the URL, finds no 'topic' or 'subject' parameters, and initializes the form with an unselected (or default) topic and empty text fields.
4) User selects a topic from the Topic dropdown.
5) User enters a subject summarizing their issue.
6) User types their detailed message.
7) User enters their name and email address.
8) User clicks the form submit button.
9) Form validation runs; if validation passes, the page calls submitContactMessage with the current form values.
10) When the API returns, the page checks the 'success' flag:
   - If success = true: the form transitions to a confirmation state showing the response 'message' from the API.
   - If success = false or the call fails: the page shows an error state with the response 'message' (if available) and keeps the form data so the user can adjust and resubmit.

Workflow 2 – Access from Help Center with topic and subject presets:
1) User clicks a "Contact Support" or similar link in the Help Center, which links to contact.html with URL parameters such as ?topic=billing_refunds&subject=<encoded-subject-text>.
2) Browser loads contact.html with the given query parameters.
3) On page load, the page controller parses 'topic' and 'subject' from the URL.
4) The Topic dropdown is preselected to the parsed 'topic' value if it matches a valid ContactMessage.topic enum; otherwise the topic remains unselected.
5) The Subject input is prefilled with the decoded 'subject' parameter value.
6) User reviews and edits the topic and subject if needed, then fills in message, name, and email.
7) User submits the form; validation, submitContactMessage call, and confirmation/error handling proceed as in Workflow 1.

Workflow 3 – Handling validation errors and retry:
1) User fills out some but not all required fields and clicks the submit button.
2) Client-side validation detects missing or invalid values (such as empty message or invalid email format).
3) The form does not call submitContactMessage; instead, inline validation messages are displayed beside or below the relevant fields and a general validation summary is shown if needed.
4) User corrects the highlighted fields; the validation messages update or disappear as values become valid.
5) User clicks submit again.
6) If validation passes, submitContactMessage is called; on success, the confirmation state is shown. On error, an API error message is displayed while retaining user-entered data.

Workflow 4 – User wants to send another message after successful submission:
1) User has just submitted a message successfully and sees the confirmation state with the API's success message.
2) User clicks a "Send another message" control within the confirmation area (if provided by the implementation).
3) The form state is reset to initial values based solely on the current URL parameters (topic and subject), reapplying any presets.
4) User completes the form again and submits; the same submitContactMessage logic is reused.
interactions: On page load, the controller parses the query string for 'topic' and 'subject', initializes internal form state, and binds that state to the form controls (Topic dropdown, Subject input).
When the user changes the Topic dropdown, the form state for topic is updated to the selected ContactMessage.topic enum value and any validation errors for topic are re-evaluated.
When the user types into the Subject input, Message textarea, Name input, or Email input, the respective fields in the form state are updated and per-field validation can run on change or on blur.

On click of the submit button, a submit handler prevents default form submission, runs client-side validation on the form state, and if valid, invokes the submitContactMessage interface with the current topic, subject, message, name, and email.

When submitContactMessage resolves successfully (success = true), a success handler stores the returned ContactMessage object (if needed), updates a local submission status state to a 'submitted-success' state, and passes the API 'message' property into a notification/confirmation component.

When submitContactMessage resolves with success = false or fails, an error handler sets submission status to 'submitted-error', surfaces the API 'message' (if provided) as an error notification, and keeps the existing form state so the user can edit and retry.

If a 'Send another message' action is implemented, clicking it will reset internal form state back to its initial values derived from the URL parameters without reloading the page, effectively presenting a fresh form while maintaining the same topic/subject presets from the URL.

If the user refreshes the page, the browser reloads contact.html with the same query parameters; the initialization logic re-applies any 'topic' and 'subject' presets from the URL while discarding any previously unsent in-memory input.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page's primary state is driven by two URL parameters ('topic' and 'subject') and internal form/submission state. On every page load, the controller reads the query string. If 'topic' is present, its value is compared against the allowed values for ContactMessage.topic (such as 'billing_refunds', 'technical_support', 'general_question', 'program_details', 'other'); if the value matches one of these, it initializes the form's topic field with that value, otherwise the topic field remains unset. The Topic dropdown's selected value is always bound to this internal topic state, so changing the dropdown does not modify the URL but does override any initial preset for the current session. If 'subject' is present in the query string, its decoded value is used to initialize the Subject input value. Subsequent user edits to the subject do not affect the URL. Internal submission state includes at least: (a) a 'formValues' object mirroring the ContactMessage fields (topic, subject, message, name, email), (b) a 'validationErrors' object keyed by field name, and (c) a 'submissionStatus' flag (e.g., 'idle', 'submitting', 'submitted-success', 'submitted-error'). These states are kept in memory only and are not written to URL parameters or any persistent storage. When submitContactMessage completes successfully, submissionStatus is set to 'submitted-success', the 'contactMessage' returned from the API may be stored in memory if needed, and the confirmation view is rendered. If the user refreshes the page, all in-memory state is reset and the form is reinitialized solely from the URL parameters and default values, meaning the confirmation state is not preserved across reloads unless the URL itself is extended by the implementation (this design does not introduce any additional URL parameters beyond 'topic' and 'subject').

components.functionality: Parses URL parameters on page load, initializes the form state for topic and subject, and sets initial submission and validation state for the Contact Support page.
Wraps all contact fields (Topic dropdown, Subject input, Message textarea, Name and Email inputs), manages local form state, triggers validation, and invokes submitContactMessage when submitted.

Renders the Topic select element using the ContactMessage.topic enum values, ensures that the option corresponding to 'billing_refunds' is available for selection, and binds the selected value to the form's topic state, honoring any initial preset from the 'topic' URL parameter.
Provides a single-line text input for the subject line, initializes its value from the 'subject' URL parameter if present, and keeps it synchronized with the form's subject state.
Provides a multi-line textarea for the user to describe their question or issue in detail and keeps its value synchronized with the form's message state.
Contains the Name and Email input fields, binds them to the corresponding fields in the form state, and supports per-field validation such as required fields and basic email format checks.

Runs client-side validation on the current form state before any submitContactMessage call, ensuring required ContactMessage fields are not empty, the topic is a valid enum value, and the email appears to be in a valid format; exposes validation error states to the form controls.

Invokes the submitContactMessage interface with the current form values (topic, subject, message, name, email), manages the submitting state, and processes the API response to update submission status and pass along the returned 'message' and 'contactMessage' object.

Displays inline feedback after a submission attempt, showing a confirmation state when submitContactMessage returns success = true (using the API's 'message') or an error state when success = false or the call fails, and optionally providing a control to reset the form for another message.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, tall hero section with a full-bleed beach photograph as the background. Centered in the viewport is a vertically oriented, dark translucent panel that behaves like an overlay card. Inside this panel, content flows linearly from top to bottom: a bold 'Contact Support' hero heading, brief supporting and reassurance copy, optional inline feedback/alerts, then the contact form fields stacked in a single column, and finally the full-width submit button with a short reassurance/payoff line below. No sidebars or multi-column layouts are used; everything follows a focused, vertical funnel similar to the original promotional mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, tall hero section with a full-bleed beach photograph as the background. Centered in the viewport is a vertically oriented, dark translucent panel that behaves like an overlay card. Inside this panel, content flows linearly from top to bottom: a bold 'Contact Support' hero heading, brief supporting and reassurance copy, optional inline feedback/alerts, then the contact form fields stacked in a single column, and finally the full-width submit button with a short reassurance/payoff line below. No sidebars or multi-column layouts are used; everything follows a focused, vertical funnel similar to the original promotional mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Horizontally, the central panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width around 520–640px), always centered, with plenty of background visible on the left and right. Vertically, the panel stretches enough to fit content with 64px top and bottom interior breathing room. Within the panel, the upper ~40% is dedicated to messaging (hero title, supporting text, instructions, and feedback banner), and the lower ~60% is dedicated to the conversion UI (topic/subject/message/contact details fields plus the submit button and final reassurance line). Spacing follows the 8px base scale, with 24–32px between major blocks and 12–16px between related elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately dense and conversion-focused: components are stacked with just enough vertical spacing to keep them legible and scannable, but not so much that the page feels leisurely. Headings and key lines have a bit more breathing room (24–32px) to reinforce hierarchy, while form fields and validation messages use tighter 8–16px spacing. The dark panel consolidates all interactive elements, while the open photographic background prevents the page from feeling cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the high-contrast hero heading near the top center of the panel, then drops to the supporting explanatory line that clarifies what kinds of issues support can help with (including billing and refunds). Below this, an instruction line nudges the user into the form. If present, the inline notification banner sits near the top of the form area to immediately confirm success or surface issues. From there the eye moves down the form in logical order: topic dropdown, subject line, message textarea, contact details, and finally to the large, bright full-width submit button. A small, calm reassurance line under the button anchors the flow and sets expectations about response time. Overall, the flow is a top-to-bottom funnel anchored in the center of the hero background.

layout.overall_description: The Contact Support page sits on top of a dramatic, full-bleed beach photograph that fills the entire browser window. In the center of this scene is a tall, dark, semi-transparent panel with soft edges and a subtle drop shadow, occupying about half the viewport width on desktop and scaling down on smaller screens. At the top of this panel, a bold, high-contrast 'Contact Support' headline spans almost the full width, followed by a short, brightly accented line that reassures visitors they can get help with account, billing, and refund questions. Just below, a quieter instruction sentence guides users to choose a topic and describe their issue. If there is any success or error feedback, a vivid notification bar appears just above the form fields, stretching edge-to-edge inside the panel. The form itself flows in a single, centered column: first a full-width topic dropdown, then a subject input, then a deeper message textarea, followed by a grouped contact details section. All inputs share consistent width and padding, separated by 16–24px vertical gaps. Anchoring the bottom of the panel is a large, bright yellow full-width submit button with a slight glow and shadow, drawing the eye as the main call-to-action. Beneath the button, a compact line of text in a cooler tone sets expectations about response time. The whole composition maintains the high-contrast, promotional feel of the mockup while adapting it to a focused support form experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller defines the full hero canvas: the browser viewport is completely covered by a high-resolution beach photograph, scaled to cover and anchored so that the horizon line sits roughly in the upper third of the screen. Centered both horizontally and vertically within this viewport is a single, tall, dark translucent panel (e.g., 80–90% viewport height on large screens, with a max-width around 520–640px). The panel has 24–32px internal padding on all sides, a subtle border glow, and a soft drop shadow to separate it from the background. On tall screens, the panel is vertically centered; on shorter screens, it aligns towards the top with at least 32–48px margin from the top and bottom. All visible components (heading, supporting text, form, notification banner) are rendered inside this central panel in a strictly vertical, center-aligned column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a full-page container with a fixed background image and a centered overlay panel. The background is fixed relative to the viewport (parallax-like feel), while the central panel scrolls only if content height exceeds the viewport, maintaining at least 16–24px margin from viewport edges on all devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form occupies the lower two-thirds of the central panel, positioned directly beneath the introductory copy and any active notification banner. It is centered horizontally within the panel and uses 100% of the panel’s content width minus the internal padding. Vertically, the form begins approximately 24–32px below the last line of instruction text, establishing a clear break between messaging and input. Inside the form, each control is stacked in a single column, left-aligned labels, and full-width inputs. Vertical spacing between individual form controls is 16–24px, with a slightly larger 24–32px gap above the submit button to visually prioritize it as the final step. The overall form height flexes with content but typically fills roughly half to a bit more than half of the panel’s interior height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally within the central panel as a vertical stack. On very small viewports, the form becomes the dominant element, causing the panel to grow vertically and scroll while maintaining constant horizontal padding and full-width controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic dropdown is the first form control and sits at the top of the form column, signaling the starting point for user interaction. It spans nearly the full usable width of the panel (100% of the form width) with a clear, left-aligned label placed directly above it. There is about 8px vertical space between the label and the dropdown field, and about 16–20px space beneath the field before the next control. The dropdown itself has 12–16px internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, matching the visual weight of the button and other inputs. It is visually emphasized slightly more than subsequent fields to underscore its importance in routing the request, but less than the main CTA button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width element aligned with other inputs in the vertical form stack; it stretches or contracts with the form’s width but maintains consistent height and padding across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subject input is placed directly below the topic dropdown, aligned flush on the left and right with identical width. It appears about 16–20px below the bottom edge of the topic field. A concise, left-aligned label sits 8px above the input field. The input itself uses the same height and padding as the dropdown, maintaining a cohesive rhythm between controls. Its relative height is smaller than the message textarea, emphasizing its role as a brief summary of the issue. Overall, it occupies a small, clearly delineated band of space in the upper half of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width text input with no special behavior; it sits in the linear vertical flow and shifts up or down only when validation messages appear above or below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The message textarea sits beneath the subject input with a 16–24px vertical gap, forming the tallest single field in the form. Its label is left-aligned, placed about 8px above the textarea. The textarea spans the full form width and has a height of approximately 3–5 lines by default, visually occupying about 20–25% of the form’s vertical space. Internal padding matches other inputs (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal), but the increased height makes this section feel like the central body of the form. This control anchors the middle of the panel and visually signals where the user should spend most of their time typing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width multiline field that can grow vertically if auto-resize is enabled; if it grows, it pushes subsequent controls downward while maintaining the same top and side alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details-group appears directly below the message textarea with about 24px vertical separation, forming a clearly distinct section. It has a group label such as 'How can we reach you?' placed 8px above the first field. Inside the group, individual details (e.g., name, email, phone) are stacked vertically in a single column to preserve the one-column layout; each field is full-width and aligned with the other inputs. Vertical spacing between fields within this group is tighter (12–16px) to visually tie them together. The group as a whole occupies a band of space just above the submit button, roughly 20–30% of the form’s vertical height, and reads as a cohesive sub-section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a mini vertical stack inside the main form flow. The group moves as a single block when space is constrained, but it does not break into multiple columns; it remains single-column across all breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This logic component does not render as a visible block but affects the micro-layout around each form control. When a field is invalid, a compact error message appears directly beneath that specific input, left-aligned, using small, high-contrast red text. Each error line occupies its own row and adds roughly 12–16px of vertical space between the field and the next control, slightly increasing the local density without disturbing overall hierarchy. For form-level errors, the handler triggers a banner-like message that appears at the top of the form area, just below the instructional text and above the first field, spanning the full width of the panel interior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Dynamically injects or removes inline error elements within the vertical form stack, causing local vertical expansion or contraction. It does not affect horizontal alignment or the position of the central panel; it only adjusts vertical spacing around affected controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit-contact-action is tied to a visually dominant CTA button located at the bottom of the form. The button spans nearly the full width of the form, with 24–32px top padding separating it from the last contact detail field to emphasize it as the final step. The button has generous internal padding (14–16px vertical, 24px horizontal), bold uppercase or heavy-weight text, and a bright yellow or similarly saturated color with a strong drop shadow and subtle glow, making it the highest-contrast interactive element inside the panel. Beneath the button, a small, muted line of text (e.g., response time or reassurance about support) sits about 8–12px below, aligned center, visually tying back to the supportive tone of the hero copy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchors the bottom of the form stack. When the action is disabled (e.g., while submitting), the button changes state (dimmed, loading indicator) but stays in the same position. On very short viewports, it may scroll off-screen, but remains the last element in the panel’s vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission feedback component appears as an inline notification banner within the central panel, not as a separate overlay. When active, it sits just below the main hero heading and immediately before the supporting/introductory copy or directly above the form fields, depending on implementation. The banner spans the full width of the panel’s content area, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and uses a high-contrast background color (e.g., teal or bright green for success, red for error) that stands out against the dark panel. There is 16–24px of vertical space above and below the banner to separate it from surrounding text. The message inside is concise and center- or left-aligned, reinforcing the outcome of the form submission. In a success state, this banner may be the primary thing the user sees after the form, while the form either remains visible below or subtly de-emphasized.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically within the vertical stack, pushing form content down when visible. It stays fixed within the panel (not sticky to the viewport) and always maintains its position near the top of the interactive area so that feedback is visible without scrolling in typical scenarios.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays dynamically loaded background information about the work-from-home business opportunity, including mission, values, approach, coaching and training overview, and experience highlights, and provides navigation to the application page.

core_features: Load and display the mission statement, values, approach summary, coaching and training overview, and experience highlights using the getAboutPageContent interface.
Provide in-page navigation to jump directly to specific sections (mission, values, approach, coaching and training, experience) based on user clicks or URL parameters.
Expose a clear "Apply Now" call-to-action that navigates to application.html and preserves the originating context via URL parameters.
Gracefully handle loading and error states for the getAboutPageContent request without relying on any static fallback content.
Keep the currently focused section in sync with URL parameters so that users can deep-link to specific parts of the About page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View full About content
1) User opens about.html from footer navigation or a direct link without additional parameters.
2) Page controller component triggers getAboutPageContent.
3) While data is loading, a functional loading state is shown (no placeholder text; only generic loading indicators).
4) On success, mission statement, values list, approach summary, coaching and training overview, and experience highlights are passed to their respective components and rendered in full.
5) User can scroll through the sections or use in-page navigation links to jump to specific sections.

Workflow 2: Deep-link to a specific section
1) User opens about.html with a URL parameter, for example about.html?section=coaching.
2) Page controller reads the section parameter and stores it as currentSection state.
3) getAboutPageContent is called and, once resolved, the controller instructs the page to scroll/focus the component that corresponds to the section value from the URL.
4) The in-page navigation component highlights the matching section as active based on the currentSection state.
5) If the user clicks another section link (e.g., Values), the controller updates the URL to about.html?section=values (using the History API) and updates currentSection, then focuses the new section.

Workflow 3: Proceed to application from About
1) User scrolls or navigates to the call-to-action area and clicks the Apply Now button.
2) The CTA component calls its click handler, which constructs a URL for application.html that includes a source reference such as application.html?source=about&section=<currentSectionValue>.
3) Browser navigates to application.html with these parameters.
4) Application page can optionally read these parameters (outside the scope of this page) to adjust its own behavior.

Workflow 4: Handle loading or error states
1) User opens about.html.
2) Page controller calls getAboutPageContent.
3) If the call is still in progress, the controller maintains a loading state and renders components in a non-data-dependent loading mode (e.g., showing only structural placeholders without any hardcoded business content).
4) If the call fails, the controller sets an error state that can be represented by functional messaging (e.g., a generic error indicator) and a retry interaction, without displaying any static mission, values, or experience text.
5) User can click a retry action which triggers getAboutPageContent again.
interactions: Click in-page navigation items (e.g., Mission, Values, Coaching and Training, Experience) to update the current section, modify the section URL parameter, and scroll to the corresponding content block.
Click the Apply Now CTA button to navigate to application.html while appending URL parameters indicating that the user came from the About page and, if applicable, which section they were viewing.
Initial page load triggers an onLoad handler that calls getAboutPageContent and reads any section URL parameter to determine the initial focus/scroll position.
Error state includes a retry interaction that, when clicked, will re-invoke getAboutPageContent without altering the URL parameters.
Browser back/forward navigation updates the visible active section according to the section parameter in the URL, ensuring the in-page navigation and focused section stay synchronized.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page state is driven by URL query parameters and API response data. The primary URL parameter is `section`, which may take values such as `mission`, `values`, `approach`, `coaching`, or `experience` (the exact values are interpreted by the page controller; no content is hardcoded). On load, the controller reads `section` from about.html?section=<value>. If present and valid, the controller sets `currentSection` state to that value and, after getAboutPageContent resolves, scrolls/focuses the matching section component. If `section` is absent or invalid, a default section is chosen within the controller logic without relying on any static business content. When users click an in-page navigation item, the controller updates both `currentSection` and the browser URL (e.g., to about.html?section=values) using the History API so that deep links can be shared and browser navigation remains consistent. When users click the Apply Now CTA, the navigation to application.html includes a `source=about` parameter and, if available, `section=<currentSection>` so the destination page can infer context; this is purely navigational and does not load any additional data on the About page. No persistent client-side storage such as localStorage or cookies is used; all persistent state is encoded in URL parameters, and all textual content is provided exclusively by getAboutPageContent.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load, calls getAboutPageContent, manages loading and error states, interprets URL parameters (such as section), and passes the resulting data and currentSection state down to child components.
Provides a list of section anchors (e.g., mission, values, approach, coaching and training, experience) that users can click to jump to each section and update the URL section parameter.

Displays the mission statement and the high-level approach summary retrieved from getAboutPageContent, and exposes an anchor target so it can be focused when section=mission or section=approach is active.
Renders the list of values returned by getAboutPageContent.values and allows the page controller to focus this block when section=values is active.
Shows the coaching and training overview text from getAboutPageContent.coachingAndTrainingOverview and serves as the target for section=coaching in URL parameters.

Displays the list of experience highlights from getAboutPageContent.experienceHighlights, helping build trust by presenting dynamic information about background, track record, and customer focus, and acts as the anchor for section=experience.

Provides an Apply Now button within the About content area that, when clicked, navigates to application.html and includes context such as source=about and the currently selected section in the query string.
Shows a functional loading indicator while getAboutPageContent is in progress, without displaying any hardcoded mission, values, or other business text.
Displays a functional error state if getAboutPageContent fails, and offers a retry action that triggers a new API call while preserving existing URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single-column layout centered within the viewport; likely a simple percentage-based grid (e.g., max-width container around 480–640px) rather than a full 12-column system.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page uses the same full-bleed beach photography background with a single, tall, dark translucent panel centered in the viewport. All About content lives in one linear, top-to-bottom column inside this panel. At the top of the panel: the About page title and short intro. Directly beneath that sits the in-page navigation. Below the navigation, the content sections (mission & approach, values, coaching & training, experience highlights) are stacked vertically with consistent spacing, culminating in a bold, full-width Apply Now CTA at the bottom of the panel. No side columns; everything is in one dominant vertical funnel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page uses the same full-bleed beach photography background with a single, tall, dark translucent panel centered in the viewport. All About content lives in one linear, top-to-bottom column inside this panel. At the top of the panel: the About page title and short intro. Directly beneath that sits the in-page navigation. Below the navigation, the content sections (mission & approach, values, coaching & training, experience highlights) are stacked vertically with consistent spacing, culminating in a bold, full-width Apply Now CTA at the bottom of the panel. No side columns; everything is in one dominant vertical funnel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central overlay panel occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving generous background visible on both sides. Vertically, the panel spans most of the viewport height and scrolls if content overflows, while the background image stays fixed. Within the panel, approximately the upper 60–65% of the vertical space is reserved for informational content (intro + four sections), and the lower 35–40% visually emphasizes conversion (Apply Now CTA plus the lower part of the experience highlights). Section titles and key lines use more vertical padding (24–32px) to breathe, while body copy and lists are spaced more tightly (12–16px) to maintain a focused, promotional density.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density follows a direct-response style: information is stacked in relatively tight vertical blocks, but separated with clear, repeated rhythm using the 8px-based spacing scale. Major sections are separated by 24–32px of vertical space, while headings, subheadings, and paragraphs inside a section are separated by 8–16px. Lists (like values and highlights) feel compact and efficient but not cramped, aided by consistent line height and small vertical gaps between items. The panel’s relatively constrained width keeps line lengths short and copy easy to scan, balancing dense information with high readability.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is drawn first to the high-contrast About headline at the top of the central panel, then immediately to the in-page navigation just below it, which acts like a table of contents. From there, the user scrolls or taps navigation items to move down the single column: mission & approach, then values, then coaching & training, then experience highlights. The content builds a narrative toward trust and clarity, guiding users down to the bold, brightly colored Apply Now CTA anchored toward the bottom of the panel. Background imagery, gradients, and accent colors (red, yellow, teal/cyan) punctuate section titles and key statements to keep the eye flowing downward in a clear funnel toward the application action.

layout.overall_description: The About page sits on top of a full-bleed, high-contrast beach photograph that fills the entire browser window. In the center of the screen, a tall, dark, semi-translucent panel rises from near the top to near the bottom of the viewport, visually similar to a large modal card. This panel is about 40–50% of the viewport width, with soft drop shadows and subtle gradients that help it stand out against the beach scene. Inside the panel, content is arranged in a clean single column with generous side padding (around 24–32px) and tight, promotional stacking of sections. At the very top, a bold About heading and short intro set the tone, followed immediately by a horizontal in-page navigation bar that lets users jump between sections. As users scroll the panel, they encounter each section—mission & approach, values, coaching & training, and experience highlights—stacked one after the other with consistent vertical spacing, each section headed by strong, high-contrast typography and occasional accent color rules or labels. The bottom of the panel culminates in a full-width, bright Apply Now button that visually echoes the primary CTA from the original promotional mockup, framed by a bit of extra breathing room so it feels like the natural, emphatic endpoint of the page. Loading and error states temporarily replace the panel’s content with centered status messaging while preserving the same central, overlay feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader logic itself is invisible, but its presence is expressed through the loading and error states that occupy the central overlay panel. Functionally, it controls whether the main About content stack (navigation + sections + Apply Now CTA) is rendered or replaced by a full-panel status indicator. When active in normal state, the controller allows the dark translucent panel to show all stacked sections from top to bottom. When in loading or error mode, it ensures that only a centered status view appears within the same panel footprint, keeping the visual structure—a single, central, modal-like card over the beach photo—consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Determines whether the primary content column or a full-panel status overlay is visible; occupies no visible space itself but controls panel content state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in-page navigation sits inside the central dark panel, directly beneath the main About page heading and intro copy. It spans nearly the full internal width of the panel (about 90% of the panel width, centered), leaving equal padding on left and right. Vertically, it occupies a low-profile bar height—roughly 48–56px—composed of 3–5 pill-like navigation items laid out in a single horizontal row on desktop. Each nav item is center-aligned within the bar and uses bold, high-contrast type, with the active section highlighted by a bright accent (e.g., yellow underline or teal pill background) matching the promotional palette. There is around 16–24px of vertical space above and below this navigation bar to separate it from the heading above and the first content section below. On scroll, the navigation can behave like a sticky subheader within the panel: once the user scrolls past the hero heading, the nav remains pinned to the top edge of the panel, ensuring section jumping is always accessible while the content below continues to scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially flows in the normal vertical layout beneath the heading; becomes sticky to the top inside edge of the central panel when the user scrolls, remaining fixed within the panel while the content sections scroll underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is the first major content block within the central panel, positioned directly below the in-page navigation with about 24–32px of vertical separation. It spans the full usable width inside the panel padding (roughly 80–90% of the panel width with equal left and right padding). At the top of the section, a bold subheading—such as “Our Mission & Approach”—appears in large, high-contrast type, possibly backed by a subtle gradient or a short horizontal accent bar in a saturated accent color (red or cyan) along its left edge. Below the heading, a short, impactful mission statement line is given extra emphasis with more generous spacing (16–24px above and below) and slightly larger type than the body. One to three paragraphs of supporting copy follow in a tight, readable column, with 8–12px between paragraphs to preserve the promotional, direct-response feel. The section is vertically bounded with about 24–32px of space before the next section begins, keeping it clearly grouped while maintaining the page’s linear, stacked narrative flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard scrollable content block within the single-column panel, expanding vertically with its content but never breaking the central alignment or the panel’s width constraints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The values section appears below the mission & approach block, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It also uses the full internal width of the panel, maintaining the single-column layout. At the top of the section, a prominent subheading like “Our Core Values” is set in bold white or near-white text, with a bright accent element such as a thin teal or yellow line running just beneath or to the left of the heading to echo the mockup’s use of color to guide the eye. Immediately below, a brief one- or two-sentence intro sets context, followed by a vertical list of values. Each value is arranged in a compact block: a short, bold value title on one line, followed by a one- or two-line explanation beneath it. Individual value blocks are separated by 12–16px of vertical space, keeping the list dense and scannable. Optional small icons or bullets in a saturated accent color can sit to the left of each value title, but the entire list remains clearly within a single column to match the mockup’s linear stacking. The section ends with a bit more vertical space (24–32px) before the next section, so it feels like its own cohesive block in the scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a vertically expanding list within the central column, maintaining the same fixed horizontal alignment and internal padding as other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned beneath the values list, separated by around 24–32px of vertical space, the coaching & training section continues the single-column rhythm. It again spans the full internal width of the panel with consistent side padding. The section opens with a strong subheading—e.g., “Coaching & Training You Actually Get”—styled similarly to other section headings but potentially accented with a warm color (yellow or red) to emphasize support and action. Directly under the heading, a short, high-impact summary sentence is given extra visual weight, potentially in slightly larger text or with a subtle background gradient strip behind it, reminiscent of the mockup’s bold simplifying statement. Below this, the content can be structured as 2–4 short subsections or bullet points, each describing a key aspect of the coaching and training (onboarding, ongoing support, tools, etc.), arranged vertically with 12–16px between items. Each item’s title is bold, with brief supporting copy beneath. Overall height of this section is moderate to tall, but still clearly contained by 24–32px of vertical space before the next section begins, so it feels like a distinct step in the narrative leading toward the final highlights and CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally as part of the main column, adjusting its height to fit content while preserving the central alignment and the panel’s dark background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The experience highlights section appears below the coaching & training block with approximately 24–32px of vertical separation and acts as the last major information section before the Apply Now CTA. It uses the same internal width as previous sections. A bold subheading such as “Why This Works: Real Experience” sits at the top, possibly paired with a teal accent line above or below it to mirror the payoff line treatment from the mockup. Under the heading, key highlights are presented in a tight, promotional format—either as a stacked set of short highlight blocks or as a dense bullet list. Each highlight is one or two lines, with a bolded phrase at the start (e.g., “10+ Years Remote Experience”) followed by a concise description. The vertical spacing between highlights is minimal (8–12px), creating a feeling of momentum and credibility building. This section gently transitions into the CTA by either ending with a slightly larger, outcome-focused statement that bridges into the Apply Now button below, or by simply leaving a slightly larger bottom margin (24–32px) before the CTA block, making it clear that the highlights are the final persuasive evidence before taking action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the penultimate scrollable block, growing vertically with its list of highlights, and aligning flush with the panel’s internal padding. It leads visually and spatially into the CTA at the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Apply Now CTA is positioned near the bottom of the central panel as the culmination of the page’s vertical funnel. It sits directly below the experience highlights section with a slightly more generous top margin (32px) to give it additional breathing room and emphasis. The CTA button itself spans nearly the full width of the content column (around 90–100% of the internal width), centered horizontally, with tall, generous internal padding—about 12–16px vertically and 24–32px horizontally—to echo the strong, high-impact button styling from the original mockup. The button is rendered in a bright, saturated color (likely yellow) with strong contrast against the dark panel, and a subtle drop shadow that makes it feel clickable and prominent. Brief supporting text—one short line such as “Ready to work from anywhere? Apply in minutes.” —can appear directly above or below the button, center-aligned, with 8–12px spacing, reinforcing the payoff and urgency. The bottom of the panel has additional padding (24–32px) below the button so it doesn’t feel cramped against the panel’s edge. On shorter viewports, as the user scrolls to the end, this CTA block becomes the clear final destination in the panel’s scrollable column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits as the final block in the scrollable column. Optionally can be made sticky within the panel’s lower edge on taller screens (remaining visible once reached), but by default behaves as a standard bottom-of-page CTA occupying the full content width inside the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the About data is loading, the central dark translucent panel still appears over the full-bleed beach background, but instead of the stacked content sections, the panel displays a single, centered loading state. This loading view is vertically and horizontally centered within the panel: a spinner or animated indicator appears at the center, with a concise loading message directly beneath it. The spinner and text together occupy a compact area—roughly the central 20–25% of the panel’s height—surrounded by generous empty space within the same dark backdrop, preserving the focused, modal-like feel. The rest of the panel remains empty, reinforcing that this is a transient state preceding the reveal of the content stack. The panel width and position remain identical to the normal state, maintaining the user’s spatial orientation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces all other panel content, centered within the fixed-width central panel. The background image remains static. When loading completes, this entire centered block is removed and replaced by the full stacked About content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: If there is an error loading the About content, the user sees an error state that occupies the same central panel area as the loading state. The dark translucent panel is still centered over the full-bleed beach background, but instead of the normal sections, a high-contrast error message block is centered both vertically and horizontally. A bold error headline (e.g., “Something went wrong”) appears at the top of this block in strong white text with a red accent icon or small red underline, followed by a short, explanatory line in smaller type. Below the message, a prominent retry button styled in a saturated accent color (e.g., yellow) appears, spanning about 40–60% of the panel’s internal width and centered. Vertical spacing between headline, description, and button follows the same 8–16px rhythm, with generous empty space around the entire block to keep the error state contained and immediately legible. This block occupies roughly the central 25–30% of the panel’s height, visually mimicking the hierarchy of a modal alert while preserving the existing background and central card structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully overlays and replaces the normal content stack inside the central panel when an error occurs, while the background image and panel dimensions remain fixed. Dismissing or retrying restores the standard scrollable content layout within the same spatial frame.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that dynamically retrieves and displays the current privacy policy, including last updated date and a privacy contact address, with deep-linkable sections and URL-based state.

core_features: Fetch and render the full privacy policy content using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface.
Display the policy's last updated timestamp returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Build an in-page navigation (table of contents) based on headings found within the returned contentHtml, without hardcoded section names.
Support deep linking to specific sections of the policy via a URL parameter that matches dynamically generated section IDs.
